explanation: One of the trickiest part of Japanese is that there is no verb for the state-of-being like the verb “to be” in English. You can, however, declare what something is by attaching the Hiragana character 「だ」 to a noun or na-adjective only. (We will learn about na-adjectives in the section on adjectives later.)
meaning: Is person.

example_sentences.ja: 学生[がくせい] だ[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Is student.

example_sentences.ja: 元気[げんき] だ[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Is well.

explanation: You can say you’re doing well or someone is a student without using 「だ」 at all.
primary_text:
Ａ 「 元気[げんき] ？ 」
Ｂ 「 元気[げんき] 。 」
meaning:
A: (Are you) well?
B: (I’m) well.

explanation: In Japanese, negative and past tense are all expressed by conjugation. We can conjugate a noun or adjective to either its negative or past tense to say that something is not [X] or that something was [X]. This may be a bit hard to grasp at first but none of these state-of-being conjugations make anything declarative like 「だ」 does. We’ll learn how to make these tenses declarative by attaching 「だ」 to the end of the sentence in a later lesson.
primary_text: 学生[がくせい] じゃない[;n] 。
meaning: Is not student.
reading:
がくせい
じゃない
。
context_notes: 004 Basic Grammar - Expressing State-of-Being: Conjugating to the negative state-of-being

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち] じゃない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Is not friend.

example_sentences.ja: 元気[げんき] じゃない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Is not well.

explanation:
We will now learn the past tense of the state-of-being. To say something was something, attach 「だった」 to the noun or na-adjective.
In order to say the negative past (was not), conjugate the negative to the negative past tense by dropping the 「い」 from 「じゃない」 and adding 「かった」.
primary_text: 学生[がくせい] だった[;n] 。
meaning: Was student.

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち] じゃなかった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Was not friend.

example_sentences.ja: 元気[げんき] じゃなかった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Was not well.

explanation: The first particle we will learn is the topic particle. The topic particle identifies what it is that you’re talking about, essentially the topic of your sentence. Let’s say a person says, “Not student.” This is a perfectly valid sentence in Japanese but it doesn’t tell us much without knowing what the person is talking about. The topic particle will allow us to express what our sentences are about. The topic particle is the character 「は」. Now, while this character is normally pronounced as /ha/, it is pronounced /wa/ only when it is being used as the topic particle.
primary_text:
ボブ 「 アリス は[;n] 学生[がくせい] ？ 」
アリス 「 うん 、 学生[がくせい] 。 」
meaning:
Bob: Is Alice (you) student?
Alice: Yeah, (I) am.
item_key: grammar:ボブ「アリスは学生?」アリス「うん、学生。」

example_sentences.ja:
ボブ 「 ジョン は[;n] 明日[あした] ？ 」
アリス 「 ううん 、 明日[あした] じゃない 。 」
example_sentences.en:
Bob: John is tomorrow?
Alice: No, not tomorrow.

example_sentences.ja:
アリス 「 今日[きょう] は[;n] 試験[しけん] だ 。 」
ボブ 「 ジョン は[;n] ？ 」
アリス 「 ジョン は[;n] 明日[あした] 。 」
example_sentences.en:
Alice: Today is exam.
Bob: What about John?

Alice: John is tomorrow. (As for John, the exam is tomorrow.)

explanation: Another particle that is very similar to the topic particle is the inclusive topic particle. It is essentially the topic particle with the additional meaning of “also”. Basically, it can introduce another topic in addition to the current topic. The inclusive topic particle is the 「も」 character and its use is best explained by an example.
primary_text:
ボブ 「 アリス は 学生[がくせい] ？ 」
アリス 「 うん 、 トム も[;n] 学生[がくせい] 。 」
meaning:
Bob: Is Alice (you) student?
Alice: Yeah, and Tom is 
also
 student.
item_key: grammar:ボブ「アリスは学生?」アリス「うん、トムも学生。」

example_sentences.ja:
ボブ 「 アリス は 学生[がくせい] ？ 」
アリス 「 ううん 、 トム も[;n] 学生[がくせい] じゃない 。 」
example_sentences.en:
Bob: Is Alice (you) student?
Alice: No, and Tom is 
also
 not student.

explanation: The inclusion of 「も」 must be consistent with the answer. It would not make sense to say, “I am a student, and Tom is also not a student.” Instead, use the 「は」 particle to make a break from the inclusion.
primary_text:
ボブ 「 アリス は 学生[がくせい] ？ 」
アリス 「 うん 、 でも トム は[;n] 学生[がくせい] じゃない 。 」
meaning:
Bob: Is Alice (you) student?
Alice: Yeah, but Tom is not student.
item_key: grammar:ボブ「アリスは学生?」アリス「うん、でもトムは学生じゃない。」

explanation:
Ok, so we can make a topic using the 「は」 and 「も」 particle. But what if we don’t know what the topic is? What if I wanted to ask, “Who is the student?” What I need is some kind of identifier because I don’t know who the student is. If I use the topic particle, the question would become, “Is who the student?” and that doesn’t make any sense because “who” is not an actual person.
This is where the 「が」 particle comes into play. It is also referred to as the subject particle but I hate that name since “subject” means something completely different in English grammar. Instead, I call it the identifier particle because the particle indicates that the speaker wants to identify something unspecified.
primary_text:
ボブ 「 誰[だれ] が[;n] 学生[がくせい] ？ 」
アリス 「 ジョン が[;n] 学生[がくせい] 。 」
meaning:
Bob: Who is the one that is student?
Alice: John is the one who is student.
item_key: grammar:ボブ「誰が学生?」アリス「ジョンが学生。」

example_sentences.ja:
誰[だれ] が[;n] 学生[がくせい] ？
学生[がくせい] は 誰[だれ] ？
example_sentences.en:
Who is the one that is student?
(The) student is who?

example_sentences.ja:
私[わたし] は[;n] 学生[がくせい] 。
私[わたし] が[;n] 学生[がくせい] 。
example_sentences.en:
As for
 me, (I am) student.
I (am) 
the one
 (that is) student.

explanation: The na-adjective is very simple to learn because it acts essentially like a noun. All the conjugation rules for both nouns and na-adjectives are the same. One main difference is that a na-adjective can directly modify a noun following it by sticking 「な」 between the adjective and noun. (Hence the name, na-adjective.)
primary_text: 静[しず;n]か な[;n] 人[ひと] 。
meaning: Quiet person.
context_notes: 018 Basic Grammar - Adjectives: The na-adjective

example_sentences.ja: きれい[;n] な[;n] 人[ひと] 。
example_sentences.en: Pretty person.

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち] は 親切[しんせつ;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Friend is kind.

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち] は 親切[しんせつ;n] な[;n] 人[ひと] だ。
example_sentences.en: Friend is kind person.

example_sentences.ja: ボブ は 魚[さかな] が 好[す;n]き だ。
example_sentences.en: Bob likes fish.

example_sentences.ja: ボブ は 魚[さかな] が 好[す;n]きじゃない 。
example_sentences.en: Bob does not like fish.

example_sentences.ja: ボブ は 魚[さかな] が 好[す;n]きだった 。
example_sentences.en: Bob liked fish.

example_sentences.ja: ボブ は 魚[さかな] が 好[す;n]きじゃなかった 。
example_sentences.en: Bob did not like fish.

example_sentences.ja: 魚[さかな] が 好[す;n]きな 人[ひと] 。
example_sentences.en: Person that likes fish.

example_sentences.ja: 魚[さかな] が 好[す;n]きじゃない 人[ひと] 。
example_sentences.en: Person that does not like fish.

example_sentences.ja: 魚[さかな] が 好[す;n]きだった 人[ひと] 。
example_sentences.en: Person that liked fish.

example_sentences.ja: 魚[さかな] が 好[す;n]きじゃなかった 人[ひと] 。
example_sentences.en: Person that did not like fish.

explanation:
Here, the entire clause 「魚が好き」、「魚が好きじゃない」、etc. is modifying “person” to talk about people that like or dislike fish. You can see why this type of sentence is useful because 「人は魚が好きだ」 would mean “People like fish”, which isn’t always the case.
We can even treat the whole descriptive noun clause as we would a single noun. For instance, we can make the whole clause a topic.
primary_text: 魚[さかな;n] が[;n] 好[す;n]きじゃない 人[ひと;n] は 、 肉[にく] が 好[す]き だ 。
meaning: Person who does not like fish like meat.
item_key: grammar:魚が好きじゃない人は、肉が好きだ。

example_sentences.ja: 魚[さかな;n] が[;n] 好[す;n]きな 人[ひと;n] は 、 野菜[やさい] も 好[す]き だ 。
example_sentences.en: Person who likes fish also likes vegetables.

explanation:
All i-adjectives always end in the Hiragana character: 「い」. However, you may have noticed that some na-adjectives also end in 「い」 such as 「きれい（な）」. So how can you tell the difference? There are actually very few na-adjectives that end with 「い」 that is usually not written in Kanji. Two of the most common include: 「きれい」 and 「嫌い」. Almost all other na-adjectives that end in 「い」 are usually written in Kanji and so you can easily tell that it’s not an i-adjective. For instance, 「きれい」 written in Kanji looks like 「綺麗」 or 「奇麗」. Since the 「い」 part of 「麗」 is part of a Kanji character, you know that it can’t be an i-adjective. That’s because the whole point of the 「い」 in i-adjectives is to allow conjugation without changing the Kanji. In fact, 「嫌い」 is one of the rare na-adjectives that ends in 「い」 without a Kanji. This has to do with the fact that 「嫌い」 is actually derived from the verb 「嫌う」.
Unlike na-adjectives, you do not need to add 「な」 to directly modify a noun with an i-adjective.
primary_text: おいしい[;n] 食[た]べ 物[もの] 。
meaning: Tasty food.

example_sentences.ja: 高[たか;n]い ビル。
example_sentences.en: Tall building.

example_sentences.ja: 高[たか;n]くない ビル。
example_sentences.en: Not tall building.

example_sentences.ja: 高[たか;n]かった ビル。
example_sentences.en: Building that was tall.

example_sentences.ja: 高[たか;n]くなかった ビル。
example_sentences.en: Building that was not tall.

explanation:
Note that you can make the same type of descriptive noun clause as we have done with na-adjectives. The only difference is that we don’t need 「な」 to directly modify the noun.
In this example, the descriptive clause 「値段が高い」 is directly modifying 「レストラン」.
primary_text: 値段[ねだん;n]が 高[たか;n]いレストラン はあまり好きじゃない。
meaning: Don’t like high price restaurants very much.
item_key: grammar:値段が高いレストランはあまり好きじゃない。
reading: ねだんがたかいレストランはあまりすきじゃない。
context_notes: 036 Basic Grammar - Adjectives: The i-adjective

explanation:
There is one i-adjective meaning “good” that acts slightly differently from all other i-adjectives. This is a classic case of how learning Japanese is harder for beginners because the most common and useful words also have the most exceptions. The word for “good” was originally 「よい（良い）」. However, with time, it soon became 「いい」. When it is written in Kanji, it is usually read as 「よい」 so 「いい」 is almost always Hiragana. That’s all fine and good. Unfortunately, all the conjugations are still derived from 「よい」 and not 「いい」. This is shown in the next table.
Another adjective that acts like this is 「かっこいい」 because it is an abbreviated version of two words merged together: 「格好」 and 「いい」. Since it uses the same 「いい」, you need to use the same conjugations.
primary_text: 値段[ねだん]があんまり よくない[;n] 。
meaning: Price isn’t very good.
item_key: grammar:値段があんまりよくない。
reading: ねだんがあんまりよくない。
context_notes: 037 Basic Grammar - Adjectives: An annoying exception

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]は かっこよかった[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: He looked really cool!

explanation:
We’ve now learned how to describe nouns in various ways with other nouns and adjectives. This gives us quite a bit of expressive power. However, we still cannot express actions. This is where verbs come in. Verbs, in Japanese, always come at the end of clauses. Since we have not yet learned how to create more than one clause, for now it means that any sentence with a verb must end with the verb. We will now learn the three main categories of verbs, which will allow us to define conjugation rules. Before learning about verbs, there is one important thing to keep in mind.
A grammatically complete sentence requires a verb only (including state-of-being).
Or to rephrase, unlike English, the only thing you need to make a grammatically complete sentence is a verb and nothing else! That’s why even the simplest, most basic Japanese sentence cannot be translated into English!
meaning: Eat. (possible translations include: I eat/she eats/they eat)
context_notes: 039 Basic Grammar - Verb Basics: Role of Verbs

explanation:
Before we can learn any verb conjugations, we first need to learn how verbs are categorized. With the exception of only two exception verbs, all verbs fall into the category of ru-verb or u-verb.
All ru-verbs end in 「る」 while u-verbs can end in a number of u-vowel sounds including 「る」. Therefore, if a verb does not end in 「る」, it will always be an u-verb. For verbs ending in 「る」, if the vowel sound preceding the 「る」 is an /a/, /u/ or /o/ vowel sound, it will always be an u-verb. Otherwise, if the preceding sound is an /i/ or /e/ vowel sound, it will be a ru-verb in most cases.
primary_text: アリスは 食[た;n]べる 。
meaning: As for Alice, eat.
context_notes: 040 Basic Grammar - Verb Basics: Classifying verbs into ru-verbs and u-verbs

example_sentences.ja: ジムが 来[く;n]る 。
example_sentences.en: Jim is the one that comes.

example_sentences.ja: ボブも する[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Bob also do.

example_sentences.ja: お 金[かね]が ある[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: There is money. (lit: Money is the thing that exists.)

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]は 買[か;n]う 。
example_sentences.en: As for me, buy.

example_sentences.ja: 猫[ねこ]は いる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: There is cat. (lit: As for cat, it exists.)

explanation: Now that we’ve seen how to declare things and perform actions with verbs, we want to be able to say the negative. In other words, we want to say that such-and-such action was not performed. This is done by conjugating the verb to the negative form just like the state-of-being for nouns and adjectives. However, the rules are a tad more complicated.
primary_text: アリスは 食[た;n]べない 。
meaning: As for Alice, does not eat.
context_notes: 046 Basic Grammar - Negative Verbs: Conjugating verbs into the negative

example_sentences.ja: ジムが 遊[あそ;n]ばない 。
example_sentences.en: Jim is the one that does not play.

example_sentences.ja: ボブも しない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Bob also does not do.

example_sentences.ja: お 金[かね]が ない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: There is no money. (lit: Money is the thing that does not exist.)

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]は 買[か;n]わない 。
example_sentences.en: As for me, not buy.

example_sentences.ja: 猫[ねこ]は いない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: There is no cat. (lit: As for cat, does not exist.)

explanation:
We will finish defining all the basic properties of verbs by learning how to express the past and past-negative tense of actions. I will warn you in advance that the conjugation rules in this section will be the most complex rules you will learn in all of Japanese. On the one hand, once you have this section nailed, all other rules of conjugation will seem simple. On the other hand, you might need to refer back to this section many times before you finally get all the rules. You will probably need a great deal of practice until you can become familiar with all the different conjugations.
We will start off with the easy ru-verb category. To change a ru-verb from the dictionary form into the past tense, you simply drop the 「る」 and add 「た」.
Changing a u-verb from dictionary form to the past tense is difficult because we must break up u-verbs into four additional categories. These four categories depend on the last character of the verb. The table below illustrates the different sub-categories. In addition, there is one exception to the rules, which is the verb 「行く」. I’ve bundled it with the regular exception verbs 「する」 and 「来る」 even though 「行く」 is a regular u-verb in all other conjugations.
primary_text: ご 飯[はん]は、 食[た;n]べた 。
meaning: As for meal, ate.
context_notes: 052 Basic Grammar - Past Tense: Past tense for ru-verbs

example_sentences.ja: 映画[えいが]は、 全部[ぜんぶ] 見[み;n]た 。
example_sentences.en: As for movie, saw them all.

example_sentences.ja: 今日[きょう]は、 走[はし;n]った 。
example_sentences.en: As for today, ran.

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち]が 来[き;n]た 。
example_sentences.en: Friend is the one that came.

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]も 遊[あそ;n]んだ 。
example_sentences.en: I also played.

example_sentences.ja: 勉強[べんきょう]は、 した[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: About study, did it.

explanation: The conjugation rules for the past-negative tense are the same for all verbs. You might have noticed that the negative of just about everything always end in 「ない」. The conjugation rule for the past-negative tense of verbs is pretty much the same as all the other negatives that end in 「ない」. You simply take the negative of any verb, remove the 「い」 from the 「ない」 ending, and replace it with 「かった」.
primary_text: アリスは 食[た;n]べなかった 。
meaning: As for Alice, did not eat.
item_key: grammar:アリスは食べなかった。
reading: アリスはたべなかった。
context_notes: 058 Basic Grammar - Past Tense: Past-negative tense for all verbs

example_sentences.ja: ジムが しなかった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Jim is the one that did not do.

example_sentences.ja: ボブも 行[い;n]かなかった 。
example_sentences.en: Bob also did not go.

example_sentences.ja: お 金[かね]が なかった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: There was no money. (lit: As for money, did not exist.)

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]は 買[か;n]わなかった 。
example_sentences.en: As for me, did not buy.

example_sentences.ja: 猫[ねこ]は いなかった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: There was no cat. (lit: As for cat, did not exist.)

explanation: The first particle we will learn is the object particle because it is a very straightforward particle. The 「を」 character is attached to the end of a word to signify that that word is the direct object of the verb. This character is essentially never used anywhere else. That is why the katakana equivalent 「ヲ」 is almost never used since particles are always written in hiragana. The 「を」 character, while technically pronounced as /wo/ essentially sounds like /o/ in real speech. Here are some examples of the direct object particle in action.
primary_text: 魚[さかな] を[;n] 食[た]べる。
meaning: Eat fish.
context_notes: 064 Basic Grammar - Particles used with verbs: The direct object 「を」 particle

example_sentences.ja: ジュース を[;n] 飲[の]んだ。
example_sentences.en: Drank juice.

explanation: Unlike the direct object we’re familiar with in English, places can also be the direct object of motion verbs such as 「歩く」 and 「走る」. Since the motion verb is done to the location, the concept of direct object is the same in Japanese. However, as you can see by the next examples, it often translates to something different in English due to the slight difference of the concept of direct object.
primary_text: 街[まち] を[;n] ぶらぶら 歩[ある]く。
meaning: Aimlessly walk through town. (Lit: Aimlessly walk town)
reading: まちをぶらぶらあるく。
context_notes: 066 Basic Grammar - Particles used with verbs: The direct object 「を」 particle

example_sentences.ja: 高速[こうそく] 道路[どうろ] を[;n] 走[はし]る。
example_sentences.en: Run through expressway. (Lit: Run expressway)

explanation: When you use 「する」 with a noun, the 「を」 particle is optional and you can treat the whole [noun+する] as one verb.
primary_text: 毎日[まいにち]、 日本語[にほんご]を 勉強[べんきょう;n]する 。
meaning: Study Japanese every day.
item_key: grammar:毎日、日本語を勉強する。
reading: まいにち、にほんごをべんきょうする。
context_notes: 068 Basic Grammar - Particles used with verbs: The direct object 「を」 particle

example_sentences.ja: メールアドレスを 登録[とうろく;n]した 。
example_sentences.en: Registered email address.

explanation: The 「に」 particle can specify a target of a verb. This is different from the 「を」 particle in which the verb does something to the direct object. With the 「に」 particle, the verb does something toward the word associated with the 「に」 particle. For example, the target of any motion verb is specified by the 「に」 particle.
primary_text: ボブは 日本[にほん] に[;n] 行[い]った。
meaning:
Bob went 
to
 Japan.
reading: ボブはにほんにいった。
context_notes: 070 Basic Grammar - Particles used with verbs: The target 「に」 particle

example_sentences.ja: 家[いえ] に[;n] 帰[かえ]らない。
example_sentences.en: Not go back home.

example_sentences.ja: 部屋[へや] に[;n] くる。
example_sentences.en:
Come 
to
 room.

explanation: As you can see in the last example, the target particle always targets “to” rather than “from”. If you wanted to say, “come from” for example, you would need to use 「から」, which means “from”. If you used 「に」, it would instead mean “come to“. 「から」 is also often paired with 「まで」, which means “up to”.
primary_text: アリスは、アメリカ から[;n] きた。
meaning:
Alice came 
from
 America.
item_key: grammar:アリスは、アメリカからきた。

example_sentences.ja: 宿題[しゅくだい]を 今日[きょう] から[;n] 明日[あした] まで[;n] する。
example_sentences.en:
Will do homework 
from
 today 
to
 tomorrow.

explanation: The idea of a target in Japanese is very general and is not restricted to motion verbs. For example, the location of an object is defined as the target of the verb for existence （ある and いる）. Time is also a common target. Here are some examples of non-motion verbs and their targets.
primary_text: 猫[ねこ]は 部屋[へや] に[;n] いる。
meaning:
Cat is 
in
 room.
context_notes: 075 Basic Grammar - Particles used with verbs: The target 「に」 particle

example_sentences.ja: いすが 台所[だいどころ] に[;n] あった。
example_sentences.en:
Chair was 
in
 the kitchen.

example_sentences.ja: いい 友達[ともだち] に[;n] 会[あ]った。
example_sentences.en: Met good friend.

example_sentences.ja: ジムは 医者[いしゃ] に[;n] なる。
example_sentences.en: Jim will become doctor.

example_sentences.ja: 先週[せんしゅう] に[;n] 図書館[としょかん] に[;n] 行[い]った。
example_sentences.en:
Went 
to
 library last week.

explanation: While the 「に」 particle is not always required to indicate time, there is a slight difference in meaning between using the target particle and not using anything at all. In the following examples, the target particle makes the date a specific target emphasizing that the friend will go to Japan at that time. Without the particle, there is no special emphasis.
primary_text:
友達[ともだち]は、 来年[らいねん]、 日本[にほん]に 行[い]く。
友達[ともだち]は、 来年[らいねん] に[;n] 日本[にほん]に 行[い]く。
meaning:
Next year, friend go to Japan.
Friend go to Japan next year.
item_key: grammar:友達は、来年、日本に行く。友達は、来年に日本に行く。

explanation: While 「へ」 is normally pronounced /he/, when it is being used as a particle, it is always pronounced /e/ （え）. The primary difference between the 「に」 and 「へ」 particle is that 「に」 goes to a target as the final, intended destination (both physical or abstract). The 「へ」 particle, on the other hand, is used to express the fact that one is setting out towards the direction of the target. As a result, it is only used with directional motion verbs. It also does not guarantee whether the target is the final intended destination, only that one is heading towards that direction. In other words, the 「に」 particle sticks to the destination while the 「へ」 particle is fuzzy about where one is ultimately headed. For example, if we choose to replace 「に」 with 「へ」 in the first three examples of the previous section, the nuance changes slightly.
primary_text: ボブは 日本[にほん] へ[;n] 行[い]った。
meaning:
Bob 
headed towards
 Japan.
reading: ボブはにほんへいった。
context_notes: 082 Basic Grammar - Particles used with verbs: The directional 「へ」 particle

example_sentences.ja: 家[いえ] へ[;n] 帰[かえ]らない。
example_sentences.en:
Not go home 
toward
 house.

example_sentences.ja: 部屋[へや] へ[;n] くる。
example_sentences.en:
Come 
towards
 room.

explanation:
Note that we cannot use the 「へ」 particle with verbs that have no physical direction. For example, the following is incorrect.
医者へなる。
（Grammatically incorrect version of 「医者になる」.）
This does not mean to say that 「へ」 cannot set out towards an abstract concept. In fact, because of the fuzzy directional meaning of this particle, the 「へ」 particle can also be used to talk about setting out towards certain future goals or expectations.
primary_text: 勝[か]ち へ[;n] 向[む]かう。
meaning:
Go 
towards
 victory.
context_notes: 085 Basic Grammar - Particles used with verbs: The directional 「へ」 particle

explanation: The 「で」 particle will allow us to specify the context in which the action is performed. For example, if a person ate a fish, where did he eat it? If a person went to school, by what means did she go? With what will you eat the soup? All of these questions can be answered with the 「で」 particle. Here are some examples.
primary_text: 映画館[えいがかん] で[;n] 見[み]た。
meaning:
Saw 
at
 movie theater.
context_notes: 086 Basic Grammar - Particles used with verbs: The contextual 「で」 particle

example_sentences.ja: バス で[;n] 帰[かえ]る。
example_sentences.en:
Go home 
by
 bus.

example_sentences.ja: レストラン で[;n] 昼[ひる]ご 飯[はん]を 食[た]べた。
example_sentences.en:
Ate lunch 
at
 restaurant.

explanation:
The word for “what” （何） is quite annoying because while it’s usually read as 「なに」, sometimes it is read as 「なん」 depending on how it’s used. And since it’s always written in Kanji, you can’t tell which it is. I would suggest sticking with 「なに」 until someone corrects you for when it should be 「なん」. With the 「で」 particle, it is read as 「なに」 as well.
Here’s the confusing part. There is a colloquial version of the word “why” that is used much more often than the less colloquial version 「どうして」 or the more forceful 「なぜ」. It is also written as 「何で」 but it is read as 「なんで」. This is a completely separate word and has nothing to do with the 「で」 particle.
The 「から」 here meaning “because” is different from the 「から」 we just learned and will be covered later in the compound sentence section. Basically the point is that the two sentences, while written the same way, are read differently and mean completely different things. Don’t worry. This causes less confusion than you think because 95% of the time, the latter is used rather than the former. And even when 「なにで」 is intended, the context will leave no mistake on which one is being used. Even in this short example snippet, you can tell which it is by looking at the answer to the question.
primary_text:
何[なに] で[;n] きた？
バス で[;n] きた。

何[なん;n]で きた？

暇[ひま]だから。
meaning:
Came 
by the way of
 what?
Came 
by the way of
 bus.

Why
 did you come?

Because I am free (as in have nothing to do).
item_key: grammar:何できた?バスできた。何できた?暇だから。

explanation: There are times when the location of an action is also the topic of a sentence. You can attach the topic particle （「は」 and 「も」） to the three particles that indicate location （「に」、「へ」、「で」） when the location is the topic.
primary_text:
ボブ「 学校[がっこう]に 行[い]った？」
アリス「 行[い]かなかった。」

ボブ「 図書館[としょかん] には[;n] ？」

アリス「 図書館[としょかん] にも[;n] 行[い]かなかった。」
meaning:
Bob: (Did you) go to school?
Alice: Didn’t go.

Bob: What about library?

Alice: Also didn’t go to library.
item_key: grammar:ボブ「学校に行った?」アリス「行かなかった。」ボブ「図書館には?」アリス「図書館にも行かなかった。」

example_sentences.ja:
ボブ「どこで 食[た]べる？」
アリス「イタリアレストラン では[;n] どう？」
example_sentences.en:
Bob: Eat where?
Alice: How about Italian restaurant?

explanation:
The direct object particle is different from particles related to location in that you cannot use any other particles at the same time. For example, going by the previous section, you might have guessed that you can say 「をは」 to express a direct object that is also the topic but this is not the case. A topic can be a direct object without using the 「を」 particle. In fact, putting the 「を」 particle in will make it wrong.
Please take care to not make this mistake.
日本語をは、習う。
(This is incorrect.)
primary_text:
日本語[にほんご] を[;n] 習[なら]う。
日本語[にほんご] は[;n] 、 習[なら]う。
meaning:
Learn Japanese.
About Japanese, (will) learn it.
item_key: grammar:日本語を習う。日本語は、習う。

explanation:
In Japanese, sometimes there are two types of the same verb often referred to as transitive and intransitive verbs. The difference between the two is that one verb is an action done by an active agent while the other is something that occurs without a direct agent. In English, this is sometimes expressed with the same verb, such as: “The ball dropped” vs “I dropped the ball” but in Japanese it becomes　「ボールが落ちた」 vs 「ボールを落とした」.　Sometimes, the verbs changes when translated into English such as “To put it in the box” (箱に入れる） vs “To enter the box” （箱に入る） but this is only from the differences in the languages. If you think in Japanese, intransitive and transitive verbs have the same meaning except that one indicates that someone had a direct hand in the action (direct object) while the other does not. While knowing the terminology is not important, it is important to know which is which in order to use the correct particle for the correct verb.
The important lesson to take away here is to learn how to use the correct particle for the correct type of verb. It might be difficult at first to grasp which is which when learning new verbs or whether there even is a transitive/intransitive distinction. If you’re not sure, you can always check whether a verb is transitive or intransitive by using an online dictionary such as jisho.org
The important thing to remember is that intransitive verbs cannot have a direct object because there is no direct acting agent. The following sentences are grammatically incorrect.
電気をついた。
（「を」 should be replaced with 「が」 or 「は」）
電気を消える。
（「を」 should be replaced with 「が」 or 「は」）
どうして窓を開いた？
（「を」 should be replaced with 「が」 or 「は」）
The only time you can use the 「を」 particle for intransitive verbs is when a location is the direct object of a motion verb as briefly described in the previous section.
部屋を出た。
I left room.
primary_text:
私[わたし]が 電気[でんき] を[;n] つけた。
電気[でんき] が[;n] ついた。
meaning:
I am the one that turned on the lights.
The lights turned on.
item_key: grammar:私が電気をつけた。電気がついた。

example_sentences.ja:
電気[でんき] を[;n] 消[け]す。
電気[でんき] が[;n] 消[き]える。
example_sentences.en:
Turn off the lights.
Lights turn off.

example_sentences.ja:
誰[だれ]が 窓[まど] を[;n] 開[あ]けた？
窓[まど] が[;n] どうして 開[あ]いた？
example_sentences.en:
Who opened the window?
Why has the window opened?

explanation:
The negative, past, and negative past conjugations of verbs can be used just like adjectives to directly modify nouns. However, we cannot do this with the plain non-past state-of-being using 「だ」. (I told you this was a pain in the butt.) The language has particles for this purpose, which will be covered in the next section.
You can, however, have a string of nouns placed together when they’re not meant to modify each other. For example, in a phrase such as “International Education Center” you can see that it is just a string of nouns without any grammatical modifications between them. It’s not an “Education Center that is International” or a “Center for International Education”, etc., it’s just “International Education Center”. In Japanese, you can express this as simply 「国際教育センタ」 (or 「センター」). You will see this chaining of nouns in many combinations. Sometimes a certain combination is so commonly used that it has almost become a separate word and is even listed as a separate entry in some dictionaries. Some examples include: 「登場人物」、「立入禁止」、or 「通勤手当」. If you have difficulties in figuring out where to separate the words, you can paste them into the WWWJDICs Translate Words in Japanese Text function and it’ll parse the words for you (most of the time).
primary_text: 学生[がくせい;n]じゃない 人[ひと]は、 学校[がっこう]に 行[い]かない。
meaning:
Person 
who is not student
 do not go to school.
item_key: grammar:学生じゃない人は、学校に行かない。
reading: がくせいじゃないひとは、がっこうにいかない。
context_notes: 102 Basic Grammar - Descriptive Subordinate Clauses and Sentence Order: Using state-of-being subclauses as adjectives

example_sentences.ja: 子供[こども;n]だった アリスが 立派[りっぱ]な 大人[おとな]になった。
example_sentences.en:
The Alice 
that was a child
 became a fine adult.

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち;n]じゃなかった アリスは、いい 友達[ともだち]になった。
example_sentences.en:
Alice 
who was not a friend
, became a good friend.

example_sentences.ja: 先週[せんしゅう;n] 医者[いしゃ;n]だった ボブは、 仕事[しごと]を 辞[や]めた。
example_sentences.en:
Bob 
who was a doctor last week
 quit his job.

explanation: Verbs clauses can also be used just like adjectives to modify nouns.
primary_text: 先週[せんしゅう;n]に 映画[えいが;n]を 見[み;n]た 人[ひと]は 誰[だれ]？
meaning:
Who is person 
who watched movie last week
?
item_key: grammar:先週に映画を見た人は誰?
reading: せんしゅうにえいがをみたひとはだれ？
context_notes: 106 Basic Grammar - Descriptive Subordinate Clauses and Sentence Order: Using subordinate verb clauses as adjectives

example_sentences.ja: ボブは、 いつも[;n] 勉強[べんきょう;n]する 人[ひと]だ。
example_sentences.en:
Bob is a person 
who always studies
.

example_sentences.ja: 赤[あか;n]いズボンを 買[か;n]う 友達[ともだち]はボブだ。
example_sentences.en:
Friend 
who buy red pants
 is Bob.

example_sentences.ja: 晩[ばん;n]ご 飯[はん;n]を 食[た;n]べなかった 人[ひと]は、 映画[えいが;n]で 見[み;n]た 銀行[ぎんこう]に 行[い]った。
example_sentences.en:
Person 
who did not eat dinner
 went to the bank 
she saw at movie
.

explanation: The 「と」 particle is similar to the 「も」 particle in that it contains a meaning of inclusion. It can combine two or more nouns together to mean “and”.
primary_text: ナイフ と[;n] フォークでステーキを 食[た]べた。
meaning:
Ate steak by means of knife 
and
 fork.
item_key: grammar:ナイフとフォークでステーキを食べた。

example_sentences.ja: 本[ほん] と[;n] 雑誌[ざっし] と[;n] 葉書[はがき]を 買[か]った。
example_sentences.en:
Bought book
,
 magazine
, and
 post card.

explanation: Another similar use of the 「と」 particle is to show an action that was done together with someone or something else.
primary_text: 友達[ともだち] と[;n] 話[はな]した。
meaning:
Talked 
with
 friend.
context_notes: 112 Basic Grammar - Noun-related Particles: The Inclusive 「と」 particle

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい] と[;n] 会[あ]った。
example_sentences.en:
Met 
with
 teacher.

explanation: The 「や」 particle, just like the 「と」 particle, is used to list one or more nouns except that it is much more vague than the 「と」 particle. It implies that there may be other things that are unlisted and that not all items in the list may apply. In English, you might think of this as an “and/or, etc.” type of listing.
primary_text: 飲[の]み 物[もの] や[;n] カップ や[;n] ナプキンは、いらない？
meaning:
You don’t need 
(things like)
 drink
,
 cup
, or
 napkin
, etc.
?
item_key: grammar:飲み物やカップやナプキンは、いらない?
reading: のみものやカップやナプキンは、いらない？
context_notes:
114 Basic Grammar - Noun-related Particles: The Vague Listing 
「や」
 and 「とか」 particles

example_sentences.ja: 靴[くつ] や[;n] シャツを 買[か]う。
example_sentences.en:
Buy 
(things like)
 shoes 
and
 shirt
, etc…

explanation: 「とか」 also has the same meaning as 「や」 but is a slightly more colloquial expression.
primary_text: 飲[の]み 物[もの] とか[;n] カップ とか[;n] ナプキンは、いらない？
meaning:
You don’t need 
(things like)
 drink
,
 cup
, or
 napkin
, etc.
?
item_key: grammar:飲み物とかカップとかナプキンは、いらない?
reading: のみものとかカップとかナプキンは、いらない？
context_notes:
116 Basic Grammar - Noun-related Particles: The Vague Listing 「や」 and 
「とか」
 particles

example_sentences.ja: 靴[くつ] とか[;n] シャツを 買[か]う。
example_sentences.en:
Buy 
(things like) 
shoes 
and
 shirt
, etc…

explanation: The 「の」 particle has many uses and it is a very powerful particle. It is introduced here because like the 「と」 and 「や」 particle, it can be used to connect one or more nouns. Let’s look at a few examples.
primary_text: ボブ の[;n] 本[ほん]。
meaning:
Book 
of
 Bob.
context_notes: 118 Basic Grammar - Noun-related Particles: The 「の」 particle

example_sentences.ja: ボブは、アメリカ の[;n] 大学[だいがく] の[;n] 学生[がくせい]だ。
example_sentences.en:
Bob is student 
of
 college 
of
 America.

explanation:
The noun that is being modified can be omitted if the context clearly indicates what is being omitted.
（「その」 is an abbreviation of 「それ+の」 so it directly modifies the noun because the 「の」 particle is intrinsically attached. Other words include 「この」 from 「これの」 and 「あの」 from 「あれの」.）
The 「の」 particle in this usage essentially replaces the noun and takes over the role as a noun itself. We can essentially treat adjectives and verbs just like nouns by adding the 「の」 particle to it. The particle then becomes a generic noun, which we can treat just like a regular noun.
However, the 「の」 particle is very useful in that you don’t have to specify a particular noun.
Even when substituting 「の」 for a noun, you still need the 「な」 to modify the noun when a na-adjective is being used.
primary_text:
そのシャツは 誰[だれ] の[;n] ？
ボブ の[;n] だ。
meaning:
Whose shirt is that?
It is of Bob.
item_key: grammar:そのシャツは誰の?ボブのだ。

example_sentences.ja: 白[しろ;n]いの は、かわいい。
example_sentences.en: Thing that is white is cute.

example_sentences.ja: 授業[じゅぎょう;n]に 行[い;n]くの を 忘[わす]れた。
example_sentences.en: Forgot the event of going to class.

example_sentences.ja: 毎日[まいにち;n] 勉強[べんきょう;n]するの は 大変[たいへん]。
example_sentences.en: The thing of studying every day is tough.

example_sentences.ja: 毎日[まいにち;n] 同[おな;n]じ 物[もの;n]を 食[た;n]べるの は、 面白[おもしろ]くない。
example_sentences.en: It’s not interesting to eat same thing every day.

example_sentences.ja: 静[しず;n]かなの が、アリスの 部屋[へや]だ。
example_sentences.en: Quiet one is room of Alice.

explanation:
The 「の」 particle attached at the end of the last clause of a sentence can also convey an explanatory tone to your sentence. For example, if someone asked you if you have time, you might respond, “The thing is I’m kind of busy right now.” The abstract generic noun of “the thing is…” can also be expressed with the 「の」 particle. This type of sentence has an embedded meaning that explains the reason(s) for something else.
To express state-of-being, when the 「の」 particle is used to convey this explanatory tone, we need to add 「な」 to distinguish it from the 「の」 particle that simply means “of”.
primary_text: 今[いま]は 忙[いそが]しい の[;n] 。
meaning: The thing is that (I’m) busy now. (feminine)
context_notes: 128 Basic Grammar - Noun-related Particles: The 「の」 particle as explanation

example_sentences.ja: 今[いま]は 忙[いそが]しい のだ[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: The thing is that (I’m) busy now. (masculine)

example_sentences.ja: 今[いま]は 忙[いそが]しい の[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: Is it that (you) are busy now? (gender-neutral)

example_sentences.ja:
ジムのだ。
ジム なのだ[;n] 。
example_sentences.en:
It is of Jim. (It is Jim’s.)
It is Jim (with explanatory tone).

example_sentences.ja:
アリス「どこに 行[い]く の[;n] ？」
ボブ「 授業[じゅぎょう]に 行[い]く んだ[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
Alice: Where is it that (you) are going?
Bob: It is that (I) go to class.

Alice: Where are you going? (Seeking explanation)
Bob: I’m going to class. (Explanatory)

example_sentences.ja:
アリス「 今[いま]、 授業[じゅぎょう]がある んじゃない[;n] ？」
ボブ「 今[いま]は、ない んだ[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
Alice: Isn’t it that there is class now?
Bob: Now it is that there is no class.

Alice: Don’t you have class now? (Expecting that there is class)
Bob: No, there is no class now. (Explanatory)

example_sentences.ja:
アリス「 今[いま]、 授業[じゅぎょう]がない んじゃない[;n] ？」
ボブ「ううん、ある。」
example_sentences.en:
Alice: Isn’t it that there isn’t class now?
Bob: No, there is.

Alice: Don’t you not have class now? (Expecting that there is no class)
Bob: No, I do have class.

example_sentences.ja:
アリス「その 人[ひと] が 買[か]う んじゃなかったの[;n] ？」
ボブ「ううん、 先生[せんせい]が 買[か]う んだ[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
Alice: Wasn’t it that that person was the one to buy?
Bob: No, it is that teacher is the one to buy.

Alice: Wasn’t that person going to buy? (Expecting that the person would buy)
Bob: No, the teacher is going to. (Explanatory)

example_sentences.ja:
アリス「 朝[あさ]ご 飯[ごはん]を 食[た]べる んじゃなかった[;n] 。」
ボブ「どうして？」
example_sentences.en:
Alice: It is that breakfast wasn’t to eat.

Bob: Why?

Alice: Should not have eaten breakfast, you know. (Explaining that breakfast wasn’t to be eaten)
Bob: How come?

explanation:
Unlike English, changing adjectives to adverbs is a very simple and straightforward process. In addition, since the system of particles make sentence ordering flexible, adverbs can be placed anywhere in the clause that it applies to as long as it comes before the verb that it refers to. As usual, we have two separate rules: one for i-adjectives, and one for na-adjectives.
For i-adjectives: Substitute the 「い」 with 「く」.
Example: 早い → 早く
For na-adjectives: Attach the target particle 「に」.
Example: きれい → きれいに

Note: Not all adverbs are derived from adjectives. Some words like 「全然」 and 「たくさん」 are adverbs in themselves without any conjugation. These words can be used without particles just like regular adverbs.
primary_text: ボブは 朝[あさ]ご 飯[ごはん]を 早[はや;n]く 食[た]べた。
meaning:
Bob 
quickly
 ate breakfast.
item_key: grammar:ボブは朝ご飯を早く食べた。
reading: ボブはあさごはんをはやくたべた。
context_notes: 138 Basic Grammar - Using Adverbs and Gobi: Properties of Adverbs

example_sentences.ja: アリスは 自分[じぶん]の 部屋[へや]を きれいに[;n] した。
example_sentences.en:
Alice did her own room 
toward clean
.

example_sentences.ja: 映画[えいが]を たくさん[;n] 見[み]た。
example_sentences.en:
Saw 
a lot of
 movies.

example_sentences.ja: 最近[さいきん]、 全然[ぜんぜん;n] 食[た]べない。
example_sentences.en:
Lately, don’t eat 
at all
.

example_sentences.ja: ボブの 声[こえ]は、 結構[けっこう;n] 大[おお]きい。
example_sentences.en:
Bob’s voice is 
fairly
 large.

example_sentences.ja: この 町[まち]は、 最近[さいきん] 大[おお;n]きく 変[か]わった。
example_sentences.en:
This town had changed 
greatly
 lately.

example_sentences.ja: 図書館[としょかん]の 中[なか]では、 静[しず;n]かに する。
example_sentences.en:
Within the library, [we] do things 
quietly
.

explanation: People usually add 「ね」 to the end of their sentence when they are looking for (and expecting) agreement to what they are saying. This is equivalent to saying, “right?” or “isn’t it?” in English.
primary_text:
ボブ「いい 天気[てんき]だ ね[;n] 。」
アリス「そう ね[;n] 。」
meaning:
Bob: Good weather, 
huh
?
Alice: That is so, 
isn’t it
?
item_key: grammar:ボブ「いい天気だね。」アリス「そうね。」

example_sentences.ja:
アリス「おもしろい 映画[えいが]だった ね[;n] 。」

ボブ「え？ 全然[ぜんぜん]おもしろくなかった。」
example_sentences.en:
Alice: That was interesting movie, 
wasn’t it
?
Bob: Huh? No, it wasn’t interesting at all.

explanation: When 「よ」 is attached to the end of a sentence, it means that the speaker is informing the listener of something new. In English, we might say this with a, “You know…” such as the sentence, “You know, I’m actually a genius.”
primary_text:
アリス「 時間[じかん]がない よ[;n] 。」
ボブ「 大丈夫[だいじょうぶ]だ よ[;n] 。」
meaning:
Alice: 
You know
, there is no time.
Bob: It’s ok, 
you know
.
item_key: grammar:アリス「時間がないよ。」ボブ「大丈夫だよ。」

example_sentences.ja:
アリス「 今日[きょう]はいい 天気[てんき]だね。」
ボブ「うん。でも、 明日[あした] 雨[あめ]が 降[ふ]る よ[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
Alice: Good weather today, huh?
Bob: Yeah. But it will rain tomorrow, 
you know
.

explanation: You can also combine the two particles we just learned to create 「よね」. This is essentially used when you want to inform the listener of some new point you’re trying to make and when you’re seeking agreement on it at the same time. When combining the two, the order must always be 「よね」. You cannot reverse the order.
primary_text:
アリス「ボブは、 魚[さかな]が 好[す]きなんだ よね[;n] 。」
ボブ「そうだね。」
meaning:
Alice: 
You know
, you like fish, 
dontcha
?
Bob: That is so, huh?
item_key: grammar:アリス「ボブは、魚が好きなんだよね。」ボブ「そうだね。」

explanation:
In order to conjugate all u-verbs and ru-verbs into their respective polite forms, we will first learn about the stem of verbs. This is often called the masu-stem in Japanese textbooks but we will call it just the stem because it is used in many more conjugations than just its masu-form. The stem is really great because it’s very easy to produce and is useful in many different types of grammar.
The stem when used by itself can be a very specialized and limited way of creating nouns from verbs. While the 「の」 particle allows you to talk about verbs as if they were nouns, the stem actually turns verbs into nouns. In fact, in very rare cases, the stem is used more often than the verb itself. For example, the stem of 「怒る」（いかる） is used more often than the verb itself. The movie, “Fists of Fury” is translated as 「怒りの鉄拳」 and not 「怒る鉄拳」. In fact, 「怒る」 will most likely be read as 「おこる」, a completely different verb with the same meaning and kanji! There are a number of specific nouns (such as 「休み」) that are really verb stems that are used like regular nouns. However, in general we cannot take any verb and make it into a noun. For example, the following sentence is wrong.
However, a useful grammar that works in general for stems of all verbs is using the stem as a target with a motion verb (almost always 「行く」 and 「来る」 in this case). This grammar means, “to go or to come to do [some verb]”.
The motion target particle 「へ」 sounds like you’re literally going or coming to something while the 「に」 particle implies that you are going or coming for the purpose of doing something.
primary_text: 明日[あした]、 映画[えいが]を 見[み;n]に 行[い]く。
meaning: Tomorrow, go to see movie.

example_sentences.ja: 昨日[きのう]、 友達[ともだち]が 遊[あそ;n]びへ きた。
example_sentences.en: Yesterday, friend came to a playing activity. (Sounds a bit strange)

example_sentences.ja: 昨日[きのう]、 友達[ともだち]が 遊[あそ;n]びに きた。
example_sentences.en: Yesterday, friend came to play.

explanation: Of course, the reason I introduced the verb stem is to learn how to conjugate verbs into their polite form… the masu-form! The masu-form must always come at the end of a complete sentence and never inside a modifying relative clause. When we learn compound sentences, we will see that each sub-sentence of the compound sentence can end in masu-form as well.
primary_text: 明日[あした]、 大学[だいがく]に 行[い;n]きます 。
meaning: Tomorrow, go to college.

example_sentences.ja: 先週[せんしゅう]、ボブに 会[あ;n]いました よ。
example_sentences.en: You know, met Bob last week.

example_sentences.ja: 晩[ばん]ご 飯[はん]を 食[た;n]べませんでした ね。
example_sentences.en: Didn’t eat dinner, huh?

example_sentences.ja: 面白[おもしろ]くない 映画[えいが]は 見[み;n]ません 。
example_sentences.en: About not interesting movies, do not see (them).

explanation:
For any sentence that does not end in a ru-verb or u-verb, the only thing that needs to be done is to add 「です」 or 「でした」. You can also do this for substituted nouns (both 「の」 and 「ん」) by just treating them like regular nouns. Another important thing to remember is that if there is a declarative 「だ」, it must be removed. In being polite, I guess you can’t be so bold as to forwardly declare things the way 「だ」 does. Just like the masu-form, this must also go at the end of a complete sentence. Here is a chart illustrating the conjugations.
※ Notice in the case of noun/na-adjective only, the past tense becomes 「でした」. A very common mistake is to do the same for i-adjectives. Remember 「かわいいでした」 is wrong!
primary_text: 子犬[こいぬ]はとても 好[す;n]きです 。
meaning: About puppies, like very much. (The most natural translation is that someone likes puppies very much but there is not enough context to rule out that the puppies like something very much.)
item_key: grammar:子犬はとても好きです。

example_sentences.ja: 昨日[きのう]、 時間[じかん]が なかったんです[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: It was that there was no time yesterday.

example_sentences.ja: その 部屋[へや]はあまり 静[しず;n]かじゃないです 。
example_sentences.en: That room is not very quiet.

example_sentences.ja: 先週[せんしゅう]に 見[み]た 映画[えいが]は、とても 面白[おもしろ;n]かったです 。
example_sentences.en: Movie saw last week was very interesting.

example_sentences.ja: その 部屋[へや]はあまり 静[しず;n]かじゃありません よ。
example_sentences.en: You know, that room is not very quiet.

explanation: There are many ways to say “I” in Japanese. Some of these words are not as common and others are hopelessly outdated. We will go over the most common ones that are in use today. The usages of all the different words for “I” is separated into two categories: gender and politeness. In other words, there are words that are usually used by males and words that are usually only used by females and they all depend on the social context.
primary_text: 私[わたし;n] の 名前[なまえ]はキムです。
meaning: My name is Kim. (Neutral, polite)

example_sentences.ja: 僕[ぼく;n] の 名前[なまえ]はキムです。
example_sentences.en: My name is Kim. (Masculine, polite)

example_sentences.ja: 僕[ぼく;n] の 名前[なまえ]はボブだ。
example_sentences.en: My name is Bob. (Masculine, casual)

example_sentences.ja: 俺[おれ;n] の 名前[なまえ]はボブだ。
example_sentences.en: My name is Bob. (Masculine, casual)

example_sentences.ja: あたし[;n] の 名前[なまえ]はアリス。
example_sentences.en: My name is Alice. (Feminine, casual)

explanation: Japanese does not require the use of “you” nearly as much as English does. I hope that the examples with Bob, Alice, and Jim have shown that people refer to other people by their names even when they are directly addressing that person. Another common way to address people is by their title such as 「社長」、「課長」、「先生」, etc. The word 「先生」 is used to generally mean any person who has significant knowledge and expertise in something. For example, people usually use 「先生」 when directly addressing doctors or teachers (obviously). You can also include the person’s last name such as 「田中先生」 (teacher Tanaka). In the case where your relationship with the person doesn’t involve any title, you can use their name (usually their last name) attached with 「さん」 to show politeness. If calling them by their last name seems a little too polite and distant, the practice of attaching 「さん」 to their first name also exists. More endearing and colloquial versions of 「さん」 include 「くん」 and 「ちゃん」. 「くん」 is usually attached to the name of males who are of equal or lower social position. (For example, my boss sometimes calls me 「キムくん」). 「ちゃん」 is a very endearing way to refer to usually females of equal or lower social position.
meaning: (polite)

example_sentences.ja: ～くん
example_sentences.en: (usually attached to the name of males who are of equal or lower social position)

example_sentences.ja: ～ちゃん
example_sentences.en: (very endearing way to refer to usually females of equal or lower social position)

explanation:
It makes sense to conclude that the question marker would work in exactly the same way in casual speech as it does in polite speech. However, this is not the case. The question marker 「か」 is usually not used with casual speech to make actual questions. It is often used to consider whether something is true or not. Depending on the context and intonation, it can also be used to make rhetorical questions or to express sarcasm. It can sound quite rough so you might want to be careful about using 「か」 for questions in the plain casual form.
Instead of 「か」, real questions in casual speech are usually asked with the explanatory の particle or nothing at all except for a rise in intonation, as we have already seen in previous sections.
primary_text: こんなのを 本当[ほんき]に 食[た]べる か[;n] ？
meaning: Do you think [he/she] will really eat this type of thing?
item_key: grammar:こんなのを本当に食べるか?

example_sentences.ja: そんなのは、あるか よ[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: Do I look like I would have something like that?!

explanation:
Another use of the question marker is simply grammatical and has nothing to do with the politeness. A question marker attached to the end of a relative clause makes a mini-question inside a larger sentence. This allows the speaker to talk about the question. For example, you can talk about the question, “What did I eat today?” In the following examples, the question that is being considered is in red.
In sentences where the question being considered has a yes/no answer, it is common (but not necessary) to attach 「どうか」. This is roughly equivalent to saying, “whether or not” in English. You can also include the alternative as well to mean the same thing.
primary_text: 昨日[きのう;n] 何[なに;n]を 食[た;n]べたか 忘[わす]れた。
meaning: Forgot what I ate yesterday.
item_key: grammar:昨日何を食べたか忘れた。

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]は 何[なに;n]を 言[い;n]ったか 分[わ]からない。
example_sentences.en: Don’t understand what he said.

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい;n]が 学校[がっこう;n]に 行[い;n]ったか 教[おし]えない？
example_sentences.en: Won’t you inform me whether teacher went to school?

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい;n]が 学校[がっこう;n]に 行[い;n]ったかどうか 知[し]らない。
example_sentences.en: Don’t know whether or not teacher went to school.

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい;n]が 学校[がっこう]に 行[い;n]ったか 行[い;n]かなかったか 知[し]らない。
example_sentences.en: Don’t know whether teacher went to school or didn’t.

explanation:
Question Words
Word+Question Marker Meaning
誰か Someone
何か Something
いつか Sometime
どこか Somewhere
どれか A certain one from many
primary_text: 誰[だれ;n]か がおいしいクッキーを 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べた。
meaning: Someone ate all the delicious cookies.
item_key: grammar:誰かがおいしいクッキーを全部食べた。

example_sentences.ja: 誰[だれ]が 盗[ぬす]んだのか、 誰[だれ;n]か 知[し]りませんか。
example_sentences.en: Doesn’t anybody know who stole it?

example_sentences.ja: 犯人[はんにん]を どこか[;n] で 見[み]ましたか。
example_sentences.en: Did you see the criminal somewhere?

example_sentences.ja: この 中[なか]から どれか[;n] を 選[えら]ぶの。
example_sentences.en: (Explaining) You are to select a certain one from inside this (selection).

explanation:
The same question words in the chart above can be combined with 「も」 in a negative sentence to mean “nobody” （誰も）, “nothing” （何も）, “nowhere” （どこも）, etc.
「誰も」 and 「何も」 are primarily used only for negative sentences. Curiously, there is no way to say “everything” with question words. Instead, it is conventional to use other words like 「全部」. And although 「誰も」 can sometimes be used to mean “everybody”, it is customary to use 「皆」 or 「皆さん」
The remaining three words 「いつも」 (meaning “always”) and 「どれも」 (meaning “any and all”), and 「どこも」 (meaning everywhere) can be used in both negative and positive sentences.
Inclusive Words
Word+も Meaning
誰も Everybody/Nobody
何も Nothing (negative only)
いつも Always
どこも Everywhere
どれも Any and all
primary_text: この 質問[しつもん]の 答[こた]えは、 誰[だれ;n]も 知[し]らない。
meaning: Nobody knows the answer of this question.
item_key: grammar:この質問の答えは、誰も知らない。

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち]は いつも[;n] 遅[おく]れる。
example_sentences.en: Friend is always late.

example_sentences.ja: ここにあるレストランは どれも[;n] おいしくない 。
example_sentences.en: Any and all restaurants that are here are not tasty.

example_sentences.ja: 今週末[こんしゅうまつ]は、 どこにも[;n] 行[い]かなかった。
example_sentences.en: Went nowhere this weekend.

explanation:
The same question words combined with 「でも」 can be used to mean “any”. One thing to be careful about is that 「何でも」 is read as 「なんでも」 and not 「なにでも」
Words for “Any”
Word+でも Meaning
誰でも Anybody
何でも Anything
いつでも Anytime
どこでも Anywhere
どれでも Whichever
primary_text: この 質問[しつもん]の 答[こた]えは、 誰[だれ;n]でも 分[わ]かる。
meaning: Anybody understands the answer of this question.
item_key: grammar:この質問の答えは、誰でも分かる。

example_sentences.ja: 昼[ひる]ご 飯[はん]は、 どこでも[;n] いいです。
example_sentences.en: About lunch, anywhere is good.

example_sentences.ja: あの 人[ひと]は、 本当[ほんき]に 何[なん;n]でも 食[た]べる。
example_sentences.en: That person really eats anything.

explanation: It is very easy to combine a chain of nouns and adjectives to describe a person or object. For example, in English if we wanted to say, “He is X. He is Y. He is Z.” since all three sentences have the same noun, we would usually say, “He is X, Y, and Z.” In Japanese, we can do the same thing by conjugating the noun or adjective. The last noun or adjective remains the same as before.
primary_text: 私[わたし]の 部屋[へや]は、きれい で[;n] 、 静[しず]か で[;n] 、とても 好[す]き。
meaning: My room is clean, quiet, and I like it a lot.
item_key: grammar:私の部屋は、きれいで、静かで、とても好き。

example_sentences.ja: 彼女[かのじょ]は、 学生[がくせい]じゃな くて[;n] 、 先生[せんせい]だ。
example_sentences.en: She is not a student, she is a teacher.

example_sentences.ja: 田中[たなか]さんは、お 金持[かねも]ち で[;n] 、かっこ よくて[;n] 、 魅力的[みりょくてき]ですね。
example_sentences.en: Tanaka-san is rich, handsome, and charming, isn’t he?

explanation: In a similar fashion, you can express multiple actions. It is usually interpreted as a sequence of event. (I did [X], then I did [Y], then I finally did [Z].) There are two forms: positive and negative. The tense of all the actions is determined by the tense of the last verb.
primary_text: 食堂[しょくどう]に 行[い;n]って 、 昼[ひる]ご 飯[はん]を 食[た;n]べて 、 昼寝[ひるね]をする。
meaning: I will go to cafeteria, eat lunch, and take a nap.
item_key: grammar:食堂に行って、昼ご飯を食べて、昼寝をする。

example_sentences.ja: 食堂[しょくどう]に 行[い;n]って 、 昼[ひる]ご 飯[はん]を 食[た;n]べて 、 昼寝[ひるね]をした。
example_sentences.en: I went to cafeteria, ate lunch, and took a nap.

example_sentences.ja: 時間[じかん]が ありまして[;n] 、 映画[えいが]を 見[み]ました。
example_sentences.en: There was time and I watched a movie.

explanation:
You can connect two complete sentences using 「から」 to indicate a reason for something. The two sentences are always ordered [reason] から [result]. When the reason is a non-conjugated noun or na-adjective, you must add 「だ」 to explicitly declare the reason in the form of 「(noun/na-adjective)だから」. If you forget to add the declarative 「だ」 to 「から」, it will end up sounding like the 「から」 meaning “from” which was first introduced in the section on particles, earlier.
When you omit the reason, you must include the declarative 「だ」 or 「です」.
primary_text: 時間[じかん]がなかった から[;n] パーティーに 行[い]きませんでした。
meaning: There was no time so didn’t go to party.
item_key: grammar:時間がなかったからパーティーに行きませんでした。

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち] だから[;n] プレゼントが 来[き]た。
example_sentences.en: Present came because (the person is) friend. (This sentence sounds a bit odd.)

example_sentences.ja:
田中[たなか]さん「どうしてパーティーに 行[い]きませんでしたか。」
山田[やまだ]さん「 時間[じかん]がなかった から[;n] です。」
example_sentences.en:
Tanaka-san: Why didn’t you go to the party?
Yamada-san: It’s because I didn’t have time.

example_sentences.ja:
一郎[いちろ]「パーティーに 行[い]かなかったの？」
直子「うん、 時間[じかん]がなかった から[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
Ichiro: You didn’t go to the party?
Naoko: Yeah, because I didn’t have time.

example_sentences.ja:
直子「 時間[じかん] がなかった。」
一郎「 だから[;n] パーティーに 行[い]かなかったの？」
example_sentences.en:
Naoko: I didn’t have time.
Ichiro: Is that why you didn’t go to the party?

explanation:
Let’s say you want to combine two sentences: 「時間がなかったのだ」 and 「パーティーに行かなかった」. Remember we can treat the 「の」 just like a noun so we can use what we just learned in the first section of this lesson. 時間がなかったのだ＋パーティーに行かなかった becomes: 時間がなかったのでパーティーに行かなかった。 In fact, 「ので」 is almost interchangeable with 「から」 with a few subtle differences. 「から」 explicitly states that the sentence preceding is the reason for something while 「ので」 is merely putting two sentences together, the first with an explanatory tone. This is something I call causation where [X] happened, therefore [Y] happened. This is slightly different from 「から」 where [Y] happened explicitly because [X] happened. This difference tends to make 「ので」 sound softer and slightly more polite and it is favored over 「から」 when explaining a reason for doing something that is considered discourteous.
Reminder: Don’t forget that the explanatory 「の」 requires a 「な」 for both non-conjugated nouns and na-adjectives. Review Particles 3 to see why.
Just like how the explanatory 「の」 can be shortened to 「ん」, in speech, the 「ので」 can be changed to 「んで」 simply because it’s easier to slur the sounds together rather than pronouncing the / o / syllable.
primary_text: ちょっと 忙[いそが]しい ので[;n] 、そろそろ 失礼[しつれい]します。
meaning: Because I’m a little busy, I’ll be making my leave soon.
item_key: grammar:ちょっと忙しいので、そろそろ失礼します。

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]は 学生[がくせい] なので[;n] 、お 金[かね]がないんです。
example_sentences.en: Because I’m a student, I have no money (lit: there is no money).

example_sentences.ja: ここは 静[しず]か なので[;n] 、とても 穏[おだ]やかです。
example_sentences.en: It is very calm here because it is quiet.

example_sentences.ja: なので[;n] 、 友達[ともだち]に 会[あ]う 時間[じかん]がない。
example_sentences.en: That’s why there’s no time to meet friend.

example_sentences.ja: 時間[じかん]がなかった んで[;n] パーティーに 行[い]かなかった。
example_sentences.en: Didn’t go to the party because there was no time.

example_sentences.ja: ここは 静[しず]か なんで[;n] 、とても 穏[おだ]やかです。
example_sentences.en: It is very calm here because it is quiet.

example_sentences.ja: なんで[;n] 、 友達[ともだち]に 会[あ]う 時間[じかん]がない。
example_sentences.en: That’s why there’s no time to meet friend.

explanation: Grammatically, 「のに」 is used exactly the same way as 「ので」. When used to combine two simple sentences together, it means “[Sentence 1] despite the fact that [Sentence 2].” However the order is reversed: [Sentence 2]のに[Sentence 1].
primary_text: 毎日[まいにち] 運動[うんどう]した のに[;n] 、 全然[ぜんぜん] 痩[や]せなかった。
meaning: Despite exercising every day, I didn’t get thinner.
item_key: grammar:毎日運動したのに、全然痩せなかった。

example_sentences.ja: 学生[がくせい] なのに[;n] 、 彼女[かのじょ]は 勉強[べんきょう]しない。
example_sentences.en: Despite being a student, she does not study.

explanation:
Used in the same manner as 「から」 and 「ので」, 「が」 and 「けど」 also connect two sentences together but this time to express a contradiction. Just like 「から」 the declarative 「だ」 is required for nouns and na-adjectives. And just like 「から」 and 「ので」, either part of the contradiction can be left out.
Though this isn’t a rule as such, it is generally common to see 「が」 attached to a 「～ます」 or 「～です」 ending and 「けど」 attached to a regular, plain ending. A more formal version of 「けど」 is 「けれど」 and even more formal is 「けれども」, which we may see later when we cover formal expressions.
primary_text: デパートに 行[い]きました が[;n] 、 何[なに]も 欲[ほ]しくなかったです。
meaning: I went to department store but there was nothing I wanted.
item_key: grammar:デパートに行きましたが、何も欲しくなかったです。

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち]に 聞[き]いた けど[;n] 、 知[し]らなかった。
example_sentences.en: I asked (or heard from) a friend but he (or I) didn’t know.

example_sentences.ja: 今日[きょう]は 暇[ひま] だけど[;n] 、 明日[あした]は 忙[いそが]しい。
example_sentences.en: I’m free today but I will be busy tomorrow.

example_sentences.ja: だけど[;n] 、 彼[かれ]がまだ 好[す]きなの。
example_sentences.en: That may be so, but it is that I still like him. [explanation, feminine tone]

explanation:
Unlike the English word for contradiction such as “but” or “however”, 「けど」 and 「が」 do not always express a direct contradiction. Often times, especially when introducing a new topic, it is used as a general connector of two separate sentences.
Sometimes, the English “and” becomes a closer translation than “but”.
primary_text: デパートに 行[い]きました が[;n] 、いい 物[もの]がたくさんありました。
meaning: I went to the department store and there was a lot of good stuff.
item_key: grammar:デパートに行きましたが、いい物がたくさんありました。

example_sentences.ja: マトリックスを 見[み]た けど[;n] 、 面白[おもしろ]かった。
example_sentences.en: I watched the “Matrix” and it was interesting.

explanation: When you want to list reasons for multiple states or actions you can do so by adding 「し」 to the end of each relative clause. It is very similar to the 「や」 particle except that it lists reasons for verbs and state-of-being. Again, for states of being, 「だ」 must be used to explicitly declare the state-of-being for any non-conjugated noun or na-adjective.
primary_text:
Ａ「どうして 友達[ともだち]じゃないんですか？」
Ｂ「 先生[せんせい] だし[;n] 、 年上[としうえ] だし[;n] ・・・。」
meaning:
A: Why isn’t (he/she) friend [seeking explanation]?
B: Well, he’s/she’s the teacher, and older…
item_key: grammar:a「どうして友達じゃないんですか?」b「先生だし、年上だし・・・。」

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「どうして 彼[かれ]が 好[す]きなの？」
Ｂ「 優[やさ]しい し[;n] 、かっこいい し[;n] 、 面白[おもしろ]いから。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Why (do you) like him?
B: Because he’s kind, attractive, and interesting (among other things).

explanation: This is the verb version of the 「や」 particle. You can make an example list of verbs among a possible larger list by conjugating each verb into the past tense and adding 「り」. At the end, you need to attach the verb 「する」. Just like the 「や」 particle, the tense is determined by the last verb, which in this case will always be 「する」 (since you have to attach it at the end). You can also use this with the state-of-being to say that you are a number of things at various random times among a larger list. Similar to regular verbs, you just take the noun or adjective for each state-of-being and conjugate it to the past state-of-being and then attach 「り」. Then finally, attach 「する」 at the end.
primary_text: 映画[えいが]を 見[み;n]たり 、 本[ほん]を 読[よ;n]んだり 、 昼寝[ひるね;n]したりする 。
meaning: I do things like (among other things) watch movies, read books, and take naps.
item_key: grammar:映画を見たり、本を読んだり、昼寝したりする。

example_sentences.ja: この 大学[だいがく]の 授業[じゅぎょう]は 簡単[かんたん] だったり[;n] 、 難[むずか;n]しかったりする 。
example_sentences.en: Class of this college is sometimes easy, sometimes difficult (and other times something else maybe).

example_sentences.ja: 映画[えいが]を 見[み;n]たり 、 本[ほん]を 読[よ;n]んだりした 。
example_sentences.en: I did things like (among other things) watch movies, and read books.

example_sentences.ja: 映画[えいが]を 見[み;n]たり 、 本[ほん]を 読[よ;n]んだりしない 。
example_sentences.en: I don’t do things like (among other things) watch movies, and read books.

example_sentences.ja: 映画[えいが]を 見[み;n]たり 、 本[ほん]を 読[よ;n]んだりしなかった 。
example_sentences.en: I didn’t do things like (among other things) watch movies, and read books.

explanation:
We already know how to express a state-of-being using 「です」, 「だ」, etc. However, it only indicates a one-time thing; you are something or not. This grammar, however, describes a continuing state of an action verb. This usually translates to the progressive form in English except for a few exceptions, which we will examine later. We can make good use of the te-form we learned in the last section because the only thing left to do is add 「いる」! You can then treat the result as a regular ru-verb.
This 「いる」 is the same ru-verb describing existence, first described in the negative verb section. However, in this case, you don’t have to worry about whether the subject is animate or inanimate.
Since people are usually too lazy to roll their tongues to properly pronounce the 「い」, it is often omitted in conversational Japanese. If you are writing an essay or paper, you should always include the 「い」.
primary_text:
Ａ「 友達[ともだち]は 何[なに]をし ている[;n] の？」
Ｂ「 昼[ひる]ご 飯[はん]を 食[た]べ ている[;n] 。」
meaning:
A: What is friend doing?
B: (Friend) is eating lunch.
item_key: grammar:a「友達は何をしているの?」b「昼ご飯を食べている。」

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 何[なに]を 読[よ]ん でいる[;n] ？」
Ｂ「 教科書[きょうかしょ]を 読[よ]ん でいます[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
A: What are you reading?
B: I am reading textbook.

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 話[はなし]を 聞[き]い ています[;n] か。」
Ｂ「ううん、 聞[き]い ていない[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Are you listening to me? (lit: Are you listening to story?)
B: No, I’m not listening.

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 友達[ともだち]は 何[なに]をし てる[;n] の？」
Ｂ「 昼[ひる]ご 飯[はん]を 食[た]べ てる[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
A: What is friend doing?
B: (Friend) is eating lunch.

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 何[なに]を 読[よ]ん でる[;n] ？」
Ｂ「 教科書[きょうかしょ]を 読[よ]ん でいます[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
A: What are you reading?
B: I am reading textbook.

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 話[はなし]を 聞[き]い ています[;n] か。」
Ｂ「ううん、 聞[き]い てない[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Are you listening to me? (lit: Are you listening to story?)
B: No, I’m not listening.

explanation:
「知る」
「知る」 means “to know”. English is weird in that “know” is supposed to be a verb but is actually describing a state of having knowledge. Japanese is more consistent and 「知る」 is just a regular action verb. In other words, I “knowed” (action) something and so now I know it (state). That’s why the English word “to know” is really a continuing state in Japanese, namely: 「知っている」.
「知る」 vs 「分かる」
「分かる」 meaning “to understand” may seem similar to 「知る」 in some cases. However, there is a difference between “knowing” and “understanding”. Try not to confuse 「知っている」 with 「分かっている」. 「分かっている」 means that you are already in a state of understanding, in other words, you already get it. If you misuse this, you may sound pompous. (“Yeah, yeah, I got it already.”) On the other hand, 「知っている」 simply means you know something.
primary_text: 今日[きょう]、 知[し;n]りました 。
meaning: I found out about it today. (I did the action of knowing today.)

example_sentences.ja: この 歌[うた]を 知[し;n]っています か？
example_sentences.en: Do (you) know this song?

example_sentences.ja: 道[みち]は 分[わ;n]かります か。
example_sentences.en: Do you know the way? (lit: Do (you) understand the road?)

example_sentences.ja: はい、はい、 分[わ;n]かった 、 分[わ;n]かった 。
example_sentences.en: Yes, yes, I got it, I got it.

explanation: It is reasonable to assume the actions 「行っている」 and 「来ている」 would mean, “going” and “coming” respectively. But unfortunately, this is not the case. The 「～ている」 form of motion verbs is more like a sequence of actions we saw in the last section. You completed the motion, and now you exist in that state. (Remember, 「いる」 is the verb of existence of animate objects.) It might help to think of it as two separate and successive actions: 「行って」、and then 「いる」.
primary_text: もう、 家[いえ]に 帰[かえ;n]っている 。
meaning:
Where is Suzuki-san?
He is already at home (went home and is there now).

example_sentences.ja: 先[さき]に 行[い;n]っている よ。
example_sentences.en: I’ll go on ahead. (I’ll go and be there before you.)

example_sentences.ja: 美恵[みえ]ちゃんは、もう 来[き;n]ている よ。
example_sentences.en: Mie-chan is already here, you know. (She came and is here.)

explanation:
Appropriately enough, just like there is an 「ある」 to go with 「いる」, there is a 「～てある」 form that also has a special meaning. By replacing 「いる」 with 「ある」, instead of a continuing action, it becomes a resultant state after the action has already taken place. Usually, this expression is used to explain that something is in a state of completion. The completed action also carries a nuance of being completed in preparation for something else.
Since this grammar describes the state of a completed action, it is common to see the 「は」 and 「も」 particles instead of the 「を」 particle.
primary_text:
Ａ「 準備[じゅんび]はどうですか。」
Ｂ「 準備[じゅんび]は、もう してある[;n] よ。」
meaning:
A: How are the preparations?
B: The preparations are already done.
item_key: grammar:a「準備はどうですか。」b「準備は、もうしてあるよ。」

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 旅行[りょこう]の 計画[けいかく]は 終[お]わった？」
Ｂ「うん、 切符[きっぷ]を 買[か]ったし、ホテルの 予約[よやく]も してある[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Are the plans for the trip complete?
B: Uh huh, not only did I buy the ticket, I also took care of the hotel reservations.

explanation:
While 「～てある」 carries a nuance of a completed action in preparation for something else, 「～ておく」 explicitly states that the action is done (or will be done) with the future in mind. Imagine this: you have made a delicious pie and you’re going to place it on the window sill for it to cool so that you can eat it later. This image might help explain why the verb 「おく」 （置く）, meaning “to place”, can be used to describe a preparation for the future. (It’s just too bad that pies on window sills always seem to go through some kind of mishap especially in cartoons.) While 「置く」 by itself is written in kanji, it is customary to use hiragana when it comes attached to a conjugated verb (such as the te-form).
「ておく」 is also sometimes abbreviated to 「～とく」 for convenience.
primary_text: 晩[ばん]ご 飯[はん]を 作[つく]っ とく[;n] 。
meaning: Make dinner (in advance for the future).

example_sentences.ja: 電池[でんち]を 買[か]っ ておきます[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I’ll buy batteries (in advance for the future).

example_sentences.ja: 晩[ばん]ご 飯[はん]を 作[つく]っ ておく[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Make dinner (in advance for the future).

example_sentences.ja: 電池[でんち]を 買[か]っ ときます[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I’ll buy batteries (in advance for the future).

explanation:
You can also use the motion verbs “to go” （行く）and “to come” （来る） with the te-form, to show that an action is oriented toward or from someplace. The most common and useful example of this is the verb 「持つ」 (to hold). While 「持っている」 means you are in a state of holding something (in possession of), when the 「いる」 is replaced with 「いく」 or 「くる」, it means you are taking or bringing something. Of course, the conjugation is the same as the regular 「行く」 and 「来る」.

The motion verbs can also be used in time expressions to move forward or come up to the present.
primary_text: 鉛筆[えんぴつ]を 学校[がっこう]へ 持[も]っ ていく[;n] ？
meaning: Are (you) taking pencil to school?
item_key: grammar:鉛筆を学校へ持っていく?

example_sentences.ja: 鉛筆[えんぴつ]を 家[いえ]に 持[も]っ てくる[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: Are (you) bringing pencil to home?

example_sentences.ja: お 父[とう]さんは、 早[はや]く 帰[かえ]っ てきました[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Father came back home early.

example_sentences.ja: 駅[えき]の 方[ほう]へ 走[はし]っ ていった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Went running toward the direction of station.

example_sentences.ja: 一生懸命[いっしょうけんめい]、 頑張[がんば]っ ていく[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: Will try my hardest (toward the future) with all my might!

example_sentences.ja: 色々[いろいろ]な 人[ひと]と 付[つ]き 合[あ]っ てきた[;n] けど、いい 人[ひと]はまだ 見[み]つからない。
example_sentences.en: Went out (up to the present) with various types of people but a good person hasn’t been found yet.

example_sentences.ja: 日本語[にほんご]をずっと 前[まえ]から 勉強[べんきょう]し てきて[;n] 、 結局[けっきょく]はやめた。
example_sentences.en: Studied Japanese from way back before and eventually quit.

explanation: In Japanese, the ability to do a certain action is expressed by conjugating the verb rather than adding a word such as the words “can” or “able to” in the case of English. All verbs conjugated into the potential form become a ru-verb.
primary_text: 漢字[かんじ]は 書[か;n]けます か？
meaning: Can you write kanji?

example_sentences.ja: 残念[ざんねん]だが、 今週末[こんしゅうまつ]は 行[い;n]けない 。
example_sentences.en: It’s unfortunate, but can’t go this weekend.

example_sentences.ja: もう 信[しん;n]じられない 。
example_sentences.en: I can’t believe it already.

explanation:
There are two verbs 「見える」 and 「聞こえる」 that mean that something is visible and audible, respectively. When you want to say that you can see or hear something, you’ll want to use these verbs. If however, you wanted to say that you were given the opportunity to see or hear something, you would use the regular potential form. 
You can see that example 3 uses the generic noun for an event to say literally, “The event of seeing movie was able to be done.” which essentially means the same thing as 「見られる」. You can also just use generic noun substitution to substitute for 「こと」.
primary_text: 友達[ともだち]のおかげで、 映画[えいが]をただで 見[み;n]ることができた 。
meaning: Thanks to (my) friend, (I) was able to watch the movie for free.
item_key: grammar:友達のおかげで、映画をただで見ることができた。

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち]のおかげで、 映画[えいが]をただで 見[み;n]るのができた 。
example_sentences.en: Thanks to (my) friend, (I) was able to watch the movie for free.

explanation: Notice that 「聞こえる」 always means “audible” and never “able to ask”.
primary_text: 久[ひさ]しぶりに 彼[かれ]の 声[こえ]が 聞[き;n]けた 。
meaning: I was able to hear his voice for the first time in a long time.
item_key: grammar:久しぶりに彼の声が聞けた。

example_sentences.ja: 周[まわ]りがうるさくて、 彼[かれ]が 言[い]っていることがあんまり 聞[き;n]こえなかった 。
example_sentences.en: The surroundings were noisy and I couldn’t hear what he was saying very well.

explanation: You can say that something has a possibility of existing by combining 「ある」 and the verb 「得る」 to produce 「あり得る」. This essentially means 「あることができる」 except that nobody actually says that, they just use 「あり得る」. This verb is very curious in that it can be read as either 「ありうる」 or 「ありえる」, however; all the other conjugations such as 「ありえない」、「ありえた」、and 「ありえなかった」 only have one possible reading using 「え」.
primary_text: そんなことは ありうる[;n] 。
meaning: That kind of situation/event is possible (lit: can exist).
item_key: grammar:そんなことはありうる。

example_sentences.ja: そんなことは ありえる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: That kind of situation/event is possible (lit: can exist).

example_sentences.ja: そんなことは ありえない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: That kind of situation/event is not possible (lit: cannot exist).

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]が 寝坊[ねぼう]したことも ありうる[;n] ね。
example_sentences.en: It’s also possible that he overslept. (lit: The event that he overslept also possibly exists.)

example_sentences.ja: それは、 ありえない[;n] 話[はなし]だよ。
example_sentences.en: That’s an impossible story/scenario. (lit: That story/scenario cannot exist.)

explanation: As already explained, using 「なる」 with nouns and na-adjectives presents nothing new and acts pretty much the way you’d expect.
primary_text: 彼[かれ]の 日本語[にほんご]が 上手[じょうず] になった[;n] 。
meaning: His Japanese has become skillful.
item_key: grammar:彼の日本語が上手になった。

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]は 医者[いしゃ] になった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I became a doctor.

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]は 有名[ゆうめい]な 人[ひと] になる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I will become a famous person.

explanation: For adjectives, using the verb 「する」 with the 「に」 particle is just a review back to the lesson on adverbs. However, for nouns, when you use the verb 「する」 with the 「に」 particle, it means that you are going to do things toward something. This changes the meaning of 「する」 to mean, “to decide on [X]”. This is a common expression to use, for instance, when you are ordering items on a menu.
primary_text: 私[わたし]は、ハンバーガーとサラダ にします[;n] 。
meaning: I’ll have the hamburger and salad. (lit: I’ll do toward hamburger and salad.)
item_key: grammar:私は、ハンバーガーとサラダにします。

example_sentences.ja: 他[ほか]にいいものがたくさんあるけど、やっぱりこれ にする[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: There are a lot of other good things, but as I thought, I’ll go with this one.

explanation: Because the 「に」 particle is a target particle that is used for nouns and by extension na-adjectives, we need to use something else to show that something is becoming an i-adjective. Since “becoming” expresses a change in state, it makes sense to describe this process using an adverb. In fact, you’ll notice that we were already using adverbs (of a sort) in the previous section by using 「に」 with na-adjectives.
primary_text: 去年[きょねん]から 背[せ]が 高[たか;n]くなった ね。
meaning: Your height has gotten taller from last year, huh?
item_key: grammar:去年から背が高くなったね。

example_sentences.ja: 運動[うんどう]しているから、 強[つよ;n]くなる 。
example_sentences.en: I will become stronger because I am exercising.

example_sentences.ja: 勉強[べんきょう]をたくさんしたから、 頭[あたま]が よくなった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Since I studied a lot, I became smarter. (lit: head became better)

explanation:
You may be wondering how to use 「なる」 and 「する」 with verbs since there’s no way to directly modify a verb with another verb. The simple solution is to add a generic noun such as a generic event: こと （事） or an appearance/manner: よう （様）. These nouns don’t refer to anything specific and are used to describe something else. In this case, they allow us to describe verbs in the same manner as nouns. Here are some examples of how to use these generic nouns with 「する」 and 「なる」.
You can modify a verb with 「なる」 or 「する」 by first making it into a noun clause and then treating it just like a regular noun. Pretty clever, huh? I hope the literal translations give you a sense of why the example sentences mean what they do. For instance, in the fourth example, 「～ようにする」 translates into “to make an effort toward…” but in Japanese, it’s really only a target towards acting in a certain manner.
Since potential verbs describe a state of feasibility rather than an action (remember, that’s why the 「を」 particle couldn’t be used), it is often used in conjunction with 「～ようになる」 to describe a change in manner to a state of feasibility. Let’s take this opportunity to get some potential conjugation practice in.
primary_text: 海外[かいがい]に 行[い]く ことになった[;n] 。
meaning: It’s been decided that I will go abroad. (lit: It became the event of going abroad.)
item_key: grammar:海外に行くことになった。

example_sentences.ja: 毎日[まいにち]、 肉[にく]を 食[た]べる ようになった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: It became so that I eat meat every day. (lit: It became the appearance of eating meat every day.)

example_sentences.ja: 海外[かいがい]に 行[い]く ことにした[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I decided I will go abroad. (lit: I did toward the event of going abroad.)

example_sentences.ja: 毎日[まいにち]、 肉[にく]を 食[た]べる ようにする[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I will try to eat meat every day. (lit: I will do toward the manner of eating meat every day.)

example_sentences.ja: 日本[にほん]に 来[き]て、 寿司[すし]が 食[た]べられる ようになった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: After coming to Japan, I became able to eat sushi.

example_sentences.ja: 一年間[いちねんかん] 練習[れんしゅう]したから、ピアノが 弾[ひ]ける ようになった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Because I practiced for one year, I became able to play the piano.

explanation:
We’ll first cover the simplest type of “if” which is the natural consequence conditional. This means that if [X] happens, [Y] will happen as a natural consequence. No question about it. If I drop a ball, it will fall to the ground. If I turn off the lights at night, it will get dark.
These examples are designed to show how 「と」 is used to express natural consequence. However, even if the statement isn’t a natural consequence in itself, the 「と」 will tell the audience that it is nevertheless expected to be a natural consequence.
primary_text: ボールを 落[おと]す と[;n] 落[お]ちる。
meaning: If you drop the ball, it will fall.
item_key: grammar:ボールを落すと落ちる。

example_sentences.ja: 電気[でんき]を 消[け]す と[;n] 暗[くら]くなる。
example_sentences.en: If you turn off the lights, it will get dark.

example_sentences.ja: 学校[がっこう]に 行[い]かない と[;n] 友達[ともだち]と 会[あ]えないよ。
example_sentences.en: If you don’t go to school, you can’t meet your friends.

example_sentences.ja: たくさん 食[た]べる と[;n] 太[ふと]るよ。
example_sentences.en: If you eat a lot, you will get fat, for sure.

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい] だと[;n] 、きっと 年上[としうえ]なんじゃないですか？
example_sentences.en: If he’s a teacher, he must be older for sure, right?

explanation:
Another relatively easy to understand type of “if” is the contextual conditional. You can use this particle to express what will happen given a certain context. For example, if you wanted to say, “Well, if everybody’s going, I’m going too” you would use the 「なら」 conditional because you are saying that you will go in the context of everybody else going. The contextual conditional always requires a context in which the conditional occurs.
