For instance, you would use it for saying things like, “If that’s what you are talking about…” or “If that’s the case, then…”
In a sense, you are explaining what would occur if you assume a certain condition is satisfied. In other words, you are saying “if given a certain context, here is what will happen.” You will see this reflected in the English translations as the phrase “if given” in the examples.
The 「なら」 is attached to the context in which the conditional occurs. The format is the same as the 「と」 conditional, however, you must not attach the declarative 「だ」.
You can also decide to use 「ならば」 instead of just 「なら」. This means exactly the same thing except that it has a more formal nuance.
primary_text: みんなが 行[い]く なら[;n] 私[わたし]も 行[い]く。
meaning: If given that everybody is going, then I’ll go too.
item_key: grammar:みんなが行くなら私も行く。

example_sentences.ja: アリスさんが 言[い]う なら[;n] 問題[もんだい]ないよ。
example_sentences.en: If given that Alice-san says so, there’s no problem.

explanation: The next type of conditional just expresses a regular “if” condition without any assumptions or embedded meanings. The conjugation rules for the 「ば」 conditional is below. Note, the conjugation rule for nouns and na-adjectives is actually using the verb 「ある」 in 「である」, a formal expression we’ll learn much later.
primary_text: 友達[ともだち]に 会[あ]えれ ば[;n] 、 買[か]い 物[もの]に 行[い]きます。
meaning: If I can meet with my friend, we will go shopping.
item_key: grammar:友達に会えれば、買い物に行きます。

example_sentences.ja: お 金[かね]があれ ば[;n] いいね。
example_sentences.en: If I had money, it would be good, huh?

example_sentences.ja: 楽[たの]しけれ ば[;n] 、 私[わたし]も 行[い]く。
example_sentences.en: If it’s fun, I’ll go too.

example_sentences.ja: 楽[たの]しくなけれ ば[;n] 、 私[わたし]も 行[い]かない。
example_sentences.en: If it’s not fun, I’ll also not go.

example_sentences.ja: 食[た]べなけれ ば[;n] 病気[びょうき]になるよ。
example_sentences.en: If you don’t eat, you will become sick.

explanation:
I call this next conditional the past conditional because it is produced by taking the past tense and just adding 「ら」. It is commonly called the 「たら」 conditional because all past-tense ends with 「た／だ」 and so it always becomes 「たら／だら」. Like the 「ば」 conditional, it is also a general conditional.
You can also use 「たらば」 instead of 「たら」. Similar to 「ならば」, this means exactly the same thing except that it has a more formal nuance.
primary_text: 暇だっ たら[;n] 、 遊[あそ]びに 行[い]くよ。
meaning: If I am free, I will go play.
item_key: grammar:暇だったら、遊びに行くよ。

example_sentences.ja: 学生[がくせい]だっ たら[;n] 、 学生[がくせい] 割引[わりびき]で 買[か]えます。
example_sentences.en: If you’re a student, you can buy with a student discount.

explanation:
For i-adjectives and verbs, it is very difficult to differentiate between the two types of conditionals, and you can make life easier for yourself by considering them to be the same. However there is a small difference in that the 「たら」 conditional focuses on what happens after the condition. This is another reason why I call this the past conditional because the condition is “in the past” (not literally) and we’re interested in the result not the condition. The 「ば」 conditional, on the other hand, focuses on the conditional part.
Going by the context, the 「～たら」 form sounds more natural for both examples because it doesn’t seem like we’re really focusing on the condition itself. We’re probably more interested in what’s going to happen once we meet the friend or how nice it would be if we had money.
primary_text:
Ａ「 友達[ともだち]に 会[あ]えれ ば[;n] 、 買[か]い 物[もの]に 行[い]きます。」
Ｂ「 友達[ともだち]に 会[あ]え たら[;n] 、 買[か]い 物[もの]に 行[い]きます。」
meaning:
A: We will go shopping, if I can meet with my friend.
B: If I can meet with my friend, we will go shopping.
item_key: grammar:a「友達に会えれば、買い物に行きます。」b「友達に会えたら、買い物に行きます。」

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「お 金[かね]があれ ば[;n] いいね。」
Ｂ「お 金[かね]があっ たら[;n] いいね。」
example_sentences.en:
A: It would be good, if I had money, huh?
B: If I had money, it would be good, huh?

explanation: The past conditional is the only type of conditional where the result can be in the past. It may seem strange to have an “if” when the result has already taken place. Indeed, in this usage, there really is no “if”, it’s just a way of expressing surprise at the result of the condition. This has little to do with conditionals but it is explained here because the grammatical structure is the same.
primary_text: 家[いえ]に 帰[かえ]っ たら[;n] 、 誰[だれ]もいなかった。
meaning: When I went home, there was no one there. (unexpected result)
item_key: grammar:家に帰ったら、誰もいなかった。

example_sentences.ja: アメリカに 行[い]っ たら[;n] 、たくさん 太[ふと]りました。
example_sentences.en: As a result of going to America, I got really fat. (unexpected result)

explanation: Some of you may be aware of the word 「もし」 which means “if” and may be wondering how it fits into all of this. Well, if you want to say a conditional, you need to use one of the conditionals discussed above. 「もし」 is really a supplement to add a sense of uncertainty on whether the condition is true. For instance, you might use it when you want to make an invitation and you don’t want to presume.
primary_text: もし[;n] よかったら、 映画[えいが]を 観[みる]に 行[い]きますか？
meaning:
If 
by any chance
 it’s ok with you, go to watch movie?
item_key: grammar:もしよかったら、映画を観に行きますか?

example_sentences.ja: もし[;n] 時間[じかん]がないなら、 明日[あした]でもいいよ。
example_sentences.en:
If 
given that
 there’s no time, tomorrow is fine as well. (Not certain whether there is no time)

explanation:
If you’re not familiar with the word 「だめ」（駄目）, though it can be used in many different ways it essentially means “no good”. The other two key words in this section are 「いけない」 and 「ならない」 and they have essentially the same basic meaning as 「だめ」. However, while 「いけない」 can be used by itself, 「ならない」 must only be used in the grammar presented here. In addition, while 「いけない」 and 「ならない」 conjugate like i-adjectives they are not actual adjectives. Let’s learn how to use these words to express things that must not be done.
The difference between 「だめ」、「いけない」、and 「ならない」 is that, first of all, 「だめ」 is casual. While 「いけない」 and 「ならない」 are basically identical, 「ならない」 is generally more for things that apply to more than one person like rules and policies.
primary_text: ここに 入[はい]っ てはいけません[;n] 。
meaning: You must not enter here.
item_key: grammar:ここに入ってはいけません。

example_sentences.ja: それを 食[た]べ てはだめ[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: You can’t (must not) eat that!

example_sentences.ja: 夜[よる]、 遅[おそ]くまで 電話[でんわ] してはならない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: You must not use the phone until late at night.

example_sentences.ja: 早[はや]く 寝[ね] てはなりませんでした[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Wasn’t allowed to sleep early.

explanation:
You may have predicted that the opposite of “You must not do” would use 「いける」 or 「なる」 because they look like the positive version of 「いけない」 and 「ならない」. However, 「いけない」 and 「ならない」 must always be negative, so this is not correct. In actuality, we still use the same 「だめ／いけない／ならない」 and use the opposite of the verb that goes in front of it instead. This double negative can be kind of confusing at first but you will get used to it with practice.
※ Reality Check
Although we spent the last section explaining 「～なければ」 and 「～なくては」, the reality is that because they are so long, they are practically never used in real conversations. While they are often used in a written context, in actual speech, people usually use the 「と」 conditional or the various shortcuts described below. In casual speech, the 「と」 conditional is the most prevalent type of conditional. Though I explained in depth the meaning associated with the 「と」 conditional, you have to take it with a grain of salt here because people are inherently lazy.
primary_text: 毎日[まいにち] 学校[がっこう]に 行[い;n]かなくては なりません。
meaning: Must go to school every day.
item_key: grammar:毎日学校に行かなくてはなりません。

example_sentences.ja: 宿題[しゅくだい]を しなくては[;n] いけなかった。
example_sentences.en: Had to do homework.

example_sentences.ja: 毎日[まいにち] 学校[がっこう]に 行[い;n]かないと だめです。
example_sentences.en: Must go to school every day.

example_sentences.ja: 宿題[しゅくだい]を しないと[;n] いけない。
example_sentences.en: Have to do homework.

example_sentences.ja: 毎日[まいにち] 学校[がっこう]に 行[い;n]かなければ いけません。
example_sentences.en: Must go to school every day.

example_sentences.ja: 宿題[しゅくだい]を しなければ[;n] だめだった。
example_sentences.en: Had to do homework.

explanation:
You may have been grumbling and complaining about how long most of the expressions are just to say you must do something. You can end up with up to eight additional syllables just to say “I have to…”!
Well, others have thought the same before and people usually use short abbreviated versions of 「なくては」 and 「なければ」 in casual speech. Teachers are often reluctant to teach these overly familiar expressions because they are so much easier to use which is bad for times when they might not be appropriate. But, on the other hand, if you don’t learn casual expressions, it makes it difficult to understand your friends (or would-be friends if you only knew how to speak less stiffly!). So here they are but take care to properly practice the longer forms so that you will be able to use them for the appropriate occasions.
Simply replace 「なくて」 with 「なくちゃ」
Simply replace 「なければ」 with 「なきゃ」
primary_text: 勉強[べんきょう] しなくちゃ[;n] 。
meaning: Gotta study.

example_sentences.ja: ご 飯[はん]を 食[た]べ なきゃ[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Gotta eat.

explanation: The 「と」 conditional is also used by itself to imply 「だめ／いけない／ならない」.
primary_text: 学校[がっこう]に 行[い]かない と[;n] 。
meaning: Gotta go to school.

explanation:
There is another 「ちゃ」 abbreviation for things that you must not do. However, in this case, you cannot leave out 「だめ／いけない／ならない」. Since this is a casual abbreviation, 「だめ」 is used in most cases.
One very important difference for this casual form is that verbs that end in 「む」、「ぶ」、「ぬ」 use 「じゃ」 instead of 「ちゃ」. Essentially, all the verbs that end in 「んだ」 for past tense fall in this category.
Replace 「ては」 with 「ちゃ」
Replace 「では」 with 「じゃ」
primary_text: ここに 入[はい]っ ちゃだめ[;n] だよ。
meaning: You can’t enter here.
item_key: grammar:ここに入っちゃだめだよ。

example_sentences.ja: 死[し]ん じゃだめ[;n] だよ！
example_sentences.en: You can’t die!

explanation:
Now let’s learn how to say either that it’s ok to do or not do something. I decided to shove this section in here because in Japanese, this is essential how to say that you don’t have to something (by saying it’s ok to not do it). The grammar itself is also relatively easy to pick up and makes for a short section.
By simply using the te-form and the 「も」 particle, you are essentially saying, “even if you do X…” Common words that come after this include 「いい」, 「大丈夫」, or 「構わない」. Some examples will come in handy.
primary_text: 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べ てもいい[;n] よ。
meaning: You can go ahead and eat it all. (lit: Even if you eat it all, it’s good, you know.)

example_sentences.ja: 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べなく てもいい[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en: You don’t have to eat it all. (lit: Even if you don’t eat it all, it’s good, you know.)

example_sentences.ja: 全部[ぜんぶ] 飲[の]ん でも[;n] 大丈夫[だいじょうぶ;n] だよ。
example_sentences.en: It’s ok if you drink it all. (lit: Even if you drink it all, it’s OK, you know.)

example_sentences.ja: 全部[ぜんぶ] 飲[の]ん でも[;n] 構[かま;n]わない よ。
example_sentences.en: I don’t mind if you drink it all. (lit: Even if you drink it all, I don’t mind, you know.)

explanation: In casual speech, 「～てもいい」 sometimes get shortened to just 「～ていい」 （or 「～でいい」 instead of 「～でもいい」 ）.
primary_text: もう 帰[かえ]っ ていい[;n] ？
meaning: Can I go home already?

example_sentences.ja: これ、ちょっと 見[み] ていい[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: Can I take a quick look at this?

explanation: You can express verbs that you want to perform with the 「たい」 form. All you need to do is add 「たい」 to the stem of the verb. However, unlike most conjugations we learned where the verb turns into a ru-verb, this form actually transforms the verb into an i-adjective (notice how 「たい」 conveniently ends in 「い」). This makes sense because the conjugated form is a description of something that you want to do. Once you have the 「たい」 form, you can then conjugate it the same as you would any other i-adjective. However, the 「たい」 form is different from regular i-adjectives because it is derived from a verb. Particles we normally associate with verbs such as 「を」、「に」、「へ」、or 「で」 can all be used with the 「たい」 form in addition to the particles commonly used with regular adjectives such as 「は」 and 「が」.
primary_text: 何[なに]を したい[;n] ですか。
meaning: What do you want to do?

example_sentences.ja: 温泉[おんせん] に 行[い]き たい[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I want to go to hot spring.

example_sentences.ja: ケーキ、 食[た]べ たくない[;n] の？
example_sentences.en: You don’t want to eat cake?

example_sentences.ja: 食[た]べ たくなかった[;n] けど 食[た]べ たく[;n] なった。
example_sentences.en: I didn’t want to eat it but I became wanting to eat.

example_sentences.ja: ずっと 一緒[いっしょ]に いたい[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I want to be together forever. (lit: Want to exist together for long time.)

example_sentences.ja: 犬[いぬ]と 遊[あそ]び たい[;n] ですか。
example_sentences.en: Do you want to play with dog?

explanation:
In English, we employ a verb to say that we want something. In Japanese, “to want” is actually an i-adjective and not a verb. We saw something similar with 「好き」 which is an adjective while “to like” in English is a verb. While I didn’t get too much into the workings of 「好き」, I have dedicated a whole section to 「欲しい」 because it means, “to want something done” when combined with the te-form of a verb. We will learn a more polite and appropriate way to make requests in the “Making Requests” lesson instead of saying, “I want this done.”
Though not a set rule, whenever words come attached to the te-form of a verb to serve a special grammatical function, it is customary to write it in hiragana. This is because kanji is already used for the verb and the attached word becomes part of that verb.
primary_text: 大[おお]きい 縫[ぬ]いぐるみが 欲[ほ;n]しい ！
meaning: I want a big stuffed doll!
item_key: grammar:大きい縫いぐるみが欲しい!

example_sentences.ja: 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べ てほしい[;n] んだけど・・・。
example_sentences.en: I want it all eaten but…

example_sentences.ja: 部屋[へや]をきれいに してほしい[;n] のよ。
example_sentences.en: It is that I want the room cleaned up, you know.

explanation:
The term volitional here means a will to do something. In other words, the volitional form indicates that someone is setting out to do something. In the most common example, this simply translates into the English “let’s” or “shall we?” but we’ll also see how this form can be used to express an effort to do something in a lesson further along.
To conjugate verbs into the volitional form for casual speech, there are two different rules for ru-verbs and u-verbs. For ru-verbs, you simply remove the 「る」 and add 「よう」. For u-verbs, you replace the / u / vowel sound with the / o / vowel sound and add 「う」.
primary_text: 今日[きょう]は 何[なに]を しよう[;n] か？
meaning: What shall (we) do today?

example_sentences.ja: テーマパークに 行[い;n]こう！
example_sentences.en: Let’s go to theme park!

example_sentences.ja: 明日[あした]は 何[なに]を 食[た;n]べよう か？
example_sentences.en: What shall (we) eat tomorrow?

example_sentences.ja: カレーを 食[た;n]べよう ！
example_sentences.en: Let’s eat curry!

explanation: The conjugation for the polite form is even simpler. All you have to do is add 「～ましょう」 to the stem of the verb. Similar to the masu-form, verbs in this form must always come at the end of the sentence. In fact, all polite endings must always come at the end and nowhere else as we’ve already seen.
primary_text: 今日[きょう]は 何[なに]を しましょう[;n] か？
meaning: What shall (we) do today?
item_key: grammar:今日は何をしましょうか?

example_sentences.ja: テーマパークに 行[い;n]きましょう ！
example_sentences.en: Let’s go to theme park!

example_sentences.ja: 明日[あした]は 何[なに]を 食[た;n]べましょう か？
example_sentences.en: What shall (we) eat tomorrow?

example_sentences.ja: カレーを 食[た;n]べましょう ！
example_sentences.en: Let’s eat curry!

explanation: You can make suggestions by using the 「ば」 or 「たら」 conditional and adding 「どう」. This literally means, “If you do [X], how is it?” In English, this would become, “How about doing [X]?” Grammatically, there’s nothing new here but it is a commonly used set phrase.
primary_text: 銀行[ぎんこう]に 行[い;n]ったらどう ですか。
meaning: How about going to bank?
item_key: grammar:銀行に行ったらどうですか。

example_sentences.ja: たまにご 両親[りょうしん]と 話[はな;n]せばどう ？
example_sentences.en: How about talking with your parents once in a while?

explanation: We’ll learn the simplest type of quoted phrase, which is the direct quote. Basically, you are directly quoting something that was said. This is done by simply enclosing the statement in quotes, adding 「と」 and then inserting the appropriate verb. The most common verbs associated with a direct quote would be 「言う」 and 「聞く」 but you may use any verbs related to direct quotation such as: 「叫ぶ」, 「呼ぶ」, 「呟く」, etc. This type of quotation is often used for dialogue in novels and other narrative works.
primary_text: アリスが、 「[;n] 寒[さむ;n]い」と 言[い]った。
meaning: Alice said, “Cold”.
item_key: grammar:アリスが、「寒い」と言った。

example_sentences.ja: 「[;n] 今日[きょう;n]は 授業[じゅぎょう;n]がない」と 先生[せんせい]から 聞[き]いたんだけど。
example_sentences.en: It is that I heard from the teacher, “There is no class today.”

example_sentences.ja: 「[;n] 寒[さむ;n]い」と アリスが 田中[たなか]に 言[い]った。
example_sentences.en: “Cold,” Alice said to Tanaka.

explanation: The second type of quote is the quote along the lines of what someone actually said. It’s not a word-for-word quote. Since this is not a direct quote, no quotations are needed. You can also express thoughts as an interpreted quote as well. By using this and the verb 「思う」 you can say you think that something is so-and-so. You will hear Japanese people use this all the time. You can also use the verb 「考える」 when you are considering something.
primary_text: 先生[せんせい]から 今日[きょう]は 授業[じゅぎょう]がない と[;n] 聞[き]いたんだけど。
meaning: I heard from the teacher that there is no class today.
item_key: grammar:先生から今日は授業がないと聞いたんだけど。

example_sentences.ja: これは、 日本語[にほんご]で 何[なん] と[;n] 言[い]いますか。
example_sentences.en: What do you call this in Japanese? (lit: About this, what do you say in Japanese?)

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]は、 アリス と[;n] 言[い]います。
example_sentences.en: I am called Alice. (lit: As for me, you say Alice.)

example_sentences.ja: カレーを 食[た]べよう と[;n] 思[おも]ったけど、 食[た]べる 時間[じかん]がなかった。
example_sentences.en: I thought about setting out to eat curry but I didn’t have time to eat.

example_sentences.ja: 今[いま]、どこに 行[い]こうか と[;n] 考[かんが]えている。
example_sentences.en: Now, I’m considering where to set out to go.

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]は、これは 何[なん]だ と[;n] 言[い]いましたか。
example_sentences.en: What did he say this is?

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]は 高校生[こうこうせい]だ と[;n] 聞[き]いたけど、 信[しん]じられない。
example_sentences.en: I heard that he is a high school student but I can’t believe it.

example_sentences.ja: これは 何[なん]だ と[;n] 言[い]いましたか。
example_sentences.en: What did (he) say this is?

example_sentences.ja: 何[なん] と[;n] 言[い]いましたか。
example_sentences.en: What did (he) say?

explanation:
You may be surprised to hear that there is a shorter and casual version of the quoted relative clause since it’s already only one hiragana character, 「と」. However, the important point here is that by using this casual shortcut, you can drop the rest of the sentence and hope your audience can understand everything from context.
「って」 can also be used to talk about practically anything, not just to quote something that was said. You can hear 「って」 being used just about everywhere in casual speech. Most of the time it is used in place of the 「は」 particle to simply bring up a topic.
primary_text: 智子[ともこ]は 来年[らいねん]、 海外[かいがい]に 行[い]くんだ って[;n] 。
meaning: Tomoko said that she’s going overseas next year.
item_key: grammar:智子は来年、海外に行くんだって。

example_sentences.ja: もうお 金[かね]がない って[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I already told you I have no money.

example_sentences.ja: え？ 何[なん]だ って[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: Huh? What did you say?

example_sentences.ja: 今[いま]、 時間[じかん]がない って[;n] 聞[き]いたんだけど、 本当[ほんとう]？
example_sentences.en: I heard you don’t have time now, is that true?

example_sentences.ja: 今[いま]、 時間[じかん]がない って[;n] 、 本当[ほんとう]？
example_sentences.en: You don’t have time now (I heard), is that true?

example_sentences.ja: 明日[あした] って[;n] 、 雨[あめ]が 降[ふ]るんだ って[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: About tomorrow, I hear that it’s going to rain.

example_sentences.ja: アリス って[;n] 、すごくいい 人[ひと]でしょ？
example_sentences.en: About Alice, she’s a very good person, right?

explanation: In the last lesson, we briefly looked at how to introduce ourselves by using 「と」 and 「いう」. For instance, we had the following example, which Alice used to introduce herself.
primary_text: 私[わたし]はアリス といいます[;n] 。
meaning: I am called Alice. (lit: As for me, you say Alice.)
item_key: grammar:私はアリスといいます。

example_sentences.ja: これは、なん という[;n] 魚[さかな]ですか。
example_sentences.en: What is this fish referred to as?

example_sentences.ja: この 魚[さかな]は、 鯛[たい] といいます[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: This fish is known as “Tai“.

example_sentences.ja: ルミネ という[;n] デパートはどこにあるか、 知[し]っていますか？
example_sentences.en: Do you know where the department store called “Lumine” is?

example_sentences.ja: 「 友達[ともだち]」は、 英語[えいご]で「 friend[フレンド] 」 という[;n] 意味[いみ]です。
example_sentences.en: The meaning of “tomodachi” in English is “friend”.

explanation: We learned how to use 「という」 to describe what something is known or referred to as. However, we can take this idea even further by attaching two relative clauses. At this point, 「いう」 is so abstract that it doesn’t even really have a meaning. When a relative clause is encapsulated with 「と」, you must have a verb to go along with it and 「いう」 is simply being used as a generic verb to enable us to talk about any relative clause. This allows us to describe and explain just about anything ranging from a single word to complete sentences. As you can imagine, this construction is quite useful and employed quite often in Japanese.
primary_text: 主人公[しゅじんこう]が 犯人[はんにん]だった という[;n] のが 一番[いちばん] 面白[おもしろ]かった。
meaning: The most interesting thing was that the main character was the criminal.
item_key: grammar:主人公が犯人だったというのが一番面白かった。

example_sentences.ja: 日本人[にほんじん]はお 酒[さけ]に 弱[よわ]い という[;n] のは 本当[ほんとう]？
example_sentences.en: Is it true that Japanese people are weak to alcohol?

example_sentences.ja: 独身[どくしん]だ という[;n] のは、 嘘[うそ]だったの？
example_sentences.en: It was a lie that you were single?

example_sentences.ja: リブート という[;n] のは、パソコンを 再起動[さいきどう]する という[;n] ことです。
example_sentences.en: Reboot means to restart your computer.

example_sentences.ja: あんたは、いつも こういう[;n] 時[とき]に 来[く]るんだから、 困[こま]るんだよ。
example_sentences.en: It’s because you always come at times like these that I’m troubled.

example_sentences.ja: そういう[;n] 人[ひと]と 一緒[いっしょ]に 仕事[しごと]をするのは、 嫌[いや]だよね。
example_sentences.en: (Anybody would) dislike doing work together with that type of person, huh?

example_sentences.ja: ああいう[;n] 人[ひと]と 結婚[けっこん]できたら、 幸[しあわ]せになれると 思[おも]います。
example_sentences.en: I think you can become happy if you could marry that type of person.

example_sentences.ja: 大学[だいがく]に 行[い]かないって、 どういう[;n] 意味[いみ]なの？
example_sentences.en: What do you mean, “You’re not going to go to college?”

explanation: We can attach the question marker 「か」 to 「という」 in order to add a questioning element. This construction is used when you want to rephrase or redefine something such as the following dialogue.
primary_text:
Ａ「みきちゃんは、あんたの 彼女[かのじょ] でしょう？」
Ｂ「う～ん、 彼女[かのじょ] というか[;n] 、 友達[ともだち] というか[;n] 、なん というか[;n] ・・・。」
meaning:
A: Miki-chan is your girlfriend, right?
B:Um, you might say girlfriend, or friend, or something…
item_key: grammar:a「みきちゃんは、あんたの彼女でしょう?」b「う~ん、彼女というか、友達というか、なんというか・・・。」

example_sentences.ja: お 酒[さけ]は 好[す]き というか[;n] 、ないと 生[い]きていけない。
example_sentences.en: I like alcohol or rather, can’t live on without it.

example_sentences.ja: 多分[たぶん] 行[い]かないと 思[おも]う。 というか[;n] 、お 金[かね]がないから、 行[い]けない。
example_sentences.en: Don’t think I’ll go. Or rather, can’t because there’s no money.

example_sentences.ja: というか[;n] 、もう 帰[かえ]らないとだめですけど。
example_sentences.en: Rather than that, I have to go home already.

explanation: Rather than using 「か」 to rephrase a conclusion, we can also simply use 「こと」 to sum up something without rephrasing anything.
primary_text:
Ａ「みきちゃんが 洋介[ようすけ]と 別[わか]れたんだって。」
Ｂ「 ということ[;n] は、みきちゃんは、 今[いま] 彼氏[かれし]がいない ということ[;n] ？」
Ａ「そう。 そういうこと[;n] 。」
meaning:
A: I heard that Miki-chan broke up with Yousuke.
B: Does that mean Miki-chan doesn’t have a boyfriend now?
A: That’s right. That’s what it means.
item_key: grammar:a「みきちゃんが洋介と別れたんだって。」b「ということは、みきちゃんは、今彼氏がいないということ?」a「そう。そういうこと。」

explanation:
As mentioned in the previous lesson, 「って」 is very often used in causal slang in place of 「と」, because it allows us to leave out the rest of the sentence and assume context (or just plain assumption) will take care of the rest. We already saw that we can use 「って」 to replace 「という」 as well. However, since we just learned how to use 「という」 to do much more than just simply say something, there is a limit to just how much you can leave out. In any case, 「って」 will allow us to leave out not only 「いう」 but also any accompanying particles.
「だって」 is also another phrase that leaves out just about everything. By convention, it is used to express disagreement or dissatisfaction usually to whine, complain, or to make an excuse but you can’t tell what it means just from looking at it. It is an abbreviation of something along the lines of 「とはいっても」 meaning, “even if that was the case”.
In some cases, the small 「つ」 is left out and just 「て」 is used instead of 「って」. This is done (as is usually the case for slang) in order to make things easier to say. In general, this is when there is nothing before the 「て」 or when the sound that comes before it doesn’t require the explicit separation the 「っ」 gives us in order to be understood.
Since slang tends to be used in whichever way the person feels like, there are no definite rules defining whether you should use 「って」 or 「て」. However, 「て」 is generally not used to express what people have actually said or heard, which is why it wasn’t covered in the last lesson.
primary_text: 来年[らいねん] 留学[りゅうがく]する って[;n] 智子[ともこ]のこと？
meaning: The studying abroad next year thing, is that Tomoko?
item_key: grammar:来年留学するって智子のこと?

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「しないとだめだよ。」
Ｂ「 だって[;n] 、 時間[じかん]がないからできないよ。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Have to do it, you know.
B: But (even so), can’t do it because there is no time.

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 行[い]かなくてもいいよ。」
Ｂ「 だって[;n] 、みんな 行[い]くって。 私[わたし]も 行[い]かないと。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Don’t have to go, you know.
B: But (even so), everybody said they’re going. I have to go too.

example_sentences.ja: て[;n] ことは、みきちゃんは、 今[いま] 彼氏[かれし] がいない て[;n] こと？
example_sentences.en: Does that mean Miki-chan doesn’t have a boyfriend now?

example_sentences.ja: て[;n] いうか、もう 帰[かえ]らないとだめですけど。
example_sentences.en: Rather than that, I have to go home already.

explanation:
Because the 「という」construction is used so often, there are a lot of different variations and slang based on it. While I do not plan on covering all of them here, you can check out casual patterns and slang in the miscellaneous section for yet even more slang derived from 「という」.
The last thing I’m am going to briefly mention here is the use of 「ゆう」 instead of 「いう」. In conversations, it is quite normal to say 「ゆう」 instead of 「いう」. 「ゆう」 is easier to say because it is simply one letter with a long vowel sound instead of the two different vowel sounds of 「いう」.
primary_text: て ゆう[;n] か、もう 帰[かえ]らないとだめですけど。
meaning: Rather than that, I have to go home already.
item_key: grammar:てゆうか、もう帰らないとだめですけど。

example_sentences.ja: そう ゆう[;n] ことじゃないって！
example_sentences.en: I said it’s not like that (lit: it’s not that type of thing)!

explanation: To try something out, you simply need to change the verb to the te-form and add 「みる」. If it helps you to remember, you can think of it as a sequence of an action and then seeing the result. In fact 「みる」 conjugates just like 「見る」. However, just like the 「～てほしい」 grammar we learned, this is a set phrase and 「みる」 is usually written in hiragana.
primary_text: お 好[この]み 焼[や]きを 初[はじ]めて 食[た]べ てみた[;n] けど、とてもおいしかった！
meaning: I tried eating okonomiyaki for the first time and it was very tasty!
item_key: grammar:お好み焼きを初めて食べてみたけど、とてもおいしかった!

example_sentences.ja: お 酒[さけ]を 飲[の]ん でみました[;n] が、すごく 眠[ねむ]くなりました。
example_sentences.en: I tried drinking alcohol and I became extremely sleepy.

example_sentences.ja: 新[あたら]しいデパートに 行[い]っ てみる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I’m going to check out the new department store.

example_sentences.ja: 広島[ひろしま]のお 好[この]み 焼[や]きを 食[た]べ てみたい[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: I want to try eating Hiroshima okonomiyaki!

explanation:
We already learned that the volitional form was used to indicate a will to set out to do something. If you guessed that this next grammar for attempting to do something would involve the volitional form, you were right. To say that you tried (as in attempted) to do something, you need to conjugate the verb into the volitional, enclose it in a quotation (so that we can perform an action on the clause) and finally add the verb 「する」. Or put more simply, you just add 「とする」 to the volitional form of the verb. This is simply an extension of the quoted relative clause from the last section. Instead of saying the quote （言う） or treating it as a thought （思う、考える）, we are simply doing it with 「する」.

Though we use the verb 「する」 to say, “to do attempt”, we can use different verbs to do other things with the attempt. For instance, we can use the verb 「決める」 to say, “decide to attempt to do [X]”. Here are some examples of other actions carried out on the attempt.
primary_text: 毎日[まいにち]、 勉強[べんきょう]を 避[さ]け ようとする[;n] 。
meaning: Every day, she attempts to avoid study.
item_key: grammar:毎日、勉強を避けようとする。

example_sentences.ja: 無理矢理[むりやり]に 部屋[へや]に 入[はい] ろうとしている[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: He is attempting to force his way into the room.

example_sentences.ja: 早[はや]く 寝[ね] ようとした[;n] けど、 結局[けっきょく]は 徹夜[てつや]した。
example_sentences.en: I attempted to sleep early but ended up staying up all night.

example_sentences.ja: お 酒[さけ]を 飲[の] もうとした[;n] が、 奥[おく]さんが 止[と]めた。
example_sentences.en: He tried to drink alcohol but his wife stopped him.

example_sentences.ja: 勉強[べんきょう]をなるべく 避[さ]け ようと[;n] 思[おも;n]った 。
example_sentences.en: I thought I would attempt to avoid studying as much as possible.

example_sentences.ja: 毎日[まいにち]ジムに 行[い] こうと[;n] 決[き;n]めた。
example_sentences.en: Decided to attempt to go to gym every day.

explanation:
「あげる」 is the Japanese word for “to give” seen from the speaker’s point of view. You must use this verb when you are giving something or doing something for someone else.

In order to express the giving of a favor (verb) you must use the ever useful te-form and then attach 「あげる」. This applies to all the other sections in this lesson as well.

For third-person, this verb is used when the speaker is looking at it from the giver’s point of view. We’ll see the significance of this when we examine the verb 「くれる」 next.
primary_text: 私[わたし]が 友達[ともだち]にプレゼントを あげた[;n] 。
meaning: I gave present to friend.
item_key: grammar:私が友達にプレゼントをあげた。

example_sentences.ja: これは 先生[せんせい]に あげる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I’ll give this to teacher.

example_sentences.ja: 車[くるま]を 買[か]って あげる[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en: I’ll give you the favor of buying a car.

example_sentences.ja: 代[か]わりに 行[に]って あげる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I’ll give you the favor of going in your place.

example_sentences.ja: 学生[がくせい]がこれを 先生[せんせい]に あげる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: The student give this to teacher. (looking at it from the student’s point of view)

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち]が 父[ちち]にいいことを 教[おし]えて あげた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Friend gave the favor of teaching something good to my dad. (looking at it from the friend’s point of view)

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい]が 教[おし]えて あげる[;n] んですか。
example_sentences.en: Teacher, will you be the one to give favor of teaching to… [anybody other than the speaker]?

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]が 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べて あげました[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I gave favor of eating it all.

explanation:
Using 「やる」 to mean 「あげる」

Usually used for pets, animals, and such, you can substitute 「やる」, which normally means “to do”, for 「あげる」. You shouldn’t use this type of 「やる」 for people because it is used when looking down on someone and can be offensive.
primary_text: 犬[いぬ]に 餌[えさ]を やった[;n] ？
meaning: Did you give the dog food?

explanation:
「くれる」 is also a verb meaning “to give” but unlike 「あげる」, it is from the receiver’s point of view. You must use this verb when someone else is giving something or doing something for you (effectively the opposite of 「あげる」).

Similarly, when used in the third-person, the speaker is speaking from the receiver’s point of view and not the giver.
primary_text: 友達[ともだち]が 私[わたし]にプレゼントを くれた[;n] 。
meaning: Friend gave present to me.
item_key: grammar:友達が私にプレゼントをくれた。

example_sentences.ja: これは、 先生[せんせい]が くれた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Teacher gave this to me.

example_sentences.ja: 車[くるま]を 買[か]って くれる[;n] の？
example_sentences.en: You’ll give me the favor of buying a car for me?

example_sentences.ja: 代[か]わりに 行[い]って くれる[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: Will you give me the favor of going in my place?

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい]がこれを 学生[がくせい]に くれる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: The teacher give this to student. (looking at it from the student’s point of view)

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち]が 父[ちち]にいいことを 教[おし]えて くれた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Friend gave favor of teaching something good to my dad. (looking at it from the dad’s point of view)

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい]が 教[おし]えて くれる[;n] んですか。
example_sentences.en: Teacher, will you be the one to give favor of teaching to… [anybody including the speaker]?

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち]がプレゼントを 私[わたし]に くれた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Friend gave present to me.

example_sentences.ja: 千円[せんえん]を 貸[か]して くれる[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: Will you give me the favor of lending 1000 yen?

explanation:
「もらう」 meaning, “to receive” has only one version unlike 「あげる／くれる」 so there’s very little to explain. One thing to point out is that since you receive from someone, 「から」 is also appropriate in addition to the 「に」 target particle.

「もらう」 is seen from the perspective of the receiver, so in the case of first-person, others usually don’t receive things from you. However, you might want to use 「私からもらう」 when you want to emphasize that fact that the other person received it from you. For instance, if you wanted to say, “Hey, I gave you that!” you would use 「あげる」. However, you would use 「もらう」 if you wanted to say, “Hey, you got that from me!”
primary_text: 私[わたし]が 友達[ともだち]にプレゼントを もらった[;n] 。
meaning: I received present from friend.
item_key: grammar:私が友達にプレゼントをもらった。

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち]からプレゼントを もらった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I received present from friend.

example_sentences.ja: これは 友達[ともだち]に 買[か]って もらった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: About this, received the favor of buying it from friend.

example_sentences.ja: 宿題[しゅくだい]をチェックして もらいたかった[;n] けど、 時間[じかん]がなくて 無理[むり]だった。
example_sentences.en: I wanted to receive the favor of checking homework but there was no time and it was impossible.

example_sentences.ja: その 時計[とけい]は 私[わたし]から もらった[;n] のよ。
example_sentences.en: (He) received that watch from me.

example_sentences.ja: 千円[せんえん]を 貸[か]して もらえる[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: Can I receive the favor of you lending 1000 yen?

explanation: You can use the negative to make the request a little softer. You’ll see that this is true in many other types of grammar.
primary_text: ちょっと 静[しず]かにして くれない[;n] ？
meaning: Won’t you be a little quieter?
item_key: grammar:ちょっと静かにしてくれない?

example_sentences.ja: 漢字[かんじ]で 書[か]いて もらえません[;n] か。
example_sentences.en: Can you write this in kanji for me?

explanation: In order to request that someone not do something, you simply attach 「で」 to the negative form of the verb and proceed as before.
primary_text: 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べ ないで[;n] くれますか。
meaning: Can you not eat it all?
item_key: grammar:全部食べないでくれますか。

example_sentences.ja: 高[たか]い 物[もの]を 買[か]わ ないで[;n] くれる？
example_sentences.en: Can you not buy expensive thing(s)?

explanation:
「ください」 is a special conjugation of 「くださる」, which is the honorific form of 「くれる」. We will learn more about honorific and humble forms in the beginning of the next major section. We are going over 「ください」 here because it has a slight difference in meaning from the normal 「くれる」 and the honorific 「くださる」. 
「ください」 is a direct request for something while 「くれる」 is used as a question asking for someone to give something. However, it is similar to 「くれる」 in that you can make a request for an action by simply attaching it to the te-form of the verb.
The rules for negative requests are same as the rules for 「くれる」 as well.
meaning: Please give me that.

example_sentences.ja: 漢字[かんじ]で 書[か]いて ください[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Please write it in kanji.

example_sentences.ja: ゆっくり 話[はな]して ください[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Please speak slowly.

example_sentences.ja: 落書[らくが]きを 書[か]かないで ください[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Please don’t write graffiti.

example_sentences.ja: ここにこないで ください[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Please don’t come here.

explanation:
In casual speech, it is often common to simply drop the 「ください」 part.
For those who want to sound particularly commanding and manly, it is also possible to use 「くれる」 with the 「る」 removed.
primary_text: 日本語[にほんご]で 話[はな;n]して 。
meaning: Please speak in Japanese.

example_sentences.ja: 消[け]しゴムを 貸[か;n]して 。
example_sentences.en: Please lend me the eraser.

example_sentences.ja: 遠[とお]い 所[ところ]に 行[い;n]かないで 。
example_sentences.en: Please don’t go to a far place.

example_sentences.ja: 日本語[にほんご]で 話[はな;n]してくれ 。
example_sentences.en: Speak in Japanese.

example_sentences.ja: 消[け]しゴムを 貸[か;n]してくれ 。
example_sentences.en: Lend me the eraser.

example_sentences.ja: 遠[とお]い 所[ところ]に 行[い;n]かないでくれ 。
example_sentences.en: Don’t go to a far place.

explanation: A casual alternative of 「ください」 is 「ちょうだい」. While it can be used by anyone, it has a slightly feminine and childish nuance and is always written in Hiragana. Written in Kanji, it is usually used in a very formal expression such as 「頂戴致します」. Grammatically, it’s used exactly the same way as 「ください」.
primary_text: スプーンを ちょうだい[;n] 。
meaning: Please give me the spoon.
item_key: grammar:スプーンをちょうだい。

example_sentences.ja: ここに 名前[なまえ] を 書[か]いて ちょうだい[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Please write your name here.

explanation: 「なさい」 is a special honorific conjugation of 「する」. It is a soft yet firm way of issuing a command. It is used, for example, when a mother is scolding her child or when a teacher wants a delinquent student to pay attention. Unlike 「ください」, 「なさい」 only applies to positive verbs and uses the stem of the verb instead of the te-form. It also cannot be used by itself but must be attached to another verb.
primary_text: よく 聞[き]き なさい[;n] ！
meaning: Listen well!

example_sentences.ja: ここに 座[すわ]り なさい[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Sit here.

explanation: You can also drop 「さい」 portion of the 「なさい」 to make a casual version of this grammar.
primary_text: まだいっぱいあるから、たくさん 食[た]べ な[;n] 。
meaning: There’s still a lot, so eat a lot.
item_key: grammar:まだいっぱいあるから、たくさん食べな。

example_sentences.ja: それでいいと 思[おも]うなら、そう しな[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en: If you think that’s fine, then go ahead and do it.

explanation: We will go over the command form in the interest of covering all the possible verb conjugations. In reality, the command form is rarely used as Japanese people tend to be too polite to use imperatives. Also, this coarse type of speech is rarely, if indeed at all, used by females who tend to use 「なさい」 or an exasperated 「くれる」 when angry or irritated. This form is only really useful for reading or watching fictional works. You may often see or hear 「死ね！」 (“Die!”) in fiction which, of course, you’ll never hear in real life. (I hope!)
meaning: Do as you please.

example_sentences.ja: あっち 行[い;n]け！
example_sentences.en: Go away!

explanation: The negative command form is very simple: simply attach 「な」 to either ru-verbs or u-verbs. Don’t confuse this with the 「な」 sentence-ending particle we will be learning at the end of this section. The intonation is totally different.
primary_text: それを 食[た]べる な[;n] ！
meaning: Don’t eat that!

example_sentences.ja: 変[へん]なことを 言[い]う な[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: Don’t say such weird things!

explanation:
While written Japanese already has fairly loose rules regarding sentence order, casual spoken Japanese takes it one step further. A complete sentence requires a verb at the end of the sentence to complete the thought. However, we’ll see how this rule is bent in casual conversations.

Conversations are sporadic and chaotic in any language and it’s common for people to say the first thing that pops into their head without thinking out the whole proper sentence.

For example, if you wanted to ask what something was, the normal, proper way would be to ask, 「それは何？」 However, if the first thing that popped into your head, “What the?” then it would be more natural to say 「何」 first. However, since 「何はそれ？」 doesn’t make any sense (Is what that?), you can simply break it up into what are essentially two sentence fragments asking “what” first （何？）, and then following it up with the explanation of what you were talking about （「それ」 in this case）. For the sake of convenience, this is lumped into what looks like one sentence.
meaning: What? That. (Two sentences lumped into one)

example_sentences.ja: 見[み]た？ あの 人[ひと]？
example_sentences.en: Did you see? That guy?

example_sentences.ja: もう 食[た]べた？ 昨日[きのう] 買[か]ったアイス。
example_sentences.en: You ate it already? The ice cream I bought yesterday.

explanation:
「じゃん」 is an abbreviation of 「じゃない」, the negative conjugation for nouns and na-adjectives. 

「じゃん」 is a shorter slang for expressing the same type of thing except it doesn’t even bother to ask a question to confirm. It’s completely affirmative in tone.

You can also attach 「じゃん」 directly to verbs and i-adjectives as well as the usual nouns and na-adjectives. Because slang is usually created to make things easier, it’s not surprising that the rules for using 「じゃん」 are so lax and easy.

There’s also another variation, which attaches the question marker as well. The meaning is mostly the same but it adds more to the questioning, confirming tone.
primary_text: ほら、やっぱりレポートを 書[か]かないとだめ じゃん[;n] 。
meaning: See, as I thought, you have to write the report.
item_key: grammar:ほら、やっぱりレポートを書かないとだめじゃん。

example_sentences.ja: 誰[だれ]もいないからここで 着替[きか]えてもいい じゃん[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Since there’s nobody, it’s probably fine to change here.

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「たかし 君[くん]は、ここにいる？」

Ｂ「 知[し]らない。」

Ａ「あっ！やっぱ、いる じゃん[;n] ！」
example_sentences.en:
A: Is Takashi here?
B: Dunno.

A: Ah! See, he is here!

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 駅[えき]の 近[ちか]くにカラオケがある じゃん[;n] か。」
Ｂ「うん。」

Ａ「あそこのすぐ 隣[となり]だ。」
example_sentences.en:
A: There’s a karaoke place near the station, right?

B: Yeah.
A: It’s right next to there.

explanation:
As we learned in the defining and describing section, 「いう」 serves many more functions than the equivalent English verb, “to say”. It is used all the time and therefore, it’s not too surprising that a number of variations and slang have developed. Here’s one more that I felt was too “slangy” to cover so early at that point of the guide.

This may sound hard to believe but if you really slur 「という」 together, it becomes something resembling 「つ」. Or least, that’s what somebody thought when he or she began replacing 「という」 with 「つ」 or in some case 「つう」.

Now, in my opinion, 「つ」 is a lot harder to say than 「という」 so using it like a native might take a bit of practice. Rather than making things easier to say, as is usually the case, the real purpose of this substitution is to sound rougher because 「つ」 has a harder, hissing sound. This is ideal for when you’re pissed or for that young and rough image you’ve always wanted. As you might expect, this type of speech is usually used by males or very tough females.

If you want even more emphasis, you can even add a small 「つ」. This usually means you are really at the brink of your patience.
primary_text: つう[;n] か、なんでお 前[まえ]がここにいんのよ！
meaning: Or rather, why are you here?!
item_key: grammar:つうか、なんでお前がここにいんのよ!

example_sentences.ja: 宿題[しゅくだい]で 時間[じかん]がない つって[;n] んのに、みきちゃんとデートしにいったと 聞[き]いたよ。
example_sentences.en: Although he’s saying he doesn’t have time due to homework, I heard he went on a date with Miki-chan.

example_sentences.ja: 明日[あした]は 試験[しけん]だぞ。 つって[;n] も、 勉強[べんきょう]はしてないだろうな。
example_sentences.en: Yo, tomorrow’s the test. Even if I say that, you probably didn’t study anyway, huh?

example_sentences.ja: だから、 違[ちが]うんだ つう[;n] の！
example_sentences.en: Like I said, you’re wrong!

example_sentences.ja: だから、 違[ちが]うんだ っつう[;n] の！
example_sentences.en: Like I said, you’re wrong!

explanation: 「ってば」 and 「ったら」 is yet another type of abbreviation for 「という」 similar to 「って」 as discussed in the defining and describing section. In this case, it’s an abbreviation of the conditional form of 「という」, which is 「といえば」 and 「といったら」. By using this abbreviation, you are essentially saying something along the lines of, “If I told you once, I told you a million times!” You can use this expression when you tired of repeating yourself or when you are exasperated with somebody for not listening to you.
primary_text: もう 行[い]く ってば[;n] ！
meaning: I told you I’m going already!

example_sentences.ja: あなた ったら[;n] 、いつも 忘[わす]れるんだから。
example_sentences.en: You’re always forgetting.

explanation:
By now, you’re probably aware that 「何」 can be either read as 「なに」 or 「なん」 depending on what comes after it such as 「何色」（なにいろ） versus 「何人」（なんにん）. In the case of 「何か」, while 「なにか」 is the correct reading, it is often contracted to just 「なんか」 in casual speech.

However, 「なんか」 also has a function similar to the word “like” in English. By “like”, I’m not talking about the actual word but the kind that has no meaning and some people use just about anywhere in the sentence. Similarly, 「なんか」 can also be used as a filler without any actual meaning.
meaning: Eat something?

example_sentences.ja: 今日[きょう]は、 なんか[;n] 忙[いそが]しいみたいよ。
example_sentences.en: I guess he’s like busy today.

example_sentences.ja: なんか[;n] ね。お 風呂[ふろ]って 超[ちょう] 気持[きも]ちいいよね！
example_sentences.en: Like, baths feel really good, huh?

example_sentences.ja: お 母[かあ]さんが、 なんか[;n] 明日[あした]まで 戻[もど]らないんだってよ。
example_sentences.en: Mom said she’s not coming back until like tomorrow.

example_sentences.ja: なんか[;n] さ。ボブは、 私[わたし]のこと なんか[;n] 本当[ほんとう]に 好[す]きかな？
example_sentences.en: Hey like, do you really think that Bob likes somebody like me?

explanation:
「やがる」 is a verb suffix used to indicate hatred or contempt for the person doing the action. Unlike the rest of the slang covered here, this extremely strong language is not used in normal, everyday conversations. You will probably never hear this expression outside of movies, comic books, games, and the like. However, it is covered here so that you can understand when it is used in those mediums.

In order to use 「やがる」, you simply attach it to the stem of the verb. After that, 「やがる」 is conjugated just like a regular u-verb.
primary_text: あんなやつに 負[ま]け やがって[;n] 。じゃ、どうすんだよ？
meaning: Losing to a guy like that. Well, what are you going to do?
item_key: grammar:あんなやつに負けやがって。じゃ、どうすんだよ?

example_sentences.ja: やる 気[き]か？だったらさっさと 来[き] やがれ[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: You want to fight? If so, then hurry up and come on!

explanation:
After the 「よ」 and 「ね」, 「さ」 and 「な」 are the next most commonly used sentence-ending particles.

「さ」, which is basically a very casual form of 「よ」, is similar to the English “like” in that some people throw it in at the end of almost every single phrase. Of course, that doesn’t mean it’s necessarily a very sophisticated manner of speech but just like using “like” all the time, I cannot deny that it is an easy habit to fall into. In that sense, due to its over-use, it has almost lost any specific meaning.
meaning: A: Hey...

example_sentences.ja: Ａ「この 間[あいだ] さ[;n] ・・・。」
example_sentences.en: A: This one time…

example_sentences.ja: Ａ「ディズニーランドに 行[い]ったんだけど さ[;n] 、なんか さ[;n] 、すごい 込[こ]んでて・・・。」
example_sentences.en: A: I went to Disney Land and it was really crowded…

example_sentences.ja: Ａ「 何[なに]もできなくて さ[;n] ・・・。」
example_sentences.en: A: Couldn’t do anything, you know…

explanation:
You can use 「な」 in place of 「ね」 when it sounds too soft and reserved for what you want to say or for the audience you are speaking to. Its rough sound generally applies to the male gender but is not necessarily restricted to only males.
The 「な」 sentence-ending particle is often used with the question marker 「か」 to indicate that the speaker is considering something.
primary_text:
洋介[ようすけ]「 今[いま]、 図書館[としょかん]に 行[い]くんだよ な[;n] 。」
智子[ともこ]「うん、なんで？」
meaning:
Yousuke: You are going to the library now huh? (seeking explanation)

Tomoko: Yeah, why?
item_key: grammar:洋介「今、図書館に行くんだよな。」智子「うん、なんで?」

example_sentences.ja:
ボブ「 日本語[にほんご]は、たくさん 勉強[べんきょう]したけど な[;n] 。まだ 全然[ぜんぜん]わからない。
アリス「 大丈夫[だいじょうぶ]よ。きっとわかるようになるからさ。」
ボブ「ならいいけど な[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
Bob: I studied Japanese a lot, right? But, I still don’t get it at all.
Alice: No problem. You’ll become able to understand for sure, you know?
Bob: If so, it would be good.

example_sentences.ja: 今日[きょう]は 雨[あめ]が 降[ふ]るか な[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: I wonder if it’ll rain today.

example_sentences.ja: いい 大学[だいがく]に 行[い]けるか な[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: I wonder if I can go to a good college.

explanation: 「かい」 and 「だい」 are strongly masculine sentence endings for asking questions. 「かい」 is used for yes/no questions while 「だい」 is used for open-ended questions.
primary_text: おい、どこに 行[い]くん だい[;n] ？
meaning: Hey, where are (you) going?
item_key: grammar:おい、どこに行くんだい?

example_sentences.ja: さきちゃんって 呼[よ]んでもいい かい[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: Can (I) call you Saki-chan?

example_sentences.ja: 一体[いったい] 何時[なんじ]に 帰[かえ]ってくるつもりだったん だい[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: What time were (you) planning on coming home exactly?

example_sentences.ja: 俺[おれ]は 土曜日[どようび]、 映画[えいが]を 見[み]に 行[い]くけど、 一緒[いっしょ]に 行[い]く かい[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: I’m going to see a movie Saturday, go together?

explanation: These sentence-ending particles are primarily used just to emphasize something and doesn’t really have a meaning per se. However, they can make your statements sound much stronger and/or very gender-specific. Using 「わ」 is just like 「よ」 except it will make you sound very feminine (this is a different sound from the 「わ」 used in Kansai dialect). 「かしら」 is also a very feminine version of 「かな」, which we just went over. 「ぞ」 and 「ぜ」 are identical to 「よ」 except that it makes you sound “cool” and manly, or at least, that is the intent. These examples may not be very helpful without actually hearing what they sound like.
primary_text: もう 時間[じかん]がない わ[;n] 。
meaning: There is no more time.

example_sentences.ja: おい、 行[い]く ぞ[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: Hey, we’re going!

example_sentences.ja: これで、もう 終[お]わりだ ぜ[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: With this, it’s over already.

example_sentences.ja: いい 大学[だいがく]に 入[はい]れる かしら[;n] ？
example_sentences.en: I wonder if I can enter a good college.

explanation: Verbs conjugated into the causative form are used to indicate an action that someone makes happen. Like Captain Picard so succinctly puts it, the causative verb means to “make it so”. This verb is usually used in the context of making somebody do something. The really confusing thing about the causative verb is that it can also mean to let someone do something. Or maybe this is a different type of verb with the exact same conjugation rules. Whichever the case may be, a verb in the causative form can mean either making or letting someone do something. The only good news is that when the causative form is used with 「あげる」 and 「くれる」, it almost always means to “let someone do”. Once you get used to it, surprisingly, it becomes quite clear which meaning is being used when.
primary_text: 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べ させた[;n] 。
meaning:
Made/Let
 (someone) eat it all.

example_sentences.ja: 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べ させてくれた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en:
Let
 (someone) eat it all.

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい]が 学生[がくせい]に 宿題[しゅくだい]をたくさん させた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en:
Teacher 
made
 students 
do
 lots of homework.

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい]が 質問[しつもん]をたくさん 聞[き] かせてくれた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en:
Teacher 
let
 (someone) ask lots of questions.

example_sentences.ja: 今日[きょう]は 仕事[しごと]を 休[やす] ませてください[;n] 。
example_sentences.en:
Please let
 me rest from work today. (
Please let
 me take the day off today.)

example_sentences.ja: その 部長[ぶちょう]は、よく 長時間[ちょうじかん] 働[はたら] かせる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en:
That manager often 
makes
 (people) work long hours.

explanation:
There is a shorter version of the causative conjugation, which I will go over for completeness. However, since this version is mostly used in very rough slang, you are free to skip this section until you’ve had time to get used to the regular form. Also, textbooks usually don’t cover this version of the causative verb.

The key difference in this version is that all verbs become an u-verbs with a 「す」 ending. Therefore, the resulting verb would conjugate just like any other u-verb ending in 「す」 such as 「話す」 or 「指す」. The first part of the conjugation is the same as the original causative form. However, for ru-verbs, instead of attaching 「させる」, you attach 「さす」 and for u-verbs, you attach 「す」 instead of 「せる」. As a result, all the verbs become an u-verb ending in 「す」.
primary_text: 同[おな]じことを 何回[なんかい]も 言[い] わすな[;n] ！
meaning:
Don’t make
 me say the same thing again and again!
item_key: grammar:同じことを何回も言わすな!

example_sentences.ja: お 腹[なか] 空[あ]いているんだから、なんか 食[た]べ さしてくれ[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en:
I’m hungry so 
let
 me eat something.

explanation: Passive verbs are verbs that are done to the (passive) subject. Unlike English style of writing which discourages the use of the passive form, passive verbs in Japanese are often used in essays and articles.
primary_text: ポリッジが 誰[だれ]かに 食[た]べ られた[;n] ！
meaning: The porridge was eaten by somebody!
item_key: grammar:ポリッジが誰かに食べられた!

example_sentences.ja: みんなに 変[へん]だと 言[い] われます[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I am told by everybody that (I’m) strange.

example_sentences.ja: 光[ひかり]の 速[はや]さを 超[こ]えるのは、 不可能[ふかのう]だと 思[おも] われる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Exceeding the speed of light is thought to be impossible.

example_sentences.ja: この 教科書[きょうかしょ]は 多[おお]くの 人[ひと]に 読[よ] まれている[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: This textbook is being read by a large number of people.

example_sentences.ja: 外国人[がいこくじん]に 質問[しつもん]を 聞[き] かれた[;n] が、 答[こた]えられなかった。
example_sentences.en: I was asked a question by a foreigner but I couldn’t answer.

example_sentences.ja: このパッケージには、あらゆるものが 含[ふく] まれている[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Everything is included in this package.

explanation: The causative-passive form is simply the combination of causative and passive conjugations to mean that the action of making someone do something was done to that person. This would effectively translate into, “[someone] is made to do [something]”. The important thing to remember is the order of conjugation. The verb is first conjugated to the causative and then passive, never the other way around.
primary_text: 朝[あさ]ご 飯[はん]は 食[た]べたくなかったのに、 食[た]べ させられた[;n] 。
meaning:
Despite not wanting to eat breakfast, I 
was made to
 eat it.
item_key: grammar:朝ご飯は食べたくなかったのに、食べさせられた。

example_sentences.ja: 日本[にほん]では、お 酒[さけ]を 飲[の] ませられる[;n] ことが 多[おお]い。
example_sentences.en:
In Japan, the event of 
being made to
 drink is numerous.

example_sentences.ja: あいつに 二時間[にじかん]も 待[ま] たせられた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en:
I 
was made to
 wait 2 hours by that guy.

example_sentences.ja: 親[おや]に 毎日[まいにち] 宿題[しゅくだい]を させられる[;n] 。
example_sentences.en:
I 
am made to
 do homework every day by my parent(s).

explanation:
Going along with the shorter causative alternative, you can also use the same conjugation for the causative-passive form. I won’t cover it in too much detail because the usefulness of this form is rather limited just like the shorter causative form itself. The idea is to simply used the shortened causative form instead of using the regular causative conjugation. The rest is the same as before.

This form cannot be used in cases where the shorter causative form ends in 「さす」, in other words, you can’t have a 「さされる」 ending.
primary_text: 学生[がくせい]が 廊下[ろうか]に 立[た] たされた[;n] 。
meaning:
The student 
was made to
 stand in the hall.
item_key: grammar:学生が廊下に立たされた。

example_sentences.ja: 日本[にほん]では、お 酒[さけ]を 飲[の] まされる[;n] ことが 多[おお]い。
example_sentences.en:
In Japan, the event of 
being made to
 drink is numerous.

example_sentences.ja: あいつに 二時間[にじかん]も 待[ま] たされた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en:
I 
was made to
 wait 2 hours by that guy.

explanation: A number of these verbs do not follow the normal masu-conjugation rules and they include: 「なさる」、「いらっしゃる」、「おっしゃる」、「下さる」、 and 「ござる」 (which we will soon cover). For all masu-form tenses of these verbs, instead of the 「る」 becoming a 「り」 as it does with normal u-verbs, it instead becomes an 「い」. All other conjugations besides the masu-form do not change from regular u-verbs.
primary_text: アリスさん、もう 召[め;n]し 上[あ;n]がりました か。
meaning: Alice-san, did (you) eat already?
item_key: grammar:アリスさん、もう召し上がりましたか。

example_sentences.ja: 仕事[しごと]で 何[なに]を なさっている[;n] んですか。
example_sentences.en: What are you doing at work?

example_sentences.ja: 推薦状[すいせんじょう]を 書[か]いて くださる[;n] んですか。
example_sentences.en: You’re going to give me the favor of writing a recommendation letter?

example_sentences.ja: どちらから いらっしゃいました[;n] か。
example_sentences.en: Where did you come from?

example_sentences.ja: 今日[きょう]は、どちらへ いらっしゃいます[;n] か。
example_sentences.en: Where are you going today?

explanation: The following examples are all actions done by the speaker so they all use the humble form.
primary_text: 私[わたし]はキムと 申[もう;n]します 。
meaning: As for me, (people) say Kim. (I am called Kim.)

example_sentences.ja: 私[わたし]が 書[か]いたレポートを 見[み]て いただけます[;n] か。
example_sentences.en: Will I be able to receive the favor of getting my report looked at?

example_sentences.ja: 失礼[しつれい] 致[いた;n]します 。
example_sentences.en: Excuse me. (lit: I am doing a discourtesy.)

explanation:
In addition to these set expressions, there are some words that also have more polite counterparts. Probably the most important is the politer version of 「ある」, which is 「ござる」. This verb can be used for both inanimate and animate objects. It is neither honorific nor humble but it is a step above 「ある」 in politeness. However, unless you want to sound like a samurai, 「ござる」 is always used in the polite form: 「ございます」.

By extension, the politer version of 「です」 is 「でございます」. This is essentially the masu-form conjugation of 「でござる」, which comes from 「である」 literally meaning, “to exist as” (to be covered much later).
primary_text: こちらは、 私[わたし]の 部屋[へや] でございます[;n] 。
meaning: This way is my room.
item_key: grammar:こちらは、私の部屋でございます。

example_sentences.ja: お 手洗[てあら]いはこのビルの 二階[にかい]に ございます[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: The bathroom is on the second floor of this building.

explanation:
Honorific Conjugation 1: お + stem + に + なる
This kind of makes sense if you think of it as a person becoming the honorific state of a verb. All subsequent conjugations follow the normal rules of conjugating the u-verb 「なる」. To be honest, this type of sentence formulation is rarely used.
primary_text: 先生[せんせい]は お[;n] 見[み;n]えになります か。
meaning: Have you seen the teacher?
item_key: grammar:先生はお見えになりますか。

explanation:
Honorific Conjugation 2: お + stem + です
Service people want to be extra polite so they will often use this type of “double honorific” conjugation or 二重敬語 (in this case, the honorific 「召し上がる」 combined with the honorific conjugation). Whether it’s necessary or grammatically proper is another story.
primary_text: もう お[;n] 帰[かえ;n]りです か。
meaning: You’re going home already?

example_sentences.ja: 店内[てんない]で お[;n] 召[め;n]し 上[あ;n]がりです か。
example_sentences.en: Will you be dining in?

explanation:
Using 「ください」 with honorifics
You can also use 「下さい」 with a honorific verb by replacing 「になる」 with 「ください」. This is useful for when you want to ask somebody to do something but still use a honorific verb.

Similarly, with 「ご覧になる」, you simply replace 「になる」 with 「ください」.

This works for other nouns as well.
primary_text: 少々[しょうしょう] お[;n] 待[ま;n]ちください 。
meaning: Please wait a moment.

example_sentences.ja: こちらに ご[;n] 覧[らん;n] 下[くだ;n]さい 。
example_sentences.en: Please look this way.

example_sentences.ja: 閉[し]まるドアに ご[;n] 注意[ちゅうい;n] 下[くだ;n]さい 。
example_sentences.en: Please be careful of the closing doors.

explanation:
Humble Form
Humble verbs are formed in the following fashion.

Humble Conjugation: お + stem + する
primary_text: よろしく お[;n] 願[ねが;n]いします 。
meaning: I properly make request.
item_key: grammar:よろしくお願いします。

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい]、 お[;n] 聞[き;n]きしたい[;n] ことがありますが。
example_sentences.en: Teacher, there’s something I want to ask you.

example_sentences.ja: すみません、 お[;n] 待[ま;n]たせしました 。
example_sentences.en: Sorry, I made you wait (causative form).

example_sentences.ja: 千円[せんえん]から お[;n] 預[あず;n]かりいたします 。
example_sentences.en: We’ll be holding on [from?] your 1000 yen.

explanation: We learned how to make polite requests using 「～ください」 in a previous section and we just looked at how to use honorific verbs with requests as well. However, there is yet another way to make requests using honorific verbs. This grammar only applies to the honorific verbs with special 「～ます」 conjugations that we just covered. This includes 「下さる」、「いらっしゃる」、「なさる」、and 「おっしゃる」.　I’ve never actually seen this used with 「おっしゃる」, but it is grammatically possible.
meaning: Please come in!

example_sentences.ja: いらっしゃい[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: Please come in!

example_sentences.ja: ありがとうございました。またお 越[こ]し くださいませ[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Thank you very much. Please come again.

example_sentences.ja: どうぞ、ごゆっくり なさいませ[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Please take your time and relax.

explanation: When 「しまう」 is used in this sense, it is normal to attach it to the te-form of another verb to express an action that is done or happened unintentionally. As is common with this type of grammar, the tense is decided by the tense of 「しまう」.
primary_text: そのケーキを 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べ てしまった[;n] 。
meaning: Oops, I ate that whole cake.
item_key: grammar:そのケーキを全部食べてしまった。

example_sentences.ja: 毎日[まいにち]ケーキを 食[た]べて、２キロ 太[ふと]っ てしまいました[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I ate cake everyday and I (unintentionally) gained two kilograms.

example_sentences.ja: ちゃんと 食[た]べないと、 痩[や]せ てしまいます[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en: If you don’t eat properly, you’ll (unintentionally) lose weight you know.

example_sentences.ja: 結局[けっきょく]、 嫌[いや]なことをさせ てしまった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: In the end, I (unintentionally) made [someone] do something distasteful.

example_sentences.ja: ごめん、 待[ま]たせ てしまって[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: Sorry about (unintentionally) making you wait!

example_sentences.ja: 金魚[きんぎょ]がもう 死[し]ん でしまった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: The goldfish died already (oops).

explanation: In casual speech, the 「～てしまう」 is often substituted by 「～ちゃう」 while 「～でしまう」 is substituted by 「じゃう」. Both 「～ちゃう」 and 「～じゃう」 conjugate just like regular u-verbs.
primary_text: 金魚[きんぎょ]がもう 死[し]ん じゃった[;n] 。
meaning: The goldfish died already.
item_key: grammar:金魚がもう死んじゃった。

example_sentences.ja: もう 帰[かえ]っ ちゃって[;n] いい？
example_sentences.en: Is it ok if I went home already?

example_sentences.ja: みんな、どっか 行[い]っ ちゃった[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en: Everybody went off somewhere.

example_sentences.ja: そろそろ 遅[おそ]くなっ ちゃう[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en: It’ll gradually become late, you know.

explanation: There is yet another very colloquial version of 「～てしまう」 and 「～でしまう」 where it is replaced by 「～ちまう」 and 「～じまう」 respectively. This version conjures an image of rough and coarse middle-aged man.
primary_text: また 遅刻[ちこく] しちまった[;n] よ。
meaning: Darn, I’m late again.
item_key: grammar:また遅刻しちまったよ。

example_sentences.ja: ごめん、ついお 前[まえ]を 呼[よ]ん じまった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Sorry, I just ended up calling you unconsciously.

explanation: You may have noticed that 「しまう」 has another definition meaning “to finish something completely”. You may want to consider this a totally separate verb from the 「しまう」 we have covered so far. Occasionally but not usually, 「しまう」 will have this meaning rather than the unintended action.
primary_text: 宿題[しゅくだい]をやっ てしまい[;n] なさい。
meaning: Finish your homework completely.
item_key: grammar:宿題をやってしまいなさい。

explanation:
When you combine 「こと」, the generic word for an event with 「ある」, you can talk about whether an event exists or not.

Using the past tense of the verb with 「こと」, you can talk about whether an event has ever taken place. This is essentially the only way you can say “have done” in Japanese so this is a very useful expression. You need to use this grammar any time you want to talk about whether someone has ever done something.
primary_text: 徹夜[てつや]して、 宿題[しゅくだい] すること[;n] は ある[;n] 。
meaning: There are times when I do homework while staying up all night.
item_key: grammar:徹夜して、宿題することはある。

example_sentences.ja: 一人[ひとり]で 行[い;n]くこと は ありません[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I never go by myself.

example_sentences.ja: パリに 行[い;n]ったこと は あります[;n] か。
example_sentences.en: Have you ever gone to Paris?

example_sentences.ja: お 寿司[すし]を 食[た;n]べたこと が ある[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I’ve had sushi before.

example_sentences.ja: 日本[にほん]の 映画[えいが]を 観[み;n]たことない の？
example_sentences.en: You’ve never seen a Japanese movie?

example_sentences.ja: ヨーロッパに 行[;n]ったこと が あったら[;n] いいな。
example_sentences.en: It would be nice if I ever go to Europe.

example_sentences.ja: そういうのを 見[み;n]たこと が なかった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: I had never seen anything like that.

example_sentences.ja: 一度[いちど] 行[い;n]ったこと も ない[;n] んです。
example_sentences.en: I’ve never gone, not even once.

explanation: 「ところ」（所） is usually used to indicate a generic physical location. However, it can also hold a much broader meaning ranging from a characteristic to a place in time.
primary_text: 早[はや]くきて。 映画[えいが]は 今[いま]ちょうどいい ところ[;n] だよ。
meaning: Come quickly. We’re at the good part of the movie.
item_key: grammar:早くきて。映画は今ちょうどいいところだよ。

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]は 優[やさ]しい ところ[;n] もあるよ。
example_sentences.en: His personality has some gentle parts too.

example_sentences.ja: 今[いま]は 授業[じゅぎょう]が 終[おわ]った ところ[;n] です。
example_sentences.en: Class has ended just now.

example_sentences.ja: これから 行[い]く ところ[;n] でした。
example_sentences.en: I was just about to go from now.

explanation: The generic object noun 「もの」 can be used as a casual and feminine way of emphasizing something. This is identical to the explanatory feminine emphasis expressed by the 「の」 particle. Just like the explanatory 「の」 particle, the 「の」 is often changed into 「ん」 resulting in 「もん」. Using 「もん」 sounds very feminine and a little cheeky (in a cute way).
primary_text:
Ａ「どうしてこなかったの？」

Ｂ「 授業[じゅぎょう]があった もの[;n] 。」
meaning:
A: Why didn’t (you) come?
B: (I) had class. [feminine explanatory]
item_key: grammar:a「どうしてこなかったの?」b「授業があったもの。」

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「どうしてこなかったの？」

Ｂ「 授業[じゅぎょう]があった もん[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Why didn’t (you) come?
B: (I) had class, so there. [feminine explanatory]

explanation: 「かもしれない」 is used to mean “maybe” or “possibly” and is less certain than the word 「多分」. It attaches to the end of a complete clause. For noun and na-adjective clauses, the declarative 「だ」 must be removed. It can also be written in kanji as 「かも知れない」 and you can treat it the same as a negative ru-verb (there is no positive equivalent) so the masu-form would become 「かもしれません」. In casual speech, it can be abbreviated to just 「かも」. There is also a very masculine version 「かもしれん」, which is simply a different type of negative verb.
primary_text: スミスさんは 食堂[しょくどう]に 行[い]った かもしれません[;n] 。
meaning: Smith-san may have gone to the cafeteria.
item_key: grammar:スミスさんは食堂に行ったかもしれません。

example_sentences.ja: 雨[あめ]で 試合[しあい]は 中止[ちゅうし]になる かもしれない[;n] ね。
example_sentences.en: The game may become canceled by rain, huh?

example_sentences.ja: この 映画[えいが]は 一回[いっかい] 観[み]たことある かも[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: I might have already seen this movie once.

example_sentences.ja: あそこが 代々木[よよぎ] 公園[こうえん] かもしれない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: That might be Yoyogi park over there.

example_sentences.ja: もう 逃[に]げられない かもしれん[;n] ぞ。
example_sentences.en: Might not be able to escape anymore, you know.

explanation:
「でしょう」 is used to express a level of some certainty and is close in meaning to 「多分」. Just like 「～です／～ます」, it must come at the end of a complete sentence. It does not have any other conjugations. You can also replace 「～ですか」 with 「～でしょうか」 to make the question sound slightly more polite and less assuming by adding a slight level of uncertainty.

If you want to sound really, really polite, you can even add 「～でしょうか」 to the end of a 「～ます」 ending.
primary_text: 明日[あした]も 雨[あめ] でしょう[;n] 。
meaning: Probably rain tomorrow too.

example_sentences.ja: 学生[がくせい]さん でしょう[;n] か。
example_sentences.en: Are (you) student?

example_sentences.ja: これからどこへ 行[い]くん でしょう[;n] か？
example_sentences.en: Where (are you) going from here?

example_sentences.ja: 休[やす]ませていただけます でしょう[;n] か。
example_sentences.en: May I receive the favor of resting, possibly?

explanation:
The casual equivalent of 「でしょう」 is surprisingly enough 「でしょう」. However, when you are speaking in a polite manner, the 「でしょう」 is enunciated flatly while in casual speech, it has a rising intonation and can be shortened to 「でしょ」. In addition, since people tend to be more assertive in casual situations, the casual version has a much stronger flavor often sounding more like, “See, I told you so!”

「だろう」 means essentially the same thing as 「でしょう」 except that it sounds more masculine and is used mostly by males.
primary_text:
Ａ「あっ！ 遅刻[ちこく]しちゃう！」
Ｂ「だから、 時間[じかん]がないって 言[い]った でしょう[;n] ！」
meaning:
A: Ah! We’re going to be late!
B: That’s why I told you there was no time!
item_key: grammar:a「あっ!遅刻しちゃう!」b「だから、時間がないって言ったでしょう!」

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「これから 食[た]べに 行[い]くん でしょ[;n] 。」
Ｂ「だったら？」
example_sentences.en:
A: You’re going to eat from now aren’t you?
B: So what if I am?

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 掃除[そうじ]、 手伝[てつだ]ってくれる でしょう[;n] 。」
Ｂ「え？そうなの？」
example_sentences.en:
A: You’re going to help me clean, right?
B: Huh? Is that so?

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「アリスはどこだ？」
Ｂ「もう 寝[ね]ている だろう[;n] 。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Where is Alice?
B: Probably sleeping already.

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「もう 家[いえ]に 帰[かえ]るん だろう[;n] 。」
Ｂ「そうよ。」
example_sentences.en:
A: You’re going home already, right?
B: That’s right.

explanation:
The particle 「だけ」 is used to express that that’s all there is. Just like the other particles we have already learned, it is directly attached to the end of whichever word that it applies to.

When one of the major particles are also applied to a word, these particles must come after 「だけ」. In fact, the ordering of multiple particles usually start from the most specific to the most general.
meaning: Just apple(s) (and nothing else).

example_sentences.ja: これとそれ だけ[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Just that and this (and nothing else).

example_sentences.ja: それ だけ[;n] は、 食[た]べないでください。
example_sentences.en: Just don’t eat that. (Anything else is assumed to be OK).

example_sentences.ja: この 歌[いた] だけ[;n] を 歌[うた]わなかった。
example_sentences.en: Didn’t sing just this song.

example_sentences.ja: その 人[ひと] だけ[;n] が 好[す]きだったんだ。
example_sentences.en: That person was the only person I liked.

example_sentences.ja: この 販売機[はんばいき] だけ[;n] では、 五百円玉[ごひゃくえんだま]が 使[つか]えない。
example_sentences.en: Cannot use 500 yen coin in just this vending machine.

example_sentences.ja: 小林[こばやし]さんから だけ[;n] は、 返事[へんじ]が 来[こ]なかった。
example_sentences.en: A reply has not come from only Kobayashi-san.

example_sentences.ja: 準備[じゅんび]が 終[お]わったから、これからは 食[た]べる だけ[;n] だ。
example_sentences.en: Since the preparations are done, from here we just have to eat.

example_sentences.ja: ここに 名前[なまえ]を 書[か]く だけ[;n] でいいですか？
example_sentences.en: Is it ok to just write [my] name here?

explanation: A particle that is essentially identical both grammatically and in meaning to 「だけ」 is 「のみ」. However, unlike 「だけ」, which is used in regular conversations, 「のみ」 is usually only used in a written context. It is often used for explaining policies, in manuals, and other things of that nature. This grammar really belongs in the advanced section since formal language has a different flavor and tone from what we have seen so far. However, it is covered here because it is essentially identical to 「だけ」. Just googling for 「のみ」 will quickly show the difference in the type of language that is used with 「のみ」 as opposed to 「だけ」.
primary_text: この 乗車券[じょうしゃけん]は 発売[はつばい] 当日[とうじつ] のみ[;n] 有効[ゆうこう]です。
meaning: This boarding ticket is only valid on the date on which it was purchased.
item_key: grammar:この乗車券は発売当日のみ有効です。

example_sentences.ja: アンケート 対象[たいしょう]は 大学生[だいがくせい] のみ[;n] です。
example_sentences.en: The targets of this survey are only college students.

explanation: I carefully phrased the title of this section to show that 「しか」 must be used to indicate the lack of everything else. In other words, the rest of the sentence must always be negative.
primary_text: これ しか[;n] ない。
meaning: There’s nothing but this.

example_sentences.ja: これ しか[;n] 見[み]ない。
example_sentences.en: Don’t see anything else but this.

example_sentences.ja: 今日[きょう]は 忙[いそが]しくて、 朝[あさ]ご 飯[はん] しか[;n] 食[た]べられなかった 。
example_sentences.en: Today was busy and couldn’t eat anything but breakfast.

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 全部[ぜんぶ] 買[か]うの？」
Ｂ「ううん、これ しか[;n] 買[か]わない」
example_sentences.en:
A: 
You’re buying everything?

B: Nah, won’t buy anything else but this.

example_sentences.ja: アリスから しか[;n] 何[なに]ももらってない。
example_sentences.en: I didn’t receive anything except from Alice.

example_sentences.ja: これから 頑張[がんば]る しか[;n] ない！
example_sentences.en: There’s nothing to do but try our best!

example_sentences.ja: こうなったら、 逃[に]げる しか[;n] ない。
example_sentences.en: There no choice but to run away once it turns out like this.

example_sentences.ja: もう 腐[くさ]っているから、 捨[す]てる しか[;n] ないよ。
example_sentences.en: It’s rotten already so there’s nothing to do but throw it out.

explanation: 「っきゃ」 is another version of 「しか」 that means essentially the same thing and works exactly the same way. Just substitute 「しか」 with 「っきゃ」 and you’re good to go. This version is a bit stronger than 「しか」 in emphasis but it’s not used nearly as often so I wouldn’t worry about it too much. I briefly cover it here just in case you do run into this expression.
primary_text: これは 買[か]う っきゃ[;n] ない！
meaning: There’s nothing but to buy this!
item_key: grammar:これは買うっきゃない!

example_sentences.ja: こうなったら、もうやる っきゃ[;n] ない！
example_sentences.en: If things turn out like this, there nothing to do but to just do it!

explanation: 「ばかり」 is used to express the condition where there’s so much of something to the point where there’s nothing else. Notice this is fundamentally different from 「しか」 which expresses a lack of everything else but the item in question. In more casual situations, 「ばかり」 is usually pronounced 「ばっかり」 or just 「ばっか」.
primary_text: 何[なん]だよ！おばさん ばっかり[;n] じゃないか？
meaning: What the? Isn’t it nothing but obasan?
item_key: grammar:何だよ!おばさんばっかりじゃないか?

example_sentences.ja: いやだ。おばさん ばっかり[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Eww. It’s nothing but obasan.

example_sentences.ja: 崇[たかし] 君[くん]は 漫画[まんが] ばっかり[;n] 読[よ]んでてさ。かっこ 悪[わる]い。
example_sentences.en: Takashi-kun is reading nothing but comic books… He’s so uncool.

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]は 麻雀[まーじゃん] ばかり[;n] です。
example_sentences.en: He’s nothing but mahjong. (He does nothing but play mahjong.)

example_sentences.ja: 直美[なおみ]ちゃんと 遊[あそ]ぶ ばっかり[;n] でしょう！
example_sentences.en: You’re hanging out with Naomi-chan all the time, aren’t you!

example_sentences.ja: 最近[さいきん]は 仕事[しごと] ばっか[;n] だよ。
example_sentences.en: Lately, it’s nothing but work.

explanation:
「すぎる」 is a regular ru-verb written 「過ぎる」 meaning, “to exceed”. When 「すぎる」 is attached to the end of other verbs and adjectives, it means that it is too much or that it has exceeded the normal levels. For verbs, you must directly attach 「すぎる」 to the stem of the verb. For example, 「食べすぎる」 means “to eat too much” and 「飲みすぎる」 means “to drink too much”. For adjectives, you just attach it to the end after you remove the last 「い」 from the i-adjectives (as usual). One more rule is that for both negative verbs and adjectives, one must remove the 「い」 from 「ない」 and replace with 「さ」 before attaching 「すぎる」. There is no tense (past or non-past) associated with this grammar. Since 「すぎる」 is a regular ru-verb, this grammar always results in a regular ru-verb.

It is also common to change 「すぎる」 into its stem and use it as a noun.
primary_text: 佐藤[さとう]さんは 料理[りょうり]が 上手[じょうず]で、また 食[た]べ 過[す;n]ぎました 。
meaning: Satou-san is good at cooking and I ate too much again.
item_key: grammar:佐藤さんは料理が上手で、また食べ過ぎました。

example_sentences.ja: お 酒[さけ]を 飲[の]み すぎない[;n] ように 気[き]をつけてね。
example_sentences.en: Be careful to not drink too much, ok?

example_sentences.ja: 大[おお]き すぎる[;n] からトランクに 入[はい]らないぞ。
example_sentences.en: It won’t fit in the trunk cause it’s too big, man.

example_sentences.ja: 静[しず]か すぎる[;n] 。 罠[わな]かもしれないよ。
example_sentences.en: It’s too quiet. It might be a trap, you know.

example_sentences.ja: 時間[じかん]が 足[た]りな さすぎて[;n] 、 何[なに]もできなかった。
example_sentences.en: Due to too much of a lack of time, I couldn’t do anything.

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]には、 彼女[かのじょ]がもったいなさ すぎる[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en: She is totally wasted on him (too good for him).

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 昨晩[さくばん]のこと、 全然[ぜんぜん] 覚[おぼ]えてないな。」
Ｂ「それは 飲[の]み すぎ[;n] だよ。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Man, I don’t remember anything about last night.
B: That’s drinking too much.

explanation:
When the 「も」 particle comes after some type of amount, it means that the amount indicated is way too much.

Notice that the 「も」 particle is attached to the amount “three times”. This sentence implies that the speaker called even three times and still the person didn’t pick up the phone. We understand this to mean that three times are a lot of times to call someone.
primary_text: 昨日[きのう]、 電話[でんわ] 三回[さんかい] も[;n] したよ！
meaning: I called you like three times yesterday!
item_key: grammar:昨日、電話三回もしたよ!

example_sentences.ja: 試験[しけん]のために 三時間[さんじかん] も[;n] 勉強[べんきょう]した。
example_sentences.en: I studied three whole hours for the exam.

example_sentences.ja: 今年[ことし]、１０キロ も[;n] 太[ふと]っちゃった！
example_sentences.en: I gained 10 whole kilograms this year!

explanation: The noun 「ほど」（程） is attached to a word in a sentence to express the extent of something. It can modify nouns as well as verbs.
primary_text: 今日[きょう]の 天気[てんき]はそれ ほど[;n] 暑[あつ]くない。
meaning: Today’s weather is not hot to that extent.
item_key: grammar:今日の天気はそれほど暑くない。

example_sentences.ja: 寝[ね]る 時間[じかん]がない ほど[;n] 忙[いそが]しい。
example_sentences.en: Busy to the extent that there’s no time to sleep.

explanation: When you use this with conditionals, you can express something that translates into English as, “The more you [verb], the more…” The grammar is always formed in the following sequence: [conditional of verb] followed immediately by [same verb+ ほど]
primary_text: 韓国[かんこく] 料理[りょうり]は 食[た;n]べれば 食[た;n]べるほど 、おいしくなる。
meaning: About Korean food, the more you eat the tastier it becomes.
item_key: grammar:韓国料理は食べれば食べるほど、おいしくなる。

example_sentences.ja: 歩[ある;n]いたら 歩[ある;n]くほど 、 迷[まよ]ってしまった。
example_sentences.en: The more I walked, the more I got lost.

example_sentences.ja: 勉強[べんきょう]を すればするほど[;n] 、 頭[あたま]がよくなるよ。
example_sentences.en: The more you study, the more you will become smarter.

example_sentences.ja: iPodは、ハードディスクの 容量[ようりょう]が 大[おお;n]きければ 大[おお;n]きいほど もっとたくさんの 曲[きょく]が 保存[ほぞん]できます。
example_sentences.en: About iPod, the larger the hard disk capacity, the more songs you can save.

example_sentences.ja: 航空券[こうくうけん]は 安[やす;n]ければ 安[やす;n]いほど いいとは 限[かぎ]らない。
example_sentences.en: It’s not necessarily the case that the cheaper the ticket, the better it is.

example_sentences.ja: 文章[ぶんしょう]は、 短[みじか;n]ければ 短[みじか;n]いほど 、 簡単[かんたん;n]なら 簡単[かんたん;n]なほど よいです。
example_sentences.en: The shorter and simpler the sentences, the better it is.
