explanation: We will now learn how to add 「さ」 to adjectives to indicate an amount of that adjective. For example, we can attach 「さ」 to the adjective for “high” in order to get “height”. Instead of looking at the height, we can even attach 「さ」 to the adjective for “low” to focus on the amount of lowness as opposed to the amount of highness. In fact, there is nothing to stop us from using this with any adjective to indicate an amount of that adjective. The result becomes a regular noun indicating the amount of that adjective.
primary_text: このビルの 高[たか] さ[;n] は 何[なん]ですか？
meaning: What is the height of this building?
item_key: grammar:このビルの高さは何ですか?

example_sentences.ja: 犬[いぬ]の 聴覚[ちょうかく]の 敏感[びんかん] さ[;n] を 人間[にんげん]と 比[くら]べると、はるかに 上[うえ]だ。
example_sentences.en: If you compare the level of sensitivity of hearing of dogs to humans, it is far above.

explanation:
We’ve already briefly gone over 「よう」 and learned that 「よう」 means an appearance or manner. We can use this definition to say that something has an appearance or manner of a certain state. This word can be used in many ways to express similarity. The simplest example is by directly modifying the relative clause. When the sentence ends in 「よう」, you must explicitly express the state-of-being by adding 「だ」, 「です」, or 「でございます」.

When directly modifying nouns or na-adjectives, you must use the 「の」 particle for nouns or attach 「な」 to na-adjectives.

On a side note, you can’t say 「おいしいようだ」 to say that something looks tasty. This is like saying, “This dish apparently is tasty,” which can actually be kind of rude.
primary_text: ここには、 誰[だれ]もいない よう[;n] だ。
meaning: Looks like no one is here.
item_key: grammar:ここには、誰もいないようだ。

example_sentences.ja: 映画[えいが]を 観[み]た よう[;n] です。
example_sentences.en: Looks like (he) watched the movie.

example_sentences.ja: 学生[がくせい]の よう[;n] だ。
example_sentences.en: Looks like it’s a student.

example_sentences.ja: ここは 静[しず]かな よう[;n] だ。
example_sentences.en: Looks like it’s quiet.

example_sentences.ja: あの 人[ひと]を 見[み]た よう[;n] な 気[き]がした。
example_sentences.en: Had a feeling like I saw that person before.

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]は 学生[がくせい]の よう[;n] な 雰囲気[ふんいき]ですね。
example_sentences.en: He has a student-like atmosphere.

example_sentences.ja: ちょっと 怒[おこ]った よう[;n] に 聞[き]こえた。
example_sentences.en: Was able to hear it like (she) was a little mad.

example_sentences.ja: 何[なに]も 起[お]こらなかった よう[;n] に 言[い]った。
example_sentences.en: Said (it) like nothing happened.

example_sentences.ja: もう 売[う]り 切[き]れの よう[;n] だ。
example_sentences.en: It appears that it is sold-out already.

example_sentences.ja: このピザはお 好[この]み 焼[や]きの よう[;n] に 見[み]える。
example_sentences.en: This pizza looks like okonomiyaki.

explanation: Another way to express similarity which is considered more casual is by using 「みたい」. Do not confuse this with the 「たい」 conjugation of 「見る」. The main difference is that this 「みたい」 can be attached directly to nouns, adjectives, and verbs just like particles which i-adjectives like 「～たい」 obviously can’t do.
primary_text: もう 売[う]り 切[き]れ みたい[;n] 。
meaning: Looks like it’s sold out already.

example_sentences.ja: 制服[せいふく]を 着[き]ている 姿[すがた]をみると、 学生[がくせい] みたい[;n] です。
example_sentences.en: Looking at the uniform-wearing figure, (person) looks like a student.

example_sentences.ja: このピザはお 好[この]み 焼[や]き みたい[;n] じゃない？
example_sentences.en: Doesn’t this pizza looks like okonomiyaki?

explanation:
The problem with English is that the expression, “seems like” has too many meanings. It can mean similarity in appearance, similarity in behavior or even that current evidence points to a likely outcome. We will now learn how to say the third meaning: how to indicate a likely outcome given the situation.

Just like the grammar we have learned so far in this lesson, we can use this grammar by simply attaching 「そう」 to the end of verbs, and adjectives. However, there are four important different cases. Actually, I just noticed this but the conjugation rules are exactly the same as the 「～すぎる」 grammar we learned in the last section. The only difference is that for the adjective 「いい」, you need to change it to 「よさ」 before attaching 「そう」 to create 「よさそう」.
primary_text: バランスが 崩[くず]れて、 一瞬[いっしゅん] 倒[たお]れ そう[;n] だった。
meaning: Losing my balance, I seemed likely to fall for a moment.
item_key: grammar:バランスが崩れて、一瞬倒れそうだった。

example_sentences.ja: この 辺[あた]りにあり そう[;n] だけどな。
example_sentences.en: It seems likely that it would be around here but…

example_sentences.ja: この 漬物[つけもの]はおいし そう[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: I bet this pickled vegetable is tasty! (This pickled vegetable looks good!)

example_sentences.ja: これも 結構[けっこう] よさそう[;n] だけど、やっぱり 高[たか]いよね。
example_sentences.en: This one also seems to be good but, as expected, it’s expensive, huh?

example_sentences.ja: お 前[まえ]なら、 金髪[きんぱつ]の 女[おんな]が 好[す]き そう[;n] だな。
example_sentences.en: Knowing you, I bet you like blond-haired girls.

example_sentences.ja: もう１０ 時[じ]になったから、 来[こ]なさ そう[;n] だね。
example_sentences.en: Since it already became 10:00, it’s likely that (person) won’t come.

example_sentences.ja: これはただの 試合[しあい]じゃなさ そう[;n] だ。
example_sentences.en: This isn’t likely to be an ordinary match.

explanation: The reason that there are so many annoying rules to using 「～そう」 is to distinguish it from this next grammar we will learn. This is a useful grammar for talking about things you heard that doesn’t necessary have anything to do with how you yourself, think or feel. Unlike the last grammar we learned, you can simply attach 「そうだ」 to verbs and i-adjectives. For na-adjectives and nouns, you must indicate the state-of-being by adding 「だ」 to the noun/na-adjective. Also, notice that 「そう」 itself must always end in 「だ」、「です」、or 「でございます」. These differences are what distinguishes this grammar from the one we learned in the last section. There are no tenses for this grammar.
primary_text: 明日[あした]、 雨[あめ]が 降[ふ]る そう[;n] だ。
meaning: I hear that it’s going to rain tomorrow.

example_sentences.ja: 毎日[まいにち] 会[あ]いに 行[い]った そう[;n] です。
example_sentences.en: I heard he went to meet everyday.

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]は、 高校生[こうこうせい]だ そう[;n] です。
example_sentences.en: I hear that he is a high school student.

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「 今日[きょう]、 田中[たなか]さんはこないの？」
Ｂ「だ そう[;n] です。」
example_sentences.en:
A: Is Tanaka-san not coming today?
B: So I hear.

explanation:
「らしい」 can be directly attached to nouns, adjectives, or verbs to show that things appear to be a certain way due to what you’ve heard. This is different from 「～そうだ」because 「～そうだ」 indicates something you heard about specifically while 「らしい」 means things seem to be a certain way based on some things you heard about the subject. 「らしい」 conjugates like a normal i-adjective.

Another way to use 「らしい」 is to indicate that a person seems to be a certain thing due to his behavior.
primary_text:
Ａ「 今日[きょう]、 田中[たなか]さんはこないの？」
Ｂ「こない らしい[;n] 。」
meaning:
A: Is Tanaka-san not coming today?
B: Seems like it (based on what I heard).
item_key: grammar:a「今日、田中さんはこないの?」b「こないらしい。」

example_sentences.ja:
Ａ「あの 人[ひと]は 何[なん]なの？」
Ｂ「 美由紀[みゆき]さんの 友達[ともだち] らしい[;n] ですよ。」
example_sentences.en:
A: What is that person over there?
B: Seems to be Miyuki-san’s friend (based on what I heard).

example_sentences.ja: あの 子[こ]は 子供[こども] らしくない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: That child does not act like a child.

example_sentences.ja: 大人[おとな] らしく[;n] するつもりだったのに、 大騒[おおさわ]ぎしてしまった。
example_sentences.en: Despite the fact that I planned to act like an adult, I ended up making a big ruckus.

explanation: A really casual way to express similarity is to attach 「っぽい」 to the word that reflects the resemblance. Because this is a very casual expression, you can use it as a casual version for all the different types of expression for similarity covered above.
primary_text: あの 人[ひと]はちょっと 韓国人[かんこくじん] っぽい[;n] よね。
meaning: That person looks a little like Korean person, huh?
item_key: grammar:あの人はちょっと韓国人っぽいよね。

example_sentences.ja: みんなで、もう 全部[ぜんぶ] 食[た]べてしまった っぽい[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en: It appears that everybody ate everything already.

example_sentences.ja: 恭子[きょうこ]は 全然[ぜんぜん] 女[おんな] っぽくない[;n] ね。
example_sentences.en: Kyouko is not womanly at all, huh?

explanation:
The noun 「方」 is read as 「ほう」 when it is used to mean a direction or orientation. As an aside, it can also be read as 「かた」 when it is used as a politer version of 「人」.

When we use 「方」 to mean direction, we can use it for comparison by saying one way of things is better, worse, etc., than the other way. Grammatically, it works just like any other regular nouns.

For non-negative verbs, you can also use the past tense to add more certainty and confidence, particularly when making suggestions.
primary_text: ご 飯[はん]の 方[ほう;n] がおいしい。
meaning: Rice is tastier. (lit: The way of rice is tasty.)

example_sentences.ja: 鈴木[すずき]さんの 方[ほう;n] が 若[わか]い。
example_sentences.en: Suzuki-san is younger. (lit: The way of Suzuki is young.)

example_sentences.ja: 学生[がくせい]じゃない 方[ほう;n] がいいよ。
example_sentences.en: It’s better to not be a student. (lit: The way of not being student is good.)

example_sentences.ja: 赤[あか]ちゃんは、 静[しず]かな 方[ほう;n] が 好[す]き。
example_sentences.en: Like quiet babies more. (lit: About babies, the quiet way is desirable.)

example_sentences.ja: ゆっくり 食[た]べた 方[ほう;n] が 健康[けんこう]にいいよ。
example_sentences.en: It’s better for your health to eat slowly.

example_sentences.ja: こちらから 行[い]った 方[ほう;n] が 早[はや]かった。
example_sentences.en: It was faster to go from this way.

example_sentences.ja: 怖[こわ]い 映画[えいが]は 観[み]ない 方[ほう;n] がいいよ。
example_sentences.en: It’s better not to watch scary movie(s).

example_sentences.ja: そんなに 飲[の]まなかった 方[ほう;n] がよかった。
example_sentences.en: It was better not to have drunk that much.

explanation:
You can think of 「より」 as being the opposite of 「方」. It means, “rather than” or “as opposed to”. It attaches directly to the back of any word. It is usually used in conjunction with 「方」 to say something like, “This way is better as opposed to that way.”

Words associated with 「より」 do not need any tense. Notice in the following sentence that 「食べる」 in front of 「より」 is present tense even though 「食べる」 in front of 「方」 is past tense.
primary_text: 花[はな] より[;n] 団子[だんご]。
meaning: Dango rather than flowers. (This is a very famous saying.)

example_sentences.ja: ご 飯[はん]の 方[ほう]が、パン より[;n] おいしい。
example_sentences.en: Rice tastes better than bread. (lit: The rice way is tasty as opposed to bread.)

example_sentences.ja: キムさん より[;n] 鈴木[すずき]さんの 方[ほう]が 若[わか]い。
example_sentences.en: Suzuki-san is younger than Kim-san. (lit: The way of Suzuki-san is young as opposed to Kim-san.)

example_sentences.ja:
鈴木[すずき]「 毎日[まいにち] 仕事[しごと]に 行[い]くのが 嫌[いや]だ。」
スミス「 仕事[しごと]がない より[;n] ましだよ。」
example_sentences.en:
Suzuki: I don’t like going to work everyday.

Smith: It’s not as bad as opposed to not having a job.

example_sentences.ja: ゆっくり 食[た]べた 方[ほう]が 早[はや]く 食[た]べる より[;n] いい。
example_sentences.en: It is better to eat slowly as opposed to eating quickly.

explanation: You can also use 「より」 with question words such as 「誰」、「何」、or 「どこ」 to make a superlative by comparing with everything or everybody else. In this case, though not required, it is common to include the 「も」 particle.
primary_text: 商品[しょうひん]の 品質[ひんしつ]を 何[なに;n]より 大切[たいせつ]にしています。
meaning: We place value in product’s quality over anything else.
item_key: grammar:商品の品質を何より大切にしています。

example_sentences.ja: この 仕事[しごと]は 誰[だれ;n]よりも 早[はや]くできます。
example_sentences.en: Can do this job more quickly than anyone else.

explanation:
You can also attach 「方」 to the stem of verbs to express a way to do that verb. In this usage, 「方」 is read as 「かた」 and the result becomes a noun. For example, 「行き方」（いきかた） means, “the way to go” or 「食べ方」（たべかた）means, “the way to eat”. This expression is probably what you want to use when you want to ask how to do something.

When verbs are transformed to this form, the result becomes a noun clause. Sometimes, this requires a change of particles. For instance, while 「行く」 usually involves a target (the 「に」 or 「へ」 particle), since 「行き方」 is a noun clause, example 1 becomes 「新宿の行き方」 instead of the familiar 「新宿に行く」.
primary_text: 新宿[しんじゅく]の 行[い]き 方[かた;n] は 分[わ]かりますか。
meaning: Do you know the way to go to Shinjuku?
item_key: grammar:新宿の行き方は分かりますか。

example_sentences.ja: そういう 食[た]べ 方[かた;n] は 体[からだ]によくないよ。
example_sentences.en: Eating in that way is not good for your body.

example_sentences.ja: 漢字[かんじ]の 書[か]き 方[かた;n] を 教[おし]えてくれますか？
example_sentences.en: Can you teach me the way of writing kanji?

example_sentences.ja: パソコンの 使[つか]い 方[かた;n] は、みんな 知[し]っているでしょう。
example_sentences.en: Probably everybody knows the way to use PC’s.

explanation: When you want to say, “depending on [X]”, you can do this in Japanese by simply attaching 「によって」 to [X].
primary_text: 人[ひと] によって[;n] 話[はなし]が 違[ちが]う。
meaning: The story is different depending on the person.

example_sentences.ja: 季節[きせつ] によって[;n] 果物[くだもの]はおいしくなったり、まずくなったりする。
example_sentences.en: Fruit becomes tasty or nasty depending on the season.

example_sentences.ja:
和子[かずこ]「 今日[きょう]は 飲[の]みに 行[い]こうか？」
大樹[だいき]「それは、 裕子[ゆうこ] による[;n] ね。」
example_sentences.en:
Kazuko: Shall we go drinking today?
Daiki: That depends on Yuuko.

explanation: Another expression using 「よる」 is by using it with the target and the decided conditional 「と」 to indicate a source of information. In English, this would translate to “according to [X]” where 「によると」 is attached to [X].
primary_text: 天気[てんき] 予報[よほう] によると[;n] 、 今日[きょう]は 雨[あめ]だそうだ。
meaning: According to the weather forecast, I hear today is rain.
item_key: grammar:天気予報によると、今日は雨だそうだ。

example_sentences.ja: 友達[ともだち]の 話[はなし] によると[;n] 、 朋子[ともこ]はやっとボーイフレンドを 見[み]つけたらしい。
example_sentences.en: According to a friend’s story, it appears that Tomoko finally found a boyfriend.

explanation: This is a short easy lesson on how to transform verbs into adjectives describing whether that action is easy or difficult to do. Basically, it consists of changing the verb into the stem and adding 「やすい」 for easy and 「にくい」 for hard. The result then becomes a regular i-adjective. Pretty easy, huh?
primary_text: この 字[じ]は 読[よ]み にくい[;n] 。
meaning: This hand-writing is hard to read.

example_sentences.ja: カクテルはビールより 飲[の]み やすい[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Cocktails are easier to drink than beer.

example_sentences.ja: 部屋[へや]が 暗[くら]かったので、 見[み] にくかった[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: Since the room was dark, it was hard to see.

example_sentences.ja: その 肉[にく]は 食[た]べ にくい[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: That meat is hard to eat.

explanation: The kanji for 「にくい」 actually comes from 「難い」 which can also be read as 「かたい」. As a result, you can also add a voiced version 「～がたい」 as a verb suffix to express the same thing as 「にくい」. 「にくい」 is more common for speaking while 「がたい」 is more suited for the written medium. 「にくい」 tends to be used for physical actions while 「がたい」 is usually reserved for less physical actions that don’t actually require movement. However, there seems to be no hard rule on which is more appropriate for a given verb so I suggest searching for both versions in google to ascertain the popularity of a given combination. You should also always write the suffix in hiragana to prevent ambiguities in the reading.
primary_text: 彼[かれ]との 忘[わす]れ がたい[;n] 思[おも]い 出[で]を 大切[たいせつ]にしている。
meaning: I am treating importantly the hard to forget memories of and with him.
item_key: grammar:彼との忘れがたい思い出を大切にしている。

example_sentences.ja: とても 信[しん]じ がたい[;n] 話[はなし]だが、 本当[ほんとう]に 起[お]こったらしい。
example_sentences.en: It’s a very difficult to believe story but it seems (from hearsay) that it really happened.

explanation: Yet another, more coarse variation of stem + 「にくい」 is to use 「づらい」 instead which is a slightly transformed version of 「辛い」（つらい）. This is not to be confused with the same 「辛い」（からい）, which means spicy!
primary_text: 日本語[にほんご]は 読[よ]み づらい[;n] な。
meaning: Man, Japanese is hard to read.
item_key: grammar:日本語は読みづらいな。

example_sentences.ja: 待[ま]ち 合[あ]わせは、 分[わ]かり づらい[;n] 場所[ばしょ]にしないでね。
example_sentences.en: Please don’t pick a difficult to understand location for the meeting arrangement.

explanation: Way back when, we learned how to express a sequence of actions and this worked fine for both positive and negative verbs. For instance, the sentence “I didn’t eat, and then I went to sleep” would become 「食べなくて寝た。」 However, this sentence sounds a bit strange because eating doesn’t have much to do with sleeping. What we probably really want to say is that we went to sleep without eating. To express this, we need to use a more generalized form of the negative request we covered at the very end of the giving and receiving lesson. In other words, instead of substituting the last 「い」 with 「くて」, we need only append 「で」 instead.
primary_text: 何[なに]も 食[た]べ ないで[;n] 寝[ね]ました。
meaning: Went to sleep without eating anything.
item_key: grammar:何も食べないで寝ました。

example_sentences.ja: 歯[は]を 磨[みが]か ないで[;n] 、 学校[がっこう]に 行[い]っちゃいました。
example_sentences.en: Went to school without brushing teeth (by accident).

example_sentences.ja: 宿題[しゅくだい]を しないで[;n] 、 授業[じゅぎょう]に 行[い]くのは、やめた 方[ほう]がいいよ。
example_sentences.en: It’s better to stop going to class without doing homework.

example_sentences.ja: 先生[せんせい]と 相談[そうだん] しないで[;n] 、この 授業[じゅぎょう]を 取[と]ることは 出来[でき]ない。
example_sentences.en: You cannot take this class without consulting with teacher.

explanation: Hopefully not too difficult. Another way to express the exact same thing is to replace the last 「ない」 part with 「ず」. However, the two exception verbs 「する」 and 「くる」 become 「せず」 and 「こず」 respectively. It is also common to see this grammar combined with the target 「に」 particle. This version is more formal than 「ないで」 and is not used as much in regular conversations.
primary_text: 彼[かれ]は 何[なに]も 言[い]わ ず[;n] 、 帰[かえ]ってしまった。
meaning: He went home without saying anything.
item_key: grammar:彼は何も言わず、帰ってしまった。

example_sentences.ja: 何[なに]も 食[た]べ ず[;n] にそんなにお 酒[さけ]を 飲[の]むと 当然[どうぜん] 酔[よ]っ 払[ぱら]いますよ。
example_sentences.en: Obviously, you’re going to get drunk if you drink that much without eating anything.

example_sentences.ja: 勉強[べんきょう] せず[;n] に 東大[とうだい] に 入[はい]れると 思[おも]わないな。
example_sentences.en: I don’t think you can get in Tokyo University without studying.

explanation: Finally, we cover another type of negative that is used mostly by older men. Since 「ない」 is so long and difficult to say (sarcasm), you can shorten it to just 「ん」. However, you can’t directly modify other words in this form; in other words, you can’t make it a modifying relative clause. In the same manner as before, 「する」 becomes 「せん」 and 「くる」 becomes 「こん」 though I’ve never heard or seen 「こん」 actually being used. If you have ever heard ｢すまん」 and wondered what that meant, it’s actually an example of this grammar. Notice that 「すみません」 is actually in polite negative form. Well, the plain form would be 「すまない」, right? That further transforms to just 「すまん」. The word brings up an image of おじさん but that may be just me. Anyway, it’s a male expression.
meaning: Sorry.

example_sentences.ja: 韓国人[かんこくじん]と 結婚[けっこん]しなくてはなら ん[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: You must marry a Korean!

example_sentences.ja: そんなことはさせ ん[;n] ！
example_sentences.en: I won’t let you do such a thing!

example_sentences.ja: 皆[みんな]、 今日[きょう] 行[い]くって、 知[し]ら んかった[;n] よ。
example_sentences.en: I didn’t know everybody was going today.

explanation:
There is yet another version of the negative verb conjugation and it uses 「ぬ」 instead of the 「ない」 that attaches to the end of the verb. While this version of the negative conjugation is old-fashioned and part of classical Japanese, you will still encounter it occasionally. In fact, I just saw this conjugation on a sign at the train station today, so it’s not too uncommon.

For any verb, you can replace 「ない」 with 「ぬ」 to get to an old-fashion sounding version of the negative. Similar to the last section, 「する」 becomes 「せぬ」 and 「くる」 becomes 「こぬ」. You may hear this grammar being used from older people or your friends if they want to bring back ye olde days.
primary_text: 韓国人[かんこくじん]と 結婚[けっこん]してはなら ぬ[;n] ！
meaning: You must not marry a Korean!
item_key: grammar:韓国人と結婚してはならぬ!

example_sentences.ja: 模擬[もぎ] 試験[しけん]に 何回[なんかい]も 失敗[しっぱい]して、 実際[じっさい]に 受[う]けてみたら 思[おも]わ ぬ[;n] 結果[けっか]が 出[で]た。
example_sentences.en: After having failed mock examination any number of times, a result I wouldn’t have thought came out when I actually tried taking the test.

explanation: The noun 「わけ」（訳） is a bit difficult to describe but it’s defined as: “meaning; reason; can be deduced”.
primary_text:
直子[なおこ]「いくら 英語[えいご]を 勉強[べんきょう]しても、うまくならないの。」
ジム「つまり、 語学[ごがく]には、 能力[のうりょく]がないという 訳[わけ;n] か。」
直子[なおこ]「 失礼[しつれい]ね。」
meaning:
Naoko: No matter how much I study, I don’t become better at English.
Jim: So basically, it means that you don’t have ability at language.
Naoko: How rude.
item_key: grammar:直子「いくら英語を勉強しても、うまくならないの。」ジム「つまり、語学には、能力がないという訳か。」直子「失礼ね。」

example_sentences.ja: 中国語[ちゅうごくご]が 読[よ]める わけがない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: There’s no way I can read Chinese. (lit: There is no reasoning for [me] to be able to read Chinese.)

example_sentences.ja:
直子[なおこ]「 広子[ひろこ]の 家[いえ]に 行[い]ったことある？」
一郎[いちろう]「ある わけない[;n] でしょう。」
example_sentences.en:
Naoko: Have you ever gone to Hiroko’s house?
Ichirou: There’s no way I would have ever gone to her house, right?

example_sentences.ja:
直子[なおこ]「 微積分[びせきぶん]は 分[わ]かる？」
一郎[いちろう]「 分[わ]かる わけない[;n] よ！」
example_sentences.en:
Naoko: Do you understand (differential and integral) calculus?
Ichirou: There’s no way I would understand!

explanation: There is one thing to be careful of because 「わけない」 can also mean that something is very easy (lit: requires no explanation). You can easily tell when this meaning is intended however, because it is used in the same manner as an adjective.
primary_text: ここの 試験[しけん]に 合格[ごうかく]するのは わけない[;n] 。
meaning: It’s easy to pass the tests here.
item_key: grammar:ここの試験に合格するのはわけない。

explanation: Finally, although not as common, 「わけ」 can also be used as a formal expression for saying that something must or must not be done at all costs. This is simply a stronger and more formal version of 「～てはいけない」. This grammar is created by simply attaching 「わけにはいかない」. The 「は」 is the topic particle and is pronounced 「わ」. The reason 「いけない」 changes to 「いかない」 is probably related to intransitive and transitive verbs but I don’t want to get too caught up in the logistics of it. Just take note that it’s 「いかない」 in this case and not 「いけない」.
primary_text: 今度[こんど]は 負[ま]ける わけにはいかない[;n] 。
meaning: This time, I must not lose at all costs.
item_key: grammar:今度は負けるわけにはいかない。

example_sentences.ja: ここまできて、あきらめる わけにはいかない[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: After coming this far, I must not give up.

explanation: While this next grammar doesn’t necessarily have anything directly related to the previous grammar, I thought it would fit nicely together. In a previous lesson, we learn how to combine the volitional form with 「とする」 to indicate an attempt to perform an action. We will now learn several other ways 「とする」 can be used. It may help to keep in mind that 「とする」 is really just a combination of the quotation particle 「と」 and the verb 「する」 meaning “to do”. Let’s say you have a sentence: [verb]とする. This means literally that you are doing like “[verb]” (in quotes). As you can see, when used with the volitional, it becomes: “Doing like making motion to do [verb]”. In other words, you are acting as if to make a motion to do [verb]. As we have already seen, this translates to “attempt to do [verb]”. Let’s see what happens when we use it on plain verbs.
primary_text: 明日[あした]に 行[い]く とする[;n] 。
meaning: Assume we go tomorrow.

example_sentences.ja: 今[いま]から 行[い]く としたら[;n] 、９ 時[じ]に 着[つ]くと 思[おも]います。
example_sentences.en: If we suppose that we go from now, I think we will arrive at 9:00.

example_sentences.ja: 観客[かんきゃく] として[;n] 参加[さんか]させてもらった。
example_sentences.en: Received favor of allowing to participate as spectator.

example_sentences.ja: 被害者[ひがいしゃ] として[;n] は、 非常[ひじょう]に 幸[さいわ]いだった。
example_sentences.en: As a victim, was extremely fortunate.

example_sentences.ja: 朝[あさ]ご 飯[はん]を 食[た]べた として[;n] も、もう 昼[ひる]だからお 腹[なか]が 空[す]いたでしょう。
example_sentences.en: Even assuming that you ate breakfast, because it’s already noon, you’re probably hungry, right?

explanation:
This is a very useful grammar that is used to indicate that one has just finished doing something. For instance, the first time I really wished I knew how to say something like this was when I wanted to politely decline an invitation to eat because I had just eaten. To do this, take the past tense of verb that you want to indicate as just being completed and add 「ばかり」. This is used with only the past tense of verbs and is not to be confused with the 「ばかり」 used with nouns to express amounts.

Just like the other type of 「ばかり」 we have covered before, in slang, you can hear people use 「ばっか」 instead of 「ばかり」.
primary_text: すみません、 今[いま] 食[た]べた ばかり[;n] なので、お 腹[なか]がいっぱいです。
meaning: Sorry, but I’m full having just eaten.
item_key: grammar:すみません、今食べたばかりなので、お腹がいっぱいです。

example_sentences.ja: １０キロを 走[はし]った ばかり[;n] で、 凄[すご]く 疲[つか]れた。
example_sentences.en: I just ran 10 kilometers and am really tired.

example_sentences.ja: 今[いま]、 家[いえ]に 帰[かえ]った ばかり[;n] です。
example_sentences.en: I got back home just now.

example_sentences.ja: 昼[ひる]ご 飯[はん]を 食[た]べた ばっか[;n] なのに、もうお 腹[なか]が 空[ｓ]いた。
example_sentences.en: Despite the fact that I just ate lunch, I’m hungry already.

example_sentences.ja: まさか、 今[いま] 起[お]きた ばっか[;n] なの？
example_sentences.en: No way, did you wake up just now?

explanation:
Kind of as a supplement to 「ばかり」, we will cover one way to say something happened as soon as something else occurs. To use this grammar, add 「とたん」 to the past tense of the first action that happened. It is also common to add the 「に」 target particle to indicate that specific point in time.

※Note: You can only use this grammar for things that happen outside your control.
primary_text: 窓[まど]を 開[あ]けた とたん[;n] に、 猫[ねこ]が 跳[と]んでいった。
meaning: As soon as I opened window, cat jumped out.
item_key: grammar:窓を開けたとたんに、猫が跳んでいった。

example_sentences.ja: 映画[えいが]を 観[み]た とたん[;n] に、 眠[ねむ]くなりました。
example_sentences.en: As soon as I watched the movie, I got sleepy.

explanation: You can use 「ながら」 to express that one action is taking place in conjunction with another action. To use 「ながら」, you must change the first verb to the stem and append 「ながら」. Though probably rare, you can also attach 「ながら」 to the negative of the verb to express the negative. This grammar has no tense since it is determined by the second verb.
primary_text: テレビを 観[み] ながら[;n] 、 宿題[しゅくだい]をする。
meaning: Do homework while watching TV.
item_key: grammar:テレビを観ながら、宿題をする。

example_sentences.ja: 音楽[おんがく]を 聴[き]き ながら[;n] 、 学校[がっこう]へ 歩[ある]くのが 好[す]き。
example_sentences.en: Like to walk to school while listening to music.

example_sentences.ja: 相手[あいて]に 何[なに]も 言[い]わない ながら[;n] 、 自分[じぶん]の 気持[きも]ちをわかってほしいのは 単[たん]なるわがままだと 思[おも]わない？
example_sentences.en: Don’t you think that wanting the other person to understand one’s feelings while not saying anything is just simply selfishness?

example_sentences.ja: ポップコーンを 食[た]べ ながら[;n] 、 映画[えいが]を 観[み]る。
example_sentences.en: Watch movie while eating popcorn.

example_sentences.ja: ポップコーンを 食[た]べ ながら[;n] 、 映画[えいが]を 観[み]た。
example_sentences.en: Watched movie while eating popcorn.

example_sentences.ja: 口笛[くちぶえ]を しながら[;n] 、 手紙[てがみ]を 書[か]いていた。
example_sentences.en: Was writing letter while whistling.

explanation:
A more advanced use of 「ながら」 is to use it with the implied state-of-being. In other words, you can use it with nouns or adjectives to talk about what something is while something else. The implied state-of-being means that you must not use the declarative 「だ」, you just attach 「ながら」 to the noun or adjective. For example, a common way this grammar is used is to say, “While it’s unfortunate, something something…” In Japanese, this would become 「残念ながら・・・」

You can also attach the inclusive 「も」 particle to 「ながら」 to get 「ながらも」. This changes the meaning from “while” to “even while”.
primary_text: 仕事[しごと]がいっぱい 入[はい]って、 残念[ざんねん] ながら[;n] 、 今日[きょう]は 行[い]けなくなりました。
meaning: While it’s unfortunate, a lot of work came in and it became so that I can’t go today.
item_key: grammar:仕事がいっぱい入って、残念ながら、今日は行けなくなりました。

example_sentences.ja: 貧乏[びんぼう] ながら[;n] も、 高級[こうきゅう]なバッグを 買[か]っちゃったよ。
example_sentences.en: Even while I’m poor, I ended up buying a high quality bag.

example_sentences.ja: 彼[かれ]は、 初心者[しょしんしゃ] ながら[;n] も、 実力[じつりょく]はプロと 同[おな]じだ。
example_sentences.en: Even while he is a beginner, his actual skills are the same as a pro.

explanation: The WWWJDIC very succinctly defines the definition of this verb as a “verb suffix to indicate reckless abandon to the activity”. Unfortunately, it doesn’t go on to tell you exactly how it’s actually used. Actually, there’s not much to explain. You take the stem of the verb and simply attach 「まくる」. However, since this is a continuing activity, it is an enduring state unless you’re going to do it in the future. This is a very casual expression.
primary_text: ゲームにはまっちゃって、 最近[さいきん]パソコンを 使[つか]い まくっている[;n] よ。
meaning: Having gotten hooked by games, I do nothing but use the computer lately.
item_key: grammar:ゲームにはまっちゃって、最近パソコンを使いまくっているよ。

example_sentences.ja: アメリカにいた 時[とき]はコーラを 飲[の]み まくっていた[;n] 。
example_sentences.en: When I was in the US, I drank coke like all the time.

explanation: 「まま」, not to be confused with the childish expression for “mother” （ママ）, is a grammatical phrase to express a lack of change in something. Grammatically, it is used just like a regular noun. You’ll most likely hear this grammar at a convenience store when you buy a very small item. Since store clerks use super polite expressions and at lightning fast speeds, learning this one expression will help you out a bit in advance. (Of course, upon showing a lack of comprehension, the person usually repeats the exact same phrase… at the exact same speed.)
primary_text: この まま[;n] で 宜[よろ]しいですか？
meaning: Is it ok just like this?
item_key: grammar:このままで宜しいですか?

example_sentences.ja: 半分[はんぶん]しか 食[た]べてない まま[;n] で 捨[す]てちゃダメ！
example_sentences.en: You can’t throw it out leaving it in that half-eaten condition!

example_sentences.ja: 今日[きょう]だけは 悲[かな]しい まま[;n] でいさせてほしい。
example_sentences.en: For only today, I want you to let me stay in this sad condition.

example_sentences.ja: その 格好[かっこう]の まま[;n] でクラブに 入[はい]れないよ。
example_sentences.en: You can’t get in the club in that getup (without changing it).

explanation:
Using 「っぱなし」 to leave something the way it is

The verb 「放す」 meaning “to set loose”, can be used in various ways in regards to leaving something the way it is. For instance, a variation 「放っとく」 is used when you want to say “Leave me alone”. For instance, you might use the command form of a request （くれる） and say, 「ほっといてくれ！」(Leave me alone!). Yet another variant 「ほったらかす」 means “to neglect”.

The grammar I would like to discuss here is the 「っぱなし」 suffix variant. You can attach this suffix to the stem of any verb to describe the act of doing something and leaving it that way without changing it. You can treat the combination like a regular noun.
primary_text: テレビをつけ っぱなし[;n] にしなければ 眠[ねむ]れない 人[ひと]は、 結構[けっこう]いる。
meaning: There exists a fair number of people who cannot sleep unless they turn on the TV and leave it that way.
item_key: grammar:テレビをつけっぱなしにしなければ眠れない人は、結構いる。

example_sentences.ja: 窓[まど]が 開[あ]け っ[;n] 放[ぱな;n]し だったので、 蚊[か]がいっぱい 入[はい]ってしまった。
example_sentences.en: The window was left wide open so a lot of mosquitoes got in.

primary_text:
２年間日本に住んでいたから、日本語が
話せる
ようになりました

。
explanation:
(I) lived in Japan for two years so (I) 
became 
able to speak Japanese.

話

はな

せる - 

to be able to speak

to be able to talk

Achieving an action

If you use the same grammar as before but with the verb 「なる」 (meaning “to become”) instead of 「する」, we can describe reaching the state of the verb.

Clerk: There is (a) children’s 
clothing 
section on the 4th floor but…

子供服 【こ・ども・ふく】 – children’s clothing

５００円

しか

持っていません。

(I) 
only 
have 500 yen.

primary_text:
５時に
会っ
たらどう

？
explanation:
How about if 
(we) meet at 5:00?

Another very similar pattern is to use the 「たら」 conditional to ask, “how about if”.

primary_text:
９時に

行けばいい

の？
explanation:
I 
should 
go at 9 o’clock?
lit: If (I) go at 9 o’clock, 
is (it) good
?

Asking for suggestions

We just learned how to ask if it’s better to do one thing by using a comparison with 「いい」. We can also ask for suggestions on what to do by using the conditional and 「いい」 as shown in the examples below.

primary_text:
9日には、アメリカの
軍
の「
海兵隊
」も
出す
と言いました。
explanation:
On the 9th, they also said they would 
send 
out the 
U.S. Marine Corps
.

9日（ここのか）– the 9th (of the month)

には – time/location particle (emphasis)

アメリカ – America

の – possessive particle

軍（ぐん）– military

の – possessive particle

海兵隊（かいへいたい）– Marine Corps

も – also

出す（だす）– to send out

と – quotation particle

言いました（いいました）– said

primary_text: Are consecutive nouns allowed in Japanese?
explanation: Yes, when not meant to modify each other. Some common noun combination are considered as their own words.

example_sentences.ja: 登場人物 - No Entry (entry prohibition)

primary_text: How does a na-adjective modify a noun for non-past positive form?
explanation: na-adj + な + noun

example_sentences.ja:
静かな人。- Quiet person.
きれいな人。- Pretty person.

primary_text: How does a na-adjective modify a noun when used in past and/or negative form?
explanation: na-adjective + same as noun conjugation + noun

example_sentences.ja:
魚が
好きな
人。

Person that likes fish.

魚が
好きじゃない
人。

Person that does not like fish.

魚が
好きだった
人。

Person that liked fish.

魚が
好きじゃなかった
人。

Person that did not like fish.

primary_text: How to add an explanatory tone to a state of being clause for na-adjective or nouns?
explanation: Using なの or more colloquially just なん

primary_text: How to add an explanatory tone to sentence?
explanation: Using the「の」particle in the end

example_sentences.ja:
今は忙しい
の
。

The thing is that (I’m) busy now.

primary_text: How to change a ru-verb from dictionary form to past tense?
explanation: Drop the「る」part of the ru-verb and add「た」

example_sentences.ja:
出る → 出た 
捨てる → 捨てた

primary_text: How to combine nouns inclusively with the nuance that there may be more unlisted things, and some nouns listed may actually not apply? i.e. how to say "noun and/or noun and/or others"? (2 options)
explanation: Using the vauge listing particle「や」or the word「とか」(more colloquially)

example_sentences.ja:
飲み物やカップやナプキンは、いらない？
 You don’t need (things like) drink, cup, or napkin, etc.? 

靴やシャツを買う。
 Buy (things like) shoes and shirt, etc…

primary_text: How to combine the よ and ね particles together?
explanation: Only in the order「よね」

example_sentences.ja:
アリス：ボブは、魚が好きなんだよね。
 Alice: You know, you like fish, dontcha?

primary_text: How to combine two nouns inclusively, i.e. how to say "noun and noun"
explanation: Using the「と」particle

example_sentences.ja:
ナイフとフォークでステーキを食べた。
 Ate steak by means of knife and fork. 

本と雑誌と葉書を買った。
 Bought book, magazine, and post card.

primary_text: How to conjugate an i-adjective in non-past negative form?
explanation: i-adjective without trailing い + くない + noun

example_sentences.ja: 高くないビル。Not tall building.

primary_text: How to conjugate an i-adjective in past form?
explanation: i-adjective without trailing い + かった + noun

example_sentences.ja: 高かったビル。Building that was tall.

primary_text: How to conjugate an i-adjective in past negative form?
explanation: i-adjective without trailing い + くなかった + noun

example_sentences.ja: 高くなかったビル。Building that was not tall.

primary_text: How to conjugate an i-adjective in positive non-past form?
explanation: i-adj + noun

example_sentences.ja: 高いビル。Tall building.

primary_text: How to conjugate any verb into past negative form?
explanation: Change the verb to the negative and replace the 「い」 with 「かった」

example_sentences.ja:
捨てる → 捨てない → 捨てなかった
行く → 行かない → 行かなかった

primary_text: How to conjugate u-vebrs that end in く,ぐ into past tense?
explanation:
く→いた
 ぐ→いだ

example_sentences.ja:
書く→書いた
 泳ぐ→泳いだ

primary_text: How to conjugate u-vebrs that end in す into past tense?
explanation: す→した

example_sentences.ja: 話す→話した

primary_text: How to conjugate u-vebrs that end in む, ぶ, ぬ into past tense?
explanation: {む, ぶ, ぬ}→んだ

example_sentences.ja:
飲む→飲んだ
 遊ぶ→遊んだ
 死ぬ→死んだ

primary_text: How to conjugate u-vebrs that end in る, う, つ into past tense?
explanation: {る, う, つ}→った

example_sentences.ja:
切る→切った
 買う→買った
 持つ→持った

primary_text: How to express past negative state of being (was not) for nouns and na-adjectives?
explanation: noun/na-adj + じゃなかった

example_sentences.ja:
人じゃなかった - was not person
学生じゃなかった - was not student
元気じゃなかった - was not well

primary_text: How to express past state of being (was) for nouns and na-adjectives?
explanation: noun/na-adj + だった

example_sentences.ja:
人だった - was person
学生だった - was student
元気だった - was well

primary_text: How to express present (non-past) negative state of being (is not) for nouns and na-adjectives?
explanation: noun/na-adj + じゃない

example_sentences.ja:
人じゃない - is not person
学生じゃない - is not student
元気じゃない - is not well

primary_text: How to express present (non-past) state of being (is, will be) for nouns and na-adjectives?
explanation:
noun/na-adj + だ
.
 The だ could also be emmited but with it the sentence is more forcefull\declarative.

example_sentences.ja:
人だ - is person
学生だ - is student
元気だ - is well

explanation: Using the「の」particle or the word「物」

は、かわいい。

Thing that is white is cute.

白い
物
は、かわいい。

explanation: Using the「の」particle

primary_text: How to indicate that an action was done together with something or someone?
explanation: Using the「と」particle

example_sentences.ja:

 Talked with friend.

primary_text: How to indicate that we are informing the listener of something new?
explanation: By adding the particle「よ」to the end of the sentence

ボブ：大丈夫だよ。

primary_text: How to indicate that we are looking for agreement\affirmation to what we said in a sentence? (like saying "isn't it?" in English)
explanation: By adding the「ね」particle to the end of the sentence

Alice: That is so, isn’t it?

primary_text: How to negate u-verbs that don't end with「う」?
explanation: Replace the u-vowel sound with the a-vowel equivalent and attach 「ない」

待つ →　待たない

primary_text: How to negate u-verbs that end with「う」?
explanation: Replace「う」with「わ」and add「ない」

primary_text: How to recognize an i-adjective and a na-adjective (generally speaking)?
explanation: All i-adjectives always end in the Hiragana character: 「い」. Note that it has explicit and not implicit within the kanji. If the kanji ends with the「い」sound but there is no 「い」at the end it wouldn't be a i-adjective.

example_sentences.ja:
i-adjectives:
高い, いい, かっこいい, 楽しい
na-adjectives:
親切, きれい, 好き

primary_text: How to say that an action was done "from" something?
explanation: Using the word「から」

example_sentences.ja:
アリスは、アメリカからきた。Alice came from America.

primary_text: How to specify direct object of a verb?
explanation: Using the「を」particle.

example_sentences.ja:

ジュースを飲んだ。Drank juice.

primary_text: How to specify the context in which a verb is performed? For example location of action, means of action
explanation: Using the「で」particle

example_sentences.ja:

バスで帰る。Go home by bus.

primary_text: How to specify the direction towards which a verb is being done?
explanation: Using the particle「へ」

example_sentences.ja:

家へ帰らない。Not go home toward house.
部屋へくる。Come towards room.

primary_text: How to specify the target of a verb? i.e. to specify when a verb does something toward and object.
explanation: Using the「に」particle.

example_sentences.ja:

家に帰らない。Not go back home.

いすが台所にあった。Chair was in the kitchen.
いい友達に会った。Met good friend.

primary_text: How to turn an i-adjective into an adverb?
explanation: Substitute the「い」with「く」

primary_text: How to turn an na-adjective into an adverb?
explanation: Attach the target particle「に」

primary_text: How to use a noun to modify another noun? e.g. "American college", "student of collage"

の
大学

primary_text: Ignoring exceptions, how to tell apart ru-verbs and u-verbs?
explanation:
If a verb doesn't end in「る」or if the vowel sound preceding the「る」is an /a/, /u/ or /o/ vowel sound, it will always be an u-verb.
It most other cases we have a ru-verb.

example_sentences.ja:

分かる – 「か」 is an a-vowel sound so it is an u-verb

primary_text:
あのペンを

使ってください

。
explanation:
Please use that pen.

Using 「ください」 to make a firm request

「ください」（下さい） is a polite way to make a firm request for something. It can also be used with the te-form of a verb to request an action. It can be written in either Kanji or Hiragana though it’s more common to use Hiragana when combined with a verb.
item_key: grammar:あのペンを使ってください。

primary_text:
あのペンを
使って
ちょうだい

。
explanation:
Use that pen.

Using 「ちょうだい」 for casual requests

「ちょうだい」 can be used instead of 「ください」 for casual speech. While 「ちょうだい」 can be used by anyone, it does have a slight feminine and childish nuance.
item_key: grammar:あのペンを使ってちょうだい。

primary_text:
あの子はとてもしっかりしていて、子供

らしくない

です。
explanation:
That child is very reliable and doesn’t act 
like 
a child.

あの – that (over there) （abbr. of あれの）

子 【こ】 – child

とても – very

しっかり – firmly; reliable; steady;

する (exception) – to do

子供 【こ・ども】 – child

Appearance from hearsay or behavior

「らしい」 is another grammatical expression that expresses either hearsay or behavior. When used to express hearsay, unlike 「～そう」 from the previous section, it can be used to express impressions from non-specific hearsay rather than something specific that was said. Simply attach 「らしい」 to the end of the clause to express hearsay or behavior. It conjugates just like a regular i-adjective.
item_key: grammar:あの子はとてもしっかりしていて、子供らしくないです。

primary_text:
あの方にホテルの

行き方

を教えないんですか。
explanation:
(Are you) not going to teach (tell) that person the 
way to go
 to the hotel?

方 【かた】 – person (honorific)

～方 【～かた】 – way of doing ~

ホテル – hotel

教える 【おし・える】 (ru-verb) – to teach; to inform

Directions

Using 「方」（かた） for directions

In the last section, we learned how to use 「方」 to make comparison. We can also use 「方」 to describe how to do something. This is done by attaching 「方」 to the verb stem. However, in this usage, the reading is 「かた」 not 「ほう」. The result is used as a regular noun (it may help to translate it as “way of doing…”).

In addition, 「方」（かた） is also used to refer to a person politely.
item_key: grammar:あの方にホテルの行き方を教えないんですか。

Slang expression for similarity

A casual way to express similarity is to attach 「っぽい」 to the word that reflects the resemblance. primary_text:
おすしや中華料理がはやっている今では、おはしの

使い方

が分かるアメリカ人は少なくない。
explanation:
Given now where things like sushi and Chinese food is common, Americans who know 
how to 
use chopsticks is not few (in number).

分かる 【わ・かる】 (u-verb) – to understand

（お）すし – sushi

中華料理 【ちゅう・か・りょう・り】 – Chinese food

はやる (u-verb) – to be popular, to come into fashion;

今 【いま】 – now

（お）はし – chopsticks

使う 【つか・う】 (u-verb) – to use

アメリカ人 【あめりか・じん】 – American (person)

少ない 【すく・ない】 (i-adj) – few (in numbers)

Directions

Using 「方」（かた） for directions

In the last section, we learned how to use 「方」 to make comparison. We can also use 「方」 to describe how to do something. This is done by attaching 「方」 to the verb stem. However, in this usage, the reading is 「かた」 not 「ほう」. The result is used as a regular noun (it may help to translate it as “way of doing…”).

In addition, 「方」（かた） is also used to refer to a person politely.
item_key: grammar:おすしや中華料理がはやっている今では、おはしの使い方が分かるアメリカ人は少なくない。

primary_text:
お金がないから、昼ご飯を
お
ごってくれる

？
explanation:
(I) don’t have money so will (you) 
treat me
 to lunch for me?

お金 (おかね)
 – "money"

が
 – subject particle (marks “お金” as the subject)

ない
 – "there isn’t / I don’t have" (negative of ある)

から
 – "because" (gives the reason casually)

昼ご飯 (ひるごはん)
 – "lunch"

を
 – object particle (marks “昼ご飯” as the object)

おごって
 – te-form of 
おごる
, "to treat (someone to food)"

くれる
 – "to do something for me" (indicates the speaker is the recipient of the action)
item_key: grammar:お金がないから、昼ご飯をおごってくれる?

primary_text:
カレーは

どうやって

作りますか。
explanation:
How do
 (you)
 go about
 making curry?

Using 「どうやって」 for instructions

Another way to describe how to do something is by using the phrase 「どうやって」. 「やる」 is a more casual version of the verb 「する」 (“to do”) so the phrase literally means “how do and”. It’s used just like the regular te-form to express a sequence of actions as we learned in the last chapter. Because 「どうして」 also means “why”, 「どうやって」 is more common.
item_key: grammar:カレーはどうやって作りますか。

primary_text:
ここで携帯を使うのは

だめ

？
explanation:
Is using cell phone here bad?

Things that one must not do

Things that one must 
not
 do are expressed by using one of the three words: 「いけない」、「ならない」、 and 「だめ」. These are all negative expressions (the first two is actually using the negative form) meaning that something won’t do or is no good. Conjugating these expressions are simple if we know where they originate from.

primary_text:
これが欲しいなら、

買ってあげる

よ。
explanation:
If (you) want this, (I will) buy (it) for you, you know.

Doing favors for others

We can use the three words we just learned for giving and receiving with other verbs to express the action as a favor. This construction is used to make requests and do things for others.

Giving and receiving favors

In order to use one of the three words we learned for giving and receiving with another verb, first change the verb to the te-form and then attach the word for giving or receiving to the end of the verb

させられた

と言うけど、結局、それはあんたのためだったんでしょう？
explanation:
(You) say (you) were 
made to do
 (it) but in the end, (it) was for your benefit, right?

させられた
 – passive/causative form of 
する
 ("to do")

→ 
させる
 = “to make someone do,”

→ 
させられる
 = “to be made to do” (passive/causative)

→ 
させられた
 = “was made to do”

と言う (という)
 – “say that…”

けど
 – "but" (casual conjunction)

結局 (けっきょく)
 – "after all," "in the end"

それ
 – "that" (refers to the action previously mentioned)

は
 – topic particle

あんた
 – "you" (casual, often blunt or direct)

のため
 – “for the sake of” / “for (your) benefit”

だった
 – past tense of 
だ
 (“was”)

んでしょう？
 – contraction of 
の + でしょう？
, used to seek confirmation

→ “wasn’t it?” / “right?”

primary_text:
しまった

！宿題を持ってくるのを忘れた！
explanation:
Oh no!
 (I) forgot to bring (my) homework!

宿題（しゅくだい）
 – homework

持って来る（もってくる）
 – to bring

忘れた（わすれた）
 – forgot

Using 「しまう」 for unintended actions

The verb 「しまう」 has two main definitions: 1) to finish; to do something completely and 2) for something unintended to happen. The latter definition is used for situations when you did something you didn’t mean to do. To use this with other verbs, attach 「しまう」 to the te-form of the verb.

Asking for suggestions

We just learned how to ask if it’s better to do one thing by using a comparison with 「いい」. We can also ask for suggestions on what to do by using the conditional and 「いい」 as shown in the examples below.

primary_text:
それは

いけません

ね。
explanation:
That’s 
wrong/bad/no good
.

Things that one must not do

Things that one must 
not
 do are expressed by using one of the three words: 「いけない」、「ならない」、 and 「だめ」. These are all negative expressions (the first two is actually using the negative form) meaning that something won’t do or is no good. Conjugating these expressions are simple if we know where they originate from.

いける (ru-verb) – can work; can make it; lit: can go （potential form of 「行く」）

なる (u-verb) – to become

駄目 【だ・め】 (na-adj) – no good

While we can use 「いけない」 and 「だめ」 by themselves as shown in the examples below 「ならない」 cannot be used by itself.

primary_text:
それは、あり

え

ないよ。
explanation:
That can never happen. (lit: That can’t exist.)

Another verb to pay attention to is 「ある」, which cannot be conjugated to the potential form. Instead, to express that something can exist, you must use the verb 「あり得る」. This verb is very curious in that 「得」 can either be read as 「う」 or 「え」 but if conjugated, it must always be read as 「え」. You may wonder how often one talks about the ability to exist. In practice, this word is primarily used to describe whether a situation or event （こと） can occur.

primary_text:
それは、あり
え

る
ね。それは、あり

う

るね。
explanation:
That could happen. (lit: That can exist.)

Another verb to pay attention to is 「ある」, which cannot be conjugated to the potential form. Instead, to express that something can exist, you must use the verb 「あり得る」. This verb is very curious in that 「得」 can either be read as 「う」 or 「え」 but if conjugated, it must always be read as 「え」. You may wonder how often one talks about the ability to exist. In practice, this word is primarily used to describe whether a situation or event （こと） can occur.
item_key: grammar:それは、ありえるね。それは、ありうるね。

primary_text:
そんなにたくさん
食べる
と

絶対太るよ。
explanation:
If 
(you) eat that much, (you’ll) get fat for sure.

絶

対(

ぜったい) - definitely

太

ふと

る - gain weight/ get fat

Natural consequence

The natural conditional is used by appending 「と」 for verbs and i-adjectives or 「だと」 for nouns and na-adjectives.

This conditional is used to describe things that happen as a natural consequence with very high certainty ie, “If you do X, Y will certainly happen.” It can also be translated as “when” in addition to “if”.

Natural conditional usage rule

Append 「と」 to the noun, verb, or adjective
Example

For nouns/na-adjectives:
 Append 「だと」
Example

友達＋
だと
 = 友達だと

静か＋
だと
 = 静かだと

For verbs/i-adjectives and negatives ending in 「ない」:
 Append 「と」
Example

する＋
と
 = すると

しない＋
と
 = しないと

忙しい＋
と
 = 忙しいと

primary_text:
タバコを
吸わない
ようにする

。
explanation:
Try not to
 smoke cigarettes.
(lit: Do toward manner of not smoking.)

Striving for a goal

In order to express “try” as striving toward a goal, we use the same 「よう」 we learned in the last section to describe the manner or appearance of the way we want to act. In this case, we use the verb 「する」 (meaning “to do”) and the 「に」 target particle to do toward the manner or appearance of the verb clause.
item_key: grammar:タバコを吸わないようにする。

primary_text:
タバコを吸わない

ようになる

。
explanation:
(It) 
became so that
 (I) don’t smoke.
(lit: Become manner of not smoking.)

Achieving an action

If you use the same grammar as before but with the verb 「なる」 (meaning “to become”) instead of 「する」, we can describe reaching the state of the verb.

primary_text:
ちゃんと約束時間を
守り
なさい

よ。
explanation:
Please properly keep the promised time.

ちゃんと
 – properly, reliably, as expected

約束時間 (やくそくじかん)
 – the promised/agreed time

約束 (やくそく)
 – promise, appointment

時間 (じかん)
 – time

守りなさい (まもりなさい)
 – imperative form of 
守る
 (“to keep,” “to obey,” “to protect”) → “keep (it)!” / “follow (it)!”

Using 「～なさい」 for polite command

「～なさい」 is the “polite” way to tell others what to do. It’s used by attaching 「なさい」 to the verb stem.

It’s polite grammatically but it doesn’t change the fact that you’re ordering others around, which is not very polite generally. It’s most commonly used by parents or other authority figures toward children.
item_key: grammar:ちゃんと約束時間を守りなさいよ。

よ。

トランプ大統領（トランプだいとうりょう）– President Trump

アメリカ – America

に – location particle (at/in)

いる – to be (for living things)

資格（しかく）– qualification, legal status

が – subject particle

ない – not exist, not have

ほかの – other

国（くに）– country

から – from

来た（きた）– came

人たち（ひとたち）– people

に – target particle (toward)

厳しく（きびしく）– strictly

しています – is doing

primary_text:
バスは、7時に出るから、5時に

起きれる

？
explanation:
Bus is leaving at 7 so (are you) 
able to wake up
 at 5?

バス – bus

～時 【～じ】 – hour counter

出る 【で・る】(ru-verb) – to come out

起きる 【お・きる】 (ru-verb) – to occur; to awake

For ru-verbs, you can drop the 「ら」 from 「
ら
れる」. For example, the potential form 「食べる」 can also be expressed as 「食べれる」 instead of 「食べられる」. However, you should practice with the full conjugation as the shorter form is more casual.
item_key: grammar:バスは、7時に出るから、5時に起きれる?

primary_text:
パソコンが古くて遅いから、動画が
見られない
。
explanation:
Computer is old and slow so not able to see video. (lit: Because computer is old and slow, not capable of seeing video.

パソコン – PC, computer

古い 【ふる・い】(i-adj) – old

遅い 【おそ・い】(i-adj) – late; slow

動画 【どう・が】 – video

見える 【み・える】 – to be visible

The potential form of 「見る」 and 「聞く」 （「見られる」 and 「聞ける」 respectively） are only used to describe the ability to see and hear, not whether something is visible or audible. Japanese has two separate verbs to describe the latter: 「見える」 and 「聞こえる」. The examples below show the difference between the ability or capability to see/hear versus what is visible/audible.
item_key: grammar:パソコンが古くて遅いから、動画が見られない。

primary_text:
ひざが痛いですけど、病院に

行った方がいい

ですか。
explanation:
(My) knee hurts but 
is (it) better to go
 to the hospital? (should I go to the hospital?)

ひざ
 – "knee"

が
 – subject particle (marks “ひざ” as the thing that hurts)

痛い (いたい)
 – "painful," "hurts"

です
 – polite copula

けど
 – "but" / "though" (used here to connect thoughts or soften the tone)

病院 (びょういん)
 – "hospital"

に
 – direction particle (“to” a place)

行った (いった)
 – past tense of 行く (“to go”)

方がいい (ほうがいい)
 – “it’s better to…” / “you should…”

ですか
 – polite question ending
item_key: grammar:ひざが痛いですけど、病院に行った方がいいですか。

primary_text:
びっくり

させないで

よ。
explanation:
Don’t make me surprised. (Don’t scare me.)

Causative and passive verbs

Causative Verb Form

The causative form gets its name because it causes something to happen either by making or letting somebody do the action. While it may seem odd to have the exact same verb form for two very different uses, we’ll find that it’s not hard to tell which is intended when given the proper context. Below are the rules for conjugating a verb to the causative form. All verbs in the causative form are ru-verbs.

Rules for causative form conjugation

For ru-verbs:
 Replace the last 「る」 with 「させる」.
Example

食べる＋させる = 食べ
させる

出る＋させる = 出
させる

For u-verbs:
 Change the last character as you would for negative verbs but attach 「せる」 instead of 「ない」.
Example

飲む＋ま＋せる = 飲
ませる

待つ＋た＋せる = 持
たせる

買う＋わ＋せる = 買
わせる

Exceptions:

する → させる

くる → こさせる
item_key: grammar:びっくりさせないでよ。

primary_text:
まだ早いのにもう

帰らなければいけない
んです
か。
explanation:
Even though (it’s) still early, do (you) 

have to go home

?

What is 
なければいけない
?

This pattern expresses 
obligation
 or 
necessity
, like "must" or "have to."

You take the verb, put it into its 
negative conditional form
 (〜なければ),

then add 
いけない
, which literally means "won't do" or "not good,"

but together it expresses "must do.

また連絡するのを
忘れちまった
のかよ？
explanation:
Did (you) forget to contact (me) again?

連絡 れんらく - contact / communication

忘れ（る）わすれ（る）- to forget

ちまった

 - (casual) ended up (regret)

There is also a much less common casual version of 「～てしまう」 and 「～でしまう」 that sounds much more rough and coarse and is usually only used by older males. This version replaces 「～てしまう」 and 「～でしまう」 with 「～ちまう」 and 「～じまう」 respectively. The result is conjugated the same as any u-verb.

primary_text:
もう

起きてしまった

事は仕方ないよ。
explanation:
There’s nothing you can do about something that already happened (unintentionally).

もう
 – already

起きてしまった
 – has happened (with 
しまった
 expressing completion and/or regret)

事（こと）
 – thing / event

は
 – topic marker

仕方[しかた]ない – can’t be helped / nothing can be done

よ
 – adds emphasis or reassurance
item_key: grammar:もう起きてしまった事は仕方ないよ。

primary_text:
もう
注文
しちゃった

けど、どうする？
explanation:
(I) 
already ordered
 (oops) so what should (we) do?

注文（ちゅうもん ) 
"order"
 (as in placing an order for food, products, etc.)

Casual version of 「～てしまう」

In casual speech, the 「～てしまう」 can be substituted by 「～ちゃう」 while 「～でしまう」 is substituted by 「じゃう」. Both 「～ちゃう」 and 「～じゃう」 conjugate just like regular u-verbs.
item_key: grammar:もう注文しちゃったけど、どうする?

。
explanation:
Because (I’m) already (a) high school student, (I) 
couldn’t receive 
(the) New Year’s gift.

Using 「もらう」 to receive

There is only one word for receiving something so you don’t have to worry about which one to use.

primary_text:
ラジオもネットで
聞ける
の？
explanation:
(You) can listen to radio on (the) net as well? (lit: Able to hear radio on (the) net as well?)

聞こえる 【き・こえる】 – to be audible

The potential form of 「見る」 and 「聞く」 （「見られる」 and 「聞ける」 respectively） are only used to describe the ability to see and hear, not whether something is visible or audible. Japanese has two separate verbs to describe the latter: 「見える」 and 「聞こえる」. The examples below show the difference between the ability or capability to see/hear versus what is visible/audible.
item_key: grammar:ラジオもネットで聞けるの?

果たして 【は・たして】 – as was expected; really? (in questions)

謎 【なぞ】 – puzzle; riddle

解ける 【と・ける】 (ru-verb) – to be solved; to come untied

者 【もの】 – person

いる (ru-verb) – to exist (animate)

Expressing wonder/doubt

We can use 「でしょう」 or 「だろう」 with the question marker 「か」 to question whether something is actually true. This is often used to express doubt or reflection.

primary_text:
我慢ができないから
ついつい

買ってしまう

んですよ。
explanation:
(I) can’t resist so (I) 
unintentionally 

end up buying 
(it).

我慢[がまん]ができない– can’t endure/resist/control oneself

から
 – because

ついつい
 – unintentionally / without meaning to / reflexively

買ってしまう
 – end up buying (しまう adds the nuance of doing something completely, often with a sense of regret or inevitability)

んですよ
 – explanatory tone, often used to justify or explain something, with a soft or empathetic feel

Using 「しまう」 for unintended actions

The verb 「しまう」 has two main definitions: 1) to finish; to do something completely and 2) for something unintended to happen. The latter definition is used for situations when you did something you didn’t mean to do. To use this with other verbs, attach 「しまう」 to the te-form of the verb.
item_key: grammar:我慢ができないからついつい買ってしまうんですよ。

？
explanation:
(I) said won’t make it in time, didn’t I?

In casual situations, when expressed with a rising intonation, it is used to seek agreement similar to 「ね」. However, while 「ね」 is used for what the speaker believes to be generally agreeable, 「でしょう」 can be more assertive and opinionated. 「だろう」 is another more masculine version of the casual usage of 「でしょう」.

が猫

好き。
explanation:
Like dog more than cat. (lit: Like the 
side 
of dog
 rather than
 cat.)

方 【ほう】 – direction; side

～より (particle) – rather than ～

Comparisons

In order to make a comparison, you have to define either side of the comparison using 「方」（ほう） and/or 「より」. 「方」 defines the direction the comparison is leaning toward while 「より」 defines the side it’s leaning away from. The important thing to remember is that 「方」 is a noun while 「より」 is a particle. Another particle often used in making comparisons is 「ほど」, which describes the extent of something.

言っていることがいまいち分からないから、何を
してほしい
のかはっきり教えてよ。
explanation:
(I) don’t really understand what (you are) saying so please tell me what (you) want (me) to do clearly.

いまいち
 – not quite / not really / kind of unclear (colloquial, slightly vague feeling)

分からない (わからない)
 – “don’t understand”

してほしい
 – “want [you] to do” (literally: "[I] want [you] to do [something]")

はっきり
 – clearly / plainly

教えて (おしえて)
 – “tell me / teach me” (te-form of 
教える
)

Wanting others to do something

We can use the i-adjective 「欲しい」, which means “desired” or “wanted” with verbs to say you want somebody to do something. This is done by first conjugating the verb to the te-form, then attaching 「欲しい」（or ほしい） to the end of the verb. The result remains an i-adjective.

今から
行かな
いと

、電車に間に合わないよ。
explanation:
If 
(we) don’t go now starting now, (we) won’t make the train.

間

ま

に

合

あ

う - to be in time (for)

Natural consequence

The natural conditional is used by appending 「と」 for verbs and i-adjectives or 「だと」 for nouns and na-adjectives.

This conditional is used to describe things that happen as a natural consequence with very high certainty ie, “If you do X, Y will certainly happen.” It can also be translated as “when” in addition to “if”.

Natural conditional usage rule

Append 「と」 to the noun, verb, or adjective
Example

For nouns/na-adjectives:
 Append 「だと」
Example

友達＋
だと
 = 友達だと

静か＋
だと
 = 静かだと

For verbs/i-adjectives and negatives ending in 「ない」:
 Append 「と」
Example

する＋
と
 = すると

しない＋
と
 = しないと

忙しい＋
と
 = 忙しいと

Slang expression for similarity

A casual way to express similarity is to attach 「っぽい」 to the word that reflects the resemblance. Because this is a very casual expression, you can use it as a casual version for the different types of expression for similarity covered above （よう、みたい、らしい）. It conjugates just like a regular i-adjective.

とても – very

楽しい 【たの・しい】 (i-adj) – fun

Appearance from hearsay or behavior

「らしい」 is another grammatical expression that expresses either hearsay or behavior. When used to express hearsay, unlike 「～そう」 from the previous section, it can be used to express impressions from non-specific hearsay rather than something specific that was said. Simply attach 「らしい」 to the end of the clause to express hearsay or behavior. It conjugates just like a regular i-adjective.

Too much of something

An excess of something is expressed with the ru-verb 「過ぎる」（す・ぎる） which means, “to pass” or “to exceed”. There are several rules for attaching this verb to adjectives and other verbs. As 「すぎる」 is a regular ru-verb, all subsequent conjugations are the same as any other ru-verb.

Using 「あげる」 to give “upwards”

The word 「あげる」, which also means to “raise” is used when giving upwards to a person of a higher social status. The important thing to remember is that the speaker is 
always
 below everybody else. As a result, when the speaker is giving something to somebody else, he/she must always use 「あげる」. In other words, when you, yourself, is giving something, you must always use 「あげる」

However, while 「ね」 is used for what the speaker believes to be generally agreeable, 「でしょう」 can be more assertive and opinionated. 「だろう」 is another more masculine version of the casual usage of 「でしょう」.

General Conditional

The most generic conditional without any assumptions or embedded meanings is the 「～ば」 conditional. The conjugation rules for the 「ば」 conditional is below.

Conjugation rules for 「ば」

For verbs:
 change the last /u/ vowel sound to the /e/ vowel sound and append 「ば」
Example

食べる　→　食べ
れ
 → 食べれ＋
ば
 = 食べれば

待つ　→　待
て
 → 待て＋
ば
 = 待てば

する　→　す
れ
 → すれ＋
ば
 = すれば

For i-adjectives and negatives ending in 「ない」:
 drop the last 「い」 and append 「ければ」
Example

おいし
い
　→　おいし＋
ければ
 = おいしければ

食べな
い
　→　食べな＋
ければ
 = 食べなければ

な
い
　→　な＋
ければ
 = なければ

For nouns and na-adjectives
: append 「であれば」
Example

学生　→　学生
であれば

暇　→　暇
であれば

Appearance from hearsay or behavior

「らしい」 is another grammatical expression that expresses either hearsay or behavior. When used to express hearsay, unlike 「～そう」 from the previous section, it can be used to express impressions from non-specific hearsay rather than something specific that was said. Simply attach 「らしい」 to the end of the clause to express hearsay or behavior. It conjugates just like a regular i-adjective.

先から誰かに

見られている

気がしない？
explanation:
Doesn’t it feel like (we’re)
 being watch
 by someone from just a while ago?

先から (さきから)
 – "for a while now" / "since earlier"

誰か (だれか)
 – "someone"

に
 – particle marking the agent in a passive sentence → "by someone"

見られている
 – passive progressive form of 
見る
 (“to see/watch”) → “is being watched”

気 (き)
 – "feeling" / "sense"

が
 – subject particle (marks “気” as the subject)

しない
 – “don’t feel” / “don’t have” (negative form of 
する
)

？
 – turns the sentence into a question

Passive Verb Form

The passive form is used to change the verb into a passive voice. A verb in the passive form is always a ru-verb. The conjugation rule for ru-verbs is the same as the rule for potential form. However, it’s not really hard to tell them apart given enough context.

Rules for passive form conjugation

For ru-verbs:
 Replace the last 「る」 with 「られる」.
Example

食べる＋られる = 食べ
られる

出る＋られる = 出
られる

For u-verbs:
 Change the last character as you would for negative verbs but attach 「れる」 instead of 「ない」.
Example

飲む＋ま＋れる = 飲
まれる

待つ＋た＋れる = 持
たれる

買う＋わ＋れる = 買
われる

Exceptions:

する → される

くる → こられる

Another store may be cheaper.

Indicating a possibility

「かもしれない」 is another sentence ending that expresses a neutral possibility with about 50% level of confidence. It is simply the 「か」 and 「も」 particles combined with the potential negative form of 「知る」 (literally meaning “can’t know even if…”). This means that it conjugates just like any other negative ru-verb.

Don’t (you) hate being made to wait?

Using Causative and Passive together

The causative and passive conjunctions can be used together to describe being made to do something. The rules are simple, you simple need to conjugate the verb to the causative and then conjugate the resulting ru-verb to the passive form.

Will (I) find (a) good job

大学 【だい・がく】 – college

Consequences

Things of no consequence

The expressions “even if”, “regardless”, or “no matter” are used when something is of no consequence to something else. In Japanese, the same notion is expressed by combining the te-form with the 「も」 inclusive particle.

explanation:
Even if it’s 
late, it’s fine until next week.

Asking for permission

We can also ask for or grant permission by saying it’s ok or fine even if we do a certain action using the same 「～ても／～でも」 grammar.

primary_text:
着替え中ですから
入らないで
ください

。
explanation:
(I’m) changing so 
please 
don’t come in.

着替え中 (きがえちゅう)
 – "in the middle of changing clothes"

着替え (きがえ)
 – "changing clothes" (noun)

中 (ちゅう)
 – "in the middle of" (used to indicate an ongoing action)

です
 – polite copula (“is/am/are”)

から
 – "because" (giving a reason)

入らないで (はいらないで)
 – “don’t come in / don’t enter” (negative te-form of 入る)

ください
 – "please" (used with ないで to make a polite negative request)

Negative verb with 「ください」

In order to ask to not do something, take the negative of the word, attach 「で」, then attach 「ください」 similar to the rule we learned in the last section.
item_key: grammar:着替え中ですから入らないでください。

Experimenting

A third way to express trying (not as a goal or effort) is to do something as an experiment. For example, trying out something for the first time. This grammar is expressed by changing the verb to the te-form and attaching the verb 「みる」 (“to see”).

primary_text:
猫は、犬

ほど

好きじゃない。
explanation:
Don’t like cat as much as dog. (lit: Don’t like cat 
to extent of 
dog.)

～ほど (particle) – extent of ～

Comparisons

In order to make a comparison, you have to define either side of the comparison using 「方」（ほう） and/or 「より」. 「方」 defines the direction the comparison is leaning toward while 「より」 defines the side it’s leaning away from. The important thing to remember is that 「方」 is a noun while 「より」 is a particle. Another 
particle often used in making comparisons is 「ほど」, which describes the extent of something
.
item_key: grammar:猫は、犬ほど好きじゃない。

～な (sentence-ending particle) – casual and masculine version of ～ね

降る 【ふ・る】(u-verb) – to precipitate

雨が降り
そう
 – Seems like it’s going to rain

Guessing from observation

In English, “seems like” or “looks like” is also used to made an educated guess. In Japanese, this is expressed by appending 「そう」 to the verb or adjective with the following rules. The resulting word becomes a na-adjective.

primary_text:
彼の
奥さん
はすごく
かわいい
みたい

よ。
explanation:
His 
wife 
is 
apparently 
amazingly cute, you know.

彼【かれ】 – he; boyfriend

奥さん 【おく・さん】 – wife (polite)

凄い 【すご・い】 (i-adj) – to a great extent

かわいい (i-adj) – cute

Outward appearance (casual)

「よう」 may, at times, sound somewhat formal and stiff. For casual situations, you can substitute 「みたい」 for 「よう」 to describe what something/someone looks like or appears to be.

「みたい」 at first glance, looks identical to 「見たい」 meaning “want to see”. You could even say it has a similar meaning as an outward appearance is how one wants to see something. However, the key difference is that while all verbs in the 「～たい」 form such as 「見たい」 conjugate as an i-adjective, this 「みたい」 acts like a noun same as 「よう」.

If
 he’s free, (he) always plays game(s).

Natural consequence

The natural conditional is used by appending 「と」 for verbs and i-adjectives or 「だと」 for nouns and na-adjectives.

This conditional is used to describe things that happen as a natural consequence with very high certainty ie, “If you do X, Y will certainly happen.” It can also be translated as “when” in addition to “if”.

Expressing hearsay

In order to express what something appears to be based on what one heard from other people, we append 「そうだ」 (or 「そうです」) to the verb clause. At first glance, this grammar looks very similar to the previous grammar, however, the grammar rules are different. Also, this grammar must end in 「だ」 or 「です」 (for polite speech).

Passive Verb Form

The passive form is used to change the verb into a passive voice. A verb in the passive form is always a ru-verb. The conjugation rule for ru-verbs is the same as the rule for potential form. However, it’s not really hard to tell them apart given enough context.

Rules for passive form conjugation

For ru-verbs:
 Replace the last 「る」 with 「られる」.
Example

食べる＋られる = 食べ
られる

出る＋られる = 出
られる

For u-verbs:
 Change the last character as you would for negative verbs but attach 「れる」 instead of 「ない」.
Example

飲む＋ま＋れる = 飲
まれる

待つ＋た＋れる = 持
たれる

買う＋わ＋れる = 買
われる

Exceptions:

する → される

くる → こられる

primary_text:
無理矢理にお酒を

飲ませる

のはよくないよな。
explanation:
(It’s) not good to forcibly 
make 
(someone) 
drink 
alcohol, huh?

無理矢理に (むりやりに)
 – "by force," "against someone's will"

お酒 (おさけ)
 – "alcohol"

を
 – object particle (marks “お酒” as what’s being acted upon)

飲ませる (のませる)
 – causative form of 
飲む
 (“to drink”) → “to make/force someone drink”

のは
 – turns the verb phrase into a noun clause and marks it as the topic (“as for the act of…”)

よくない
 – "not good" (negative of よい / いい)

よな
 – sentence-ending particle expressing shared sentiment or confirmation ("right?" / "don’t you think?")

primary_text:
明日は
雨
みたい

だよ。
explanation:
Tomorrow 
looks like 
rain, you know.

明日 【あした】 – tomorrow

雨 【あめ】 – rain

Outward appearance (casual)

「よう」 may, at times, sound somewhat formal and stiff. For casual situations, you can substitute 「みたい」 for 「よう」 to describe what something/someone looks like or appears to be.

「みたい」 at first glance, looks identical to 「見たい」 meaning “want to see”. You could even say it has a similar meaning as an outward appearance is how one wants to see something. However, the key difference is that while all verbs in the 「～たい」 form such as 「見たい」 conjugate as an i-adjective, this 「みたい」 acts like a noun same as 「よう」.

primary_text:
明日は
忙しくな
ければ

、映画を見に行かない？
explanation:
If
 tomorrow is not busy, won’t (you) to go to watch movie?

General Conditional

The most generic conditional without any assumptions or embedded meanings is the 「～ば」 conditional. The conjugation rules for the 「ば」 conditional is below.

Conjugation rules for 「ば」

For verbs:
 change the last /u/ vowel sound to the /e/ vowel sound and append 「ば」
Example

食べる　→　食べ
れ
 → 食べれ＋
ば
 = 食べれば

待つ　→　待
て
 → 待て＋
ば
 = 待てば

する　→　す
れ
 → すれ＋
ば
 = すれば

For i-adjectives and negatives ending in 「ない」:
 drop the last 「い」 and append 「ければ」
Example

おいし
い
　→　おいし＋
ければ
 = おいしければ

食べな
い
　→　食べな＋
ければ
 = 食べなければ

な
い
　→　な＋
ければ
 = なければ

For nouns and na-adjectives
: append 「であれば」
Example

学生　→　学生
であれば

暇　→　暇
であれば
item_key: grammar:明日は忙しくなければ、映画を見に行かない?

Expressing wonder/doubt

We can use 「でしょう」 or 「だろう」 with the question marker 「か」 to question whether something is actually true. This is often used to express doubt or reflection.

明日は仕事だから、今日はあまり

飲めない

。
explanation:
Tomorrow is work so
 can’t drink
 very much today.

明日 【あした】 – tomorrow

仕事 【し・ごと】 – work

今日 【きょう】 – today

飲む 【の・む】(u-verb) – to drink

Potential Form

The potential form describes the feasibility of an action. The rules for changing a verb into the potential form is given below. All verbs in the potential form become ru-verbs.

Rules for conjugating to potential form

For ru-verbs:
 Replace the 「る」 with 「られる」
Example

食べる＋られる = 食べ
られる

出る＋られる = 出
られる

For u-verbs:
 Replace the u-vowel sound with the e-vowel equivalent and attach 「る」.
Example

飲む＋め＋る = 飲
める

持つ＋て＋る = 持
てる

Exceptions:

する → 出来る【で・き・る】

くる → こられる

ある → あり得る 【あり・うる／あり・える】
item_key: grammar:明日は仕事だから、今日はあまり飲めない。

だ
。

hear 
that (it will) snow tomorrow.

降[ふ,くだ]る to fall (of rain, snow, ash, etc.); to come down

primary_text:
明日は遅くなる

かもしれない

。
explanation:
Might 
be late tomorrow.

明日 【あした】 – tomorrow

遅い 【おそ・い】(i-adj) – late

なる(u-verb) – to become

Indicating a possibility

「かもしれない」 is another sentence ending that expresses a neutral possibility with about 50% level of confidence. It is simply the 「か」 and 「も」 particles combined with the potential negative form of 「知る」 (literally meaning “can’t know even if…”). This means that it conjugates just like any other negative ru-verb.
item_key: grammar:明日は遅くなるかもしれない。

have to 
do homework by tomorrow.

Things can get quite lengthy with the double negative required to describe an action that must be done. When using the casual variations with the negative, you can also omit the 「いけない／ならない／だめ」 part of the grammar. This also applies to the 「と」 conditional.

行って欲しい
んだけど、大丈夫？
explanation:
(I) want (you) to go to (the) post office but (is it) ok?

Wanting others to do something

We can use the i-adjective 「欲しい」, which means “desired” or “wanted” with verbs to say you want somebody to do something. This is done by first conjugating the verb to the te-form, then attaching 「欲しい」（or ほしい） to the end of the verb. The result remains an i-adjective.
